title: Croatian to B1 — the validated path

days.title: A2 kickoff: Zemlje i jezici
days.objective: Start the A2 e-course and talk about countries, nationalities and languages.
days.paretoFocus: The free University of Zagreb A2 course is 80 units of exactly the right material. One unit a day, every day, is the whole engine of this phase — start the habit today.
drills: Sign in at a2.ffzg.unizg.hr (same free login as A1) and do Unit 1 of A2.hr.
Learn 12 country-language pairs: Njemačka/njemački, Francuska/francuski, Španjolska/španjolski…
Say which languages you speak, understand and are learning.
Skim the 8-module map of the course so you know the road ahead.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall 8 countries with their languages.
Say 'Učim hrvatski jer…' with your real reason.

activities.title: Zemlje i jezici — 12 country-language pairs
activities.intro: Welcome to A2! First rule of the new level: country names are capitalized (Njemačka), but language names are ALWAYS lowercase (njemački). The language is really an adjective — the same -ski family you'll dissect tomorrow. Say each pair aloud: country, then language.

items.en: Croatia — Croatian
items.note: Country capitalized, language lowercase. Always.

items.en: England — English
items.note: You say 'govorim engleski' even if you're American — the language is engleski.

items.en: Germany — German
France — French
Spain — Spanish
Italy — Italian
items.note: Watch out: the language is talijanski, not 'italijanski'.

items.en: Portugal — Portuguese
Russia — Russian
Poland — Polish
Greece — Greek
China — Chinese
Japan — Japanese

activities.title: Talking about your languages
activities.intro: Three verbs run this whole topic: govoriti (speak), razumjeti (understand), učiti (learn/study). The language goes straight after the verb, lowercase, no preposition: govorim hrvatski. These ten sentences are your template for every 'which languages do you speak?' conversation.

items.en: I speak English and Croatian.

items.hr: Razumijem hrvatski, ali još ne govorim tečno.
items.en: I understand Croatian, but I don't speak fluently yet.
items.note: tečno = fluently; još = still/yet.

items.en: I have been learning Croatian for two years.
items.note: Croatian uses the present tense + a time span: literally 'I learn Croatian two years'.

items.en: My mother tongue is English.
items.note: materinski jezik = native language.

items.en: Which languages do you speak?
items.note: jezike = accusative plural of jezik.

items.en: I speak a little German.

items.hr: Moja žena govori hrvatski, engleski i talijanski.
items.en: My wife speaks Croatian, English and Italian.

items.hr: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica.
items.en: I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian.
items.note: jer = because; the little word je jumps to right after jer (second position).

items.en: He speaks five languages.
items.note: After 5 and up: genitive plural — pet jezika, not 'pet jezici'.

items.en: foreign language — foreign languages

activities.title: Countries and languages workout
activities.intro: Match countries to languages, then produce the sentences yourself. Remember: languages are lowercase, and watch talijanski.

questions.prompt: Which language do they speak in Njemačka (Germany)?
questions.explanation: njemački is the language — lowercase, ending in -ki. Njemačka is the country, Nijemac is a German man.

questions.prompt: How are language names written in Croatian?
options: Capitalized only at the start of a sentence
questions.explanation: Languages are lowercase in Croatian: hrvatski, engleski, njemački. Only the country (Hrvatska) and the person (Hrvat/Hrvatica) are capitalized.

questions.prompt: Ona je iz Francuske. Govori ___.
questions.explanation: The language francuski — lowercase, -ski form. Francuska is the country; Francuskinja is a French woman.

questions.prompt: Write the language they speak in Italija.
questions.explanation: talijanski — this is the trap pair: the country is Italija but the language is talijanski, never 'italijanski'.

questions.prompt: Write the language they speak in Grčka (Greece).
questions.explanation: grčki — note the č. Grčka → grčki, one of the -čki languages like njemački.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I speak English and I am learning Croatian.'
questions.explanation: Govorim engleski i učim hrvatski. — verb + lowercase language, no preposition needed.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian.'
questions.answer: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica.
questions.explanation: After jer, the clitic je goes immediately into second position of its clause: jer JE moja žena Hrvatica.

questions.prompt: On govori pet ___.
questions.explanation: Numbers 5 and above take the genitive plural: pet jezika. (Compare: dva jezika, tri jezika — but that's a different, dual form; with pet it's true genitive plural.)

days.title: Language adjectives: -ski, -ški, -čki
days.objective: Form relational adjectives (hrvatski, praški, grčki) from place names.
days.paretoFocus: One suffix family turns every country or city you know into an adjective — instant vocabulary multiplication.
drills: Do Unit 2 of A2.hr.
Form 10 adjectives: hrvatski, engleski, njemački, zagrebački, splitski…
Note languages are lowercase in Croatian: govorim hrvatski.
Describe 5 things by origin: talijanska kava, francuski film.

items: Turn 6 place names into adjectives from memory.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review yesterday's country-language pairs.

activities.title: The place-to-adjective factory: -ski, -ški, -čki
activities.intro: One suffix family turns any place into an adjective. Base rule: stem + -ski (Split → splitski). But the final consonant reacts: k, c or č + ski fuses into -čki (Amerika → američki), and g or h + ski fuses into -ški (Prag → praški). These adjectives are always lowercase and never form comparatives.

items.en: Croatia → Croatian
items.note: Drop -ska, add -ski to the stem hrvat-.

items.en: England → English
France → French
Split → of Split
items.note: Plain consonant + -ski: no change.

items.en: Zagreb → of Zagreb
items.note: Some places take -ački: zagrebački kolodvor (Zagreb station).

items.en: America → American
items.note: k + ski → čki. Same in: Njemačka → njemački.

items.en: Rijeka → of Rijeka
items.note: k → č again, plus the vowel shifts: rijeČki.

items.en: Greece → Greek
Prague → of Prague
items.note: g + ski → ški: praŠki.

items.en: city → urban / city-
items.note: The suffix works on common nouns too: gradski trg = city square.

items.en: sea → maritime / sea-
items.note: morski pas = shark (literally 'sea dog').

items.en: tourist → travel agency
items.note: t + ski can become -čki/-čka via c: turistički.

activities.title: Describe things by origin
activities.intro: These adjectives agree with their noun like any other: -ski (masculine), -ska (feminine), -sko (neuter). Instant vocabulary: every country you learned yesterday now describes coffee, films, wine and food.

items.en: Italian coffee
items.note: kava is feminine → -ska.

items.en: a French film
items.note: film is masculine → -ski.

items.en: Spanish wine
items.note: vino is neuter → -sko.

items.en: a German car
Croatian cuisine
Dalmatian prosciutto
items.note: Dalmacija → dalmatinski.

items.en: a Chinese restaurant
English tea
Zagreb (train) station
I love Italian coffee.
items.note: In the accusative the adjective declines too: talijanskA kava → talijanskU kavu.

activities.title: Adjective factory practice
activities.intro: Form the adjectives, watch the consonant fusions (k→čki, g→ški), and make them agree with their noun.

questions.prompt: Engleska → the adjective is:
questions.explanation: engleski — lowercase, stem + -ski. Capitalized Engleski would be wrong: these adjectives are always lowercase.

questions.prompt: Which is written correctly?
questions.explanation: pivo is neuter → njemačko, and the adjective is lowercase even though Njemačka (the country) is capitalized.

questions.prompt: Zagreb → the adjective is:
questions.explanation: Zagreb takes -ački: zagrebački kolodvor, zagrebačka katedrala. Not every place is plain -ski — learn city adjectives as words.

questions.prompt: Form the adjective from Split.
questions.explanation: splitski — a plain consonant before -ski stays unchanged: splitska riva, splitski aerodrom.

questions.prompt: Form the adjective from Prag (Prague). Careful: g reacts with -ski.
questions.explanation: g + ski → ški: praški. Same rule for h: Čeh → češki (Czech).

questions.prompt: Translate: 'Italian coffee' (watch the gender agreement).
questions.explanation: kava is feminine, so the adjective takes -ska: talijanska kava.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'Spanish wine' (vino is neuter).
questions.explanation: Neuter agreement: -sko. španjolsko vino, but španjolska glazba (f.) and španjolski jezik (m.).

questions.prompt: Rijeka → the adjective is:
questions.explanation: k + ski → čki, so Rijeka → riječki (riječka luka = the port of Rijeka).

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I drink Italian coffee every morning.'
questions.explanation: In the accusative both adjective and noun take -u: talijanskU kavU. svako jutro = every morning.

days.title: Possessive adjectives: bratov, sestrin
days.objective: Say whose something is with -ov/-ev/-in adjectives.
days.paretoFocus: Croatian prefers 'bratov auto' over 'auto od brata' — mastering this small pattern makes your possession sound native.
drills: Do Unit 3 of A2.hr.
Form possessives: brat → bratov, muž → mužev, sestra → sestrin.
Describe 6 objects around you by owner (Ovo je ženina knjiga.).
Contrast animate vs inanimate possessives.

items: Make possessive adjectives from 5 names.
Review -ski adjectives.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Possessive adjectives: -ov, -ev, -in
activities.intro: Croatian turns a person into an adjective to show possession: brat → bratov (brother's). Three endings cover everything: masculine owner + hard consonant → -ov; masculine owner + soft consonant (č, ć, š, ž, j, lj, nj) → -ev; any owner whose name ends in -a → -in. Croatian strongly prefers 'bratov auto' over 'auto od brata'.

items.en: brother → brother's
items.note: Hard consonant → -ov: bratov auto.

items.en: Marko → Marko's
items.note: Drop the final -o, add -ov.

items.en: Ivan → Ivan's
husband → husband's
items.note: ž is a soft consonant → -ev, not -ov.

items.en: friend → friend's
items.note: lj is soft → -ev.

items.en: father → father's
items.note: Double change: the fleeting a drops (otac → otc-) and c softens to č → očev.

items.en: sister → sister's
items.note: Owner in -a → -in.

items.en: Ana → Ana's
wife → wife's
items.note: ženina knjiga = my wife's book.

items.en: mother → mother's
items.note: k softens to č before -in: majčin, never 'majkin'.

items.en: grandma → grandma's
items.note: But baka stays bakin — the k→č change is word-by-word; learn majčin as the exception you'll hear most.

items.en: dad → dad's
items.note: tata is masculine but ends in -a, so it takes -in: tatin auto.

activities.title: Whose is it? Agreement in action
activities.intro: A possessive adjective agrees with the THING owned, not the owner: bratov auto (m.), bratova kuća (f.), bratovo pismo (n.). The owner only decides -ov/-ev vs -in; the ending after that follows the noun.

items.en: This is my wife's book.
items.note: knjiga is feminine → ženinA.

items.en: My brother's car is old.
My sister's child is sleeping.
items.note: dijete is neuter → sestrinO.

items.en: Ana's husband is cooking dinner.
Marko's sister lives in Split.
I'm reading my wife's book.
items.note: In the accusative the possessive declines like an adjective: ženinA → ženinU.

items.en: I see my brother's car.
items.note: Inanimate masculine accusative = nominative, so bratov doesn't change here.

items.en: brother's car — NOT 'car of brother'
items.note: The od-construction sounds clumsy; natives reach for the possessive adjective.

activities.title: Possessive adjective drill
activities.intro: Form the possessives, pick -ov/-ev/-in correctly, and watch the two consonant tricks: otac → očev, majka → majčin.

questions.prompt: brat → the possessive adjective is:
questions.explanation: Masculine owner, hard final consonant → -ov: bratov auto, bratova kuća.

questions.prompt: sestra → the possessive adjective is:
questions.explanation: Owner ending in -a → -in: sestrin. (sestrina is the feminine agreement form — the base adjective is sestrin.)

questions.prompt: Why is it mužev and not 'mužov'?
options: ž is a soft consonant, and soft consonants take -ev
questions.answer: ž is a soft consonant, and soft consonants take -ev
questions.explanation: After soft consonants (č, ć, š, ž, j, lj, nj) the ending is -ev: mužev, prijateljev. After hard consonants: -ov.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ovo je ___ muž.' (Ana's husband)
questions.explanation: Ana → Anin. Owner in -a takes -in; muž is masculine so no extra ending is needed.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Čitam ___ knjigu.' (my wife's — from žena)
questions.explanation: žena → ženin, then agreement with knjigu in the accusative feminine: ženinU knjigU.

questions.prompt: Form the possessive from otac (father). Two changes happen!
questions.explanation: otac loses its fleeting a (otc-) and c softens to č before -ev: očev. očev auto = father's car.

questions.prompt: Form the possessive from majka (mother). Careful with the k.
questions.explanation: k softens to č before -in: majčin. 'majkin' is a classic learner error.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'Ana's husband and Marko's sister work together.'
questions.answer: Anin muž i Markova sestra rade zajedno.
questions.explanation: Anin (owner in -a → -in) agrees with muž (m.); Markova (owner Marko → -ov) agrees with sestra (f.) → MarkovA.

days.title: Accusative across the whole phrase
days.objective: Decline adjectives, demonstratives and possessives in the accusative, not just nouns.
days.paretoFocus: A1 gave you noun endings; A2 makes the whole phrase agree ('Vidim ovog novog kolegu'). This is the single biggest step toward correct Croatian.
drills: Do Unit 4 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · adjective declension (accusative).
Decline 6 full phrases: taj dobar čovjek → tog dobrog čovjeka.
Write 5 'I see/know/love + full phrase' sentences.

items: Say 'I see this new colleague' with full agreement.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall the animate vs inanimate accusative rule.

activities.title: Accusative across the whole phrase
activities.intro: A1 taught you noun endings; A2 makes everything in the phrase agree. In the accusative singular: feminine — every word takes -u (tu lijepu ženu); masculine animate — demonstrative and adjective take -og (or -eg after a soft consonant) and the noun takes -a (tog dobrog čovjeka); masculine inanimate and neuter — nothing changes. Watch each transformation.

items.hr: taj dobar čovjek → tog dobrog čovjeka
items.en: that good man → (I see) that good man
items.note: Animate masculine: -og + -og + -a. Note dobar → dobrog: the fleeting a drops.

items.hr: ovaj novi kolega → ovog novog kolegu
items.en: this new colleague → (I see) this new colleague
items.note: kolega is masculine but declines like an a-noun: adjectives masculine (-og), noun takes -u!

items.hr: moj stari auto → moj stari auto
items.en: my old car → (I see) my old car
items.note: Inanimate masculine: accusative = nominative. Nothing changes.

items.hr: naš novi učitelj → našeg novog učitelja
items.en: our new teacher → (I see) our new teacher
items.note: After a soft consonant (š) the ending is -eg: našEg, but novOg.

items.hr: ta lijepa žena → tu lijepu ženu
items.en: that beautiful woman → (I see) that beautiful woman
items.note: Feminine: -u across the board.

items.hr: ova nova knjiga → ovu novu knjigu
items.en: this new book → (I read) this new book

items.hr: moja dobra prijateljica → moju dobru prijateljicu
items.en: my good friend (f.) → (I see) my good friend

items.hr: to malo dijete → to malo dijete
items.en: that small child → (I see) that small child
items.note: Neuter: accusative = nominative, even for living beings.

activities.title: See it, know it, love it — full phrases in sentences
activities.intro: The verbs vidjeti, znati, voljeti, čekati, gledati and imati all take a direct object in the accusative. Read each sentence aloud and feel the whole phrase click into place.

items.hr: Vidim ovog novog kolegu svaki dan.
items.en: I see this new colleague every day.
Do you know that good man?
I love this old song.
I'm waiting for our new teacher.
I'm watching this old film.
items.note: film = inanimate, so the whole phrase stays in nominative form.

items.en: I have one good idea.
items.note: jedan declines like an adjective too: jednU dobrU idejU.

items.en: I know his good friend (f.).
I'm looking for that black coat.

activities.title: Whole-phrase accusative drill
activities.intro: Decline the full phrase, not just the noun. The two questions everything hinges on: is it feminine (→ all -u)? Is the masculine noun alive (→ -og/-eg + -a)?

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (taj dobar čovjek).
questions.explanation: Animate masculine: every word changes — tog dobrog čovjeka. Mixing changed and unchanged words is the classic error.

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (moj stari auto).
questions.explanation: auto is inanimate masculine → accusative = nominative. Nothing changes: Vidim moj stari auto.

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (ta lijepa žena).
questions.explanation: Feminine accusative: -u on every word — tU lijepU ženU. (toj lijepoj ženi is dative/locative.)

questions.prompt: Put 'ova nova knjiga' into the sentence: Čitam ___.
questions.explanation: Feminine phrase → all three words take -u: ovu novu knjigu.

questions.prompt: Put 'naš novi učitelj' into the sentence: Čekam ___.
questions.explanation: Animate masculine: našEg (soft š → -eg), novOg (hard v → -og), učitelja (-a).

questions.prompt: Put 'ovaj novi kolega' into the sentence: Vidim ___.
questions.explanation: kolega is masculine, so the adjectives take -og — but the noun itself declines like an a-noun: kolegU.

questions.prompt: Vidim ___ (to malo dijete).
questions.explanation: Neuter accusative always equals the nominative — even though a child is alive. The animacy rule applies only to masculine nouns.

questions.prompt: Why is it našEg (not 'našog') novog učitelja?
options: After a soft consonant like š the ending is -eg
Both našeg and našog are standard
questions.answer: After a soft consonant like š the ending is -eg
questions.explanation: Soft-stem words (naš, vaš, moj...) take -eg where hard stems take -og: našeg, ali novog. The following noun doesn't decide this — the stem of each word does.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I see this new colleague every day.'
questions.answer: Vidim ovog novog kolegu svaki dan.
questions.explanation: ovog novog kolegu — masculine agreement on the modifiers, a-noun ending on kolega. svaki dan (every day) stays accusative but inanimate, so unchanged.

days.title: svoj — 'one's own'
days.objective: Use the reflexive-possessive svoj when the owner is the subject.
days.paretoFocus: 'Volim svoj posao' vs 'Volim njegov posao' changes the meaning entirely. svoj is obligatory in Croatian and has no English equivalent — drill it early.
drills: Do Unit 5 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · reflexive svoj.
Contrast pairs: Čitam svoju knjigu / Čitam njezinu knjigu.
Fix 5 sentences where moj/tvoj should be svoj.

items: Explain in one sentence when svoj is used.
Review accusative phrase agreement.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: svoj — one's own
activities.intro: svoj points back to the subject of the sentence. If the owner IS the subject, Croatian demands svoj — no exceptions, in any person: Čitam svoju knjigu, On vozi svoj auto. Using moj/njegov instead either sounds foreign or changes the meaning completely. svoj declines exactly like moj.

items.en: I'm reading my (own) book.
items.note: Subject ja = owner → svoju, not moju.

items.en: I'm reading her book.
items.note: Owner (she) is NOT the subject (I) → njezinu is correct here.

items.en: He drives his own car.
He drives his (ANOTHER man's) car.
items.note: This is the famous trap: njegov here can only mean someone else's car.

items.en: Ana loves her (own) husband.
items.note: Masculine animate accusative of svoj: svog(a) — just like mog(a).

items.en: We love our (own) city.
items.note: svoj works for every person: ja, ti, on, mi, vi, oni.

items.en: I see my brother.
I love my job.
items.note: 'Volim njegov posao' would mean you love someone else's job.

items.en: My wife is Croatian.
items.note: svoj never describes the subject itself — in the nominative you keep moja.

items.en: Take your (own) things!
items.note: In commands the hidden subject is ti, so svoj is still required.

activities.title: svoj or not svoj?
activities.intro: One question decides everything: is the owner the subject of the verb? Yes → svoj. No → moj/tvoj/njegov/njezin.

questions.prompt: 'On vozi svoj auto.' Whose car is he driving?
questions.explanation: svoj always points back to the subject: on = the owner. His own car.

questions.prompt: 'On vozi njegov auto.' Whose car is he driving?
questions.explanation: Since svoj is obligatory for the subject's own things, njegov here can only mean some OTHER man's car. The meaning flips entirely.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Čitam ___ knjigu.' (my own book)
questions.explanation: Subject ja = owner → svoju. 'Čitam moju knjigu' is understood but marked — natives say svoju.

questions.prompt: Fix the possessive: 'Volim moj posao.' Which word should replace moj?
questions.explanation: The subject (ja) owns the job → svoj is obligatory: Volim svoj posao.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ana voli ___ muža.' (her OWN husband)
questions.explanation: Owner = subject (Ana) → svoj, in the masculine animate accusative: svog(a) muža.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Mi volimo ___ grad.' (our own city)
questions.explanation: svoj works in every person, including mi. grad is inanimate masculine, so the accusative form is just svoj.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is WRONG?
questions.explanation: svoj can never sit in the nominative describing the subject itself — the subject can't 'own itself'. Correct: Moja žena je Hrvatica.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'He is reading his own book, not hers.'
questions.answer: On čita svoju knjigu, a ne njezinu.
questions.explanation: svoju = his own (owner = subject on); njezinu = hers (owner ≠ subject). The contrast in one sentence.

days.title: Output day: Moji jezici
days.objective: Speak and write about your language history and goals.
days.paretoFocus: Producing a personal text locks in the week's grammar with content you'll reuse in every future conversation about learning Croatian.
drills: Do Unit 6 of A2.hr.
Write 8 sentences: which languages you speak, since when, and why Croatian.
Deliver it aloud twice as a 90-second talk.
Slip in one -ski adjective, one possessive and one svoj.

items: Re-deliver your talk without notes.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Note 3 words you were missing and add them.

activities.title: Model talk: Moji jezici
activities.intro: Today you produce. Here is a model 90-second talk built entirely from this week's grammar — a -ski adjective, a possessive, a svoj, and the language phrases from day 61. Read it aloud twice, then rebuild it with YOUR facts.

items.en: My mother tongue is English.
I also speak a little Spanish.
I've been learning Croatian for two years.
items.note: Fronting hrvatski gives it emphasis — very natural in speech.

items.hr: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica.
items.en: I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian.
Her family speaks only Croatian.
items.note: njezina, not svoja — the owner (she) is not the subject (her family).

items.hr: Želim razumjeti njezinu obitelj — i nju.
items.en: I want to understand her family — and her.

items.hr: Svaki dan učim nove riječi i slušam hrvatsku glazbu.
items.en: Every day I learn new words and listen to Croatian music.
items.note: hrvatskU glazbU — yesterday's -ska adjective in the accusative.

items.hr: Volim svoj mali ritual: kava i deset novih riječi.
items.en: I love my little ritual: coffee and ten new words.
items.note: svoj — because I am the subject and the owner.

items.hr: Još uvijek govorim sporo, ali razumijem puno.
items.en: I still speak slowly, but I understand a lot.
One day I want to speak fluently.
items.note: jednog dana = one day / someday, a fixed genitive time phrase.

activities.title: Build your talk
activities.intro: Assemble the pieces of the talk. Every question is a sentence you'll actually say out loud afterwards.

questions.prompt: What does 'materinski jezik' mean?
questions.explanation: materinski jezik = native language, from mati/majka (mother). Moj materinski jezik je engleski.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Učim hrvatski ___ je moja žena Hrvatica.' (because)
questions.explanation: jer = because. The clitic je follows it immediately: jer JE moja žena Hrvatica.

questions.prompt: Build the opening line: 'My mother tongue is English.'
questions.explanation: Subject phrase first, je in second position, language lowercase: engleski.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Slušam ___ glazbu.' (Croatian — watch the accusative)
questions.explanation: glazba is feminine and this is the accusative: hrvatskU glazbU. The -ska adjective declines like any adjective.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Želim razumjeti ___ obitelj.' (her family — my wife's)
questions.explanation: The subject is ja, but the owner is she — owner ≠ subject, so njezinu, not svoju. Yesterday's rule, live.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Hrvatski učim dvije ___.' (for two years)
questions.explanation: dvije godine — after dva/dvije the noun takes the -e form: dvije godine, tri godine, but pet godina.

questions.prompt: Build the closing line: 'One day I want to speak fluently.'
questions.explanation: jednog dana (fixed time phrase) + željeti + infinitive. A perfect final sentence for your talk.

questions.prompt: 'Volim ___ mali ritual.' (my own) — which word, and why?
options: svoj — the subject ja is the owner
moj — svoj is only for on/ona
questions.answer: svoj — the subject ja is the owner
questions.explanation: svoj applies in EVERY person whenever subject = owner. ritual is inanimate masculine, so the accusative is plain svoj.

activities.title: Deliver the talk to your wife
activities.intro: Read your lines aloud as a connected talk — this is your 90-second performance with a live audience of one. Then do it again without looking at the English.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mogu li ti ispričati svoj mali govor o jezicima?
lines.en: Can I tell you my little talk about languages?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Naravno, slušam te!
lines.en: Of course, I'm listening!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Moj materinski jezik je engleski. Govorim i malo španjolski.
lines.en: My mother tongue is English. I also speak a little Spanish.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A zašto baš hrvatski?
lines.en: And why Croatian, of all languages?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Učim hrvatski jer je moja žena Hrvatica. Želim razumjeti tvoju obitelj — i tebe.
lines.en: I'm learning Croatian because my wife is Croatian. I want to understand your family — and you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! A kako učiš?
lines.en: Bravo! And how do you study?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Svaki dan učim nove riječi i slušam hrvatsku glazbu.
lines.en: Every day I learn new words and listen to Croatian music.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kada si počeo?
lines.en: When did you start?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Počeo sam prije dvije godine, ali ozbiljno učim od siječnja.
lines.en: I started two years ago, but I've been studying seriously since January.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Govoriš jako dobro!
lines.en: You speak very well!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala! Još uvijek govorim sporo, ali jednog dana želim govoriti tečno.
lines.en: Thanks! I still speak slowly, but one day I want to speak fluently.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ponosna sam na tebe!
lines.en: I'm proud of you!

days.title: Week 9 review + speaking
days.objective: Consolidate the first six units in live conversation.
days.paretoFocus: A weekly speaking session converts a week of study into skill — and if you live with a Croatian speaker, 15 minutes of chat with your spouse counts fully as tutor time.
drills: Have a 15-minute conversation about countries, languages and learning (tutor or your Croatian spouse).
Re-do the exercises of your weakest A2.hr unit this week.
If any A1.hr units are still unfinished, clear one now.
Note 5 corrections from the conversation.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say the 5 corrected sentences.
Recall: -ski adjectives, possessives, svoj — one example each.

activities.title: Week 9 in ten lines
activities.intro: Everything this week taught, one example each. If any line makes you hesitate, that's your weakest unit — redo its exercises today. Say each Croatian line aloud before reading the note.

items.en: I speak English, and I'm learning Croatian.
items.note: Day 61: languages lowercase, straight after the verb.

items.en: Germany — German; France — French
items.note: Day 61: country capitalized, language not.

items.en: I love Italian coffee.
items.note: Day 62: -ski family agrees with its noun — talijanskU kavU.

items.hr: Zagreb → zagrebački, Rijeka → riječki, Prag → praški
items.en: the city adjectives
items.note: Day 62: k → čki, g → ški.

items.en: This is my wife's book.
items.note: Day 63: owner in -a → -in, agreement with the thing owned.

items.en: My brother's car is old.
items.note: Day 63: prefer bratov auto to 'auto od brata'.

items.en: I see that good man.
items.note: Day 64: animate masculine — the WHOLE phrase declines.

items.en: I see this new colleague.
items.note: Day 64: kolega — masculine adjectives, a-noun ending.

items.hr: Čitam svoju knjigu. / On vozi njegov auto.
items.en: I read my own book. / He drives another man's car.
items.note: Day 65: subject = owner → svoj; njegov then means someone else's.

items.en: How do you say 'weekend' in Croatian?
items.note: Your conversation lifeline for today's chat. (Answer: vikend!)

activities.title: Week 9 mixed review
activities.intro: All five topics shuffled together — exactly how real speech serves them. No topic labels: you decide which rule each question needs.

questions.prompt: Španjolska → the language is:
questions.explanation: Language = lowercase -ski form: španjolski. Španjolac is a Spanish man.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct adjective from Zagreb?
questions.explanation: Zagreb → zagrebački (with -ački), always lowercase.

questions.prompt: How do natives prefer to say 'my brother's car'?
questions.explanation: The possessive adjective wins: bratov auto. 'auto od brata' is understood but clumsy; bratski means 'brotherly'.

questions.prompt: Put 'taj dobar čovjek' into: Vidim ___.
questions.explanation: Animate masculine accusative: tog dobrog čovjeka — every word declines.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Čitam ___ knjigu.' (my own)
questions.explanation: Subject = owner → svoj, feminine accusative: svoju.

questions.prompt: 'On vozi njegov auto.' Whose car?
questions.explanation: Because svoj is obligatory for one's own things, njegov here necessarily points to a different man.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ovo je ___ auto.' (my sister's — from sestra)
questions.explanation: sestra → sestrin (owner in -a → -in); auto is masculine so no further ending.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I love Italian coffee and Croatian music.'
questions.answer: Volim talijansku kavu i hrvatsku glazbu.
questions.explanation: Both -ska adjectives agree with their feminine nouns in the accusative: talijanskU kavU, hrvatskU glazbU.

questions.prompt: 'Ana čita njezinu knjigu.' Whose book is Ana reading?
questions.explanation: If it were Ana's own book, Croatian would force svoju. njezinu signals a different female owner — the svoj rule working in reverse.

activities.title: Week 9 conversation: languages and learning
activities.intro: Your 15-minute conversation starter. Act it out, then keep going off-script — the questions your partner asks here are exactly the ones to keep asking each other. Notice the built-in self-correction on line 6: correcting yourself out loud is a skill, not a failure.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro jutro! Danas razgovaramo samo na hrvatskom, može?
lines.en: Good morning! Today we talk only in Croatian, okay?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može! Jučer sam učio nove riječi cijelo jutro.
lines.en: Deal! Yesterday I studied new words all morning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. Koje jezike sada govoriš?
lines.en: Great. Which languages do you speak now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Govorim engleski, malo španjolski, a hrvatski još učim.
lines.en: I speak English, a little Spanish, and Croatian I'm still learning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A što si radio jučer poslijepodne?
lines.en: And what did you do yesterday afternoon?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Čitao sam moju knjigu... ne, čekaj — svoju knjigu!
lines.en: I was reading my book... no, wait — my OWN book (svoju)!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Svoju, jer si ti subjekt. A čija je ona plava knjiga na stolu?
lines.en: Bravo! Svoju, because you're the subject. And whose is that blue book on the table?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: To je ženina knjiga. Tvoja!
lines.en: That's my wife's book. Yours!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Točno! A što voliš više: talijansku kavu ili hrvatsku?
lines.en: Correct! And what do you like more: Italian coffee or Croatian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Volim talijansku kavu, ali hrvatsku glazbu.
lines.en: I like Italian coffee, but Croatian music.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlična kombinacija! Vidim jednog vrlo dobrog studenta.
lines.en: An excellent combination! I see one very good student.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A ja vidim jednu odličnu učiteljicu.
lines.en: And I see one excellent teacher.

days.title: Kad će taj vikend? — making plans
days.objective: Talk about upcoming weekend plans with futur I and time phrases.
days.paretoFocus: Weekend plans are the small talk you'll actually have every Friday; the theme recycles futur I while new grammar loads underneath.
drills: Do Unit 7 of A2.hr.
Learn plan vocabulary: izlazak, roštilj, izlet, odmor, druženje.
Make 6 futur I sentences about this weekend (Ići ću…, Naći ćemo se…).
Ask someone 'Što radiš u subotu?' and answer for yourself.

items: Say three real plans for this weekend.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall the short forms ću/ćeš/će.

activities.title: Weekend plan vocabulary
activities.intro: The small talk you'll actually have every Friday: what's happening this weekend? Ten words carry the whole theme. Note the pattern for days: u + accusative — u subotu, u nedjelju.

items.en: weekend
items.note: Yes, it's a loanword: ovaj vikend = this weekend.

items.en: going out, a night out
items.note: Fleeting a: izlazak → plural izlasci.

items.en: barbecue
items.note: Croatia's national weekend institution.

items.en: day trip, excursion
items.note: ići na izlet = go on a day trip.

items.en: rest, break, vacation
get-together, hanging out with people
cinema
items.note: ići u kino = go to the movies.

items.en: plan — plans
items.note: Imaš li planove za vikend? = Do you have plans for the weekend?

items.en: on Saturday
items.note: u + accusative for days: u subotu, u petak.

items.en: on Sunday

activities.title: Futur I in plan sentences
activities.intro: Futur I refresher, loaded with weekend content. Short forms: ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će. Two spelling rules: infinitives in -ti drop the i before ću (gledat ću), infinitives in -ći keep everything (ići ću). And the clitic ću can never stand first in the sentence.

items.hr: Ići ću na izlet u subotu.
items.en: I'll go on a day trip on Saturday.
items.note: -ći infinitive stays whole: ići ću.

items.en: What will you do on Saturday?

items.hr: Naći ćemo se u centru u sedam.
items.en: We'll meet in the centre at seven.
items.note: naći se = to meet up; ćemo + se cluster together after the verb.

items.en: We'll watch a film in the evening.
items.note: gledati drops its final i in writing: gledat ću, gledat ćemo.

items.en: On Sunday we'll have a barbecue.
items.note: When something else starts the sentence, ćemo slides into second position.

items.en: Friends will come to our place.
items.note: nam = to us (dative clitic) — right after će.

items.en: I'll get in touch with you tomorrow.
items.note: Watch the clitic train: ću + ti + se. Tomorrow's whole lesson hides in this line.

items.hr: Nećemo ništa raditi — odmarat ćemo se.
items.en: We won't do anything — we'll rest.
items.note: Negative future: neću, nećeš, neće... written as one word.

items.en: I will go. (both correct)
items.note: But never 'Ću ići' — the clitic can't open a sentence.

activities.title: Weekend futur workout
activities.intro: Vocabulary plus futur I mechanics: short forms, spelling, and where ću is allowed to sit.

questions.prompt: What is 'roštilj'?
questions.explanation: roštilj = barbecue. izlet = day trip, odmor = rest, izlazak = night out.

questions.prompt: The short future form for 'ja' is:
questions.explanation: ja ću, ti ćeš, on/ona će, mi ćemo, vi ćete, oni će.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct?
questions.explanation: -ti infinitives drop the final i before ću in Croatian: gledat ću. (Gledaću is Serbian spelling; ću can never come first.)

questions.prompt: Complete: '___ ćemo se u centru u sedam.' (We'll MEET...)
questions.explanation: naći se = to meet up. -ći infinitives keep their full form: Naći ćemo se.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'U subotu ___ ići na izlet.' (I will)
questions.explanation: Something else (U subotu) opens the sentence, so the clitic ću takes second position: U subotu ću ići na izlet.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I'll go on a day trip on Saturday.'
questions.answer: Ići ću na izlet u subotu.
questions.explanation: Verb-first future: Ići ću... The clitic ću lands in second position, right after the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: 'On ___ raditi u nedjelju.' (won't)
questions.explanation: Negative futur I is one word: neću, nećeš, neće, nećemo, nećete, neće. It CAN start a sentence because it's stressed.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'On Sunday we'll have a barbecue.'
questions.explanation: U nedjelju fills first position, so ćemo must come immediately after it — before the infinitive.

activities.title: Plan the real weekend
activities.intro: Plan your actual upcoming weekend with your wife — start from the script, then swap in your real plans. Every 'Me' line uses futur I; keep that going when you improvise.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što ćemo raditi ovaj vikend?
lines.en: What will we do this weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U subotu ujutro radit ću u vrtu, a poslije sam slobodan.
lines.en: On Saturday morning I'll work in the garden, and after that I'm free.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super! Hoćemo li ići na izlet?
lines.en: Great! Shall we go on a day trip?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može! Ići ćemo u planine ako bude sunca.
lines.en: Sure! We'll go to the mountains if it's sunny.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A navečer? Marko i Ana nas zovu na roštilj.
lines.en: And in the evening? Marko and Ana are inviting us to a barbecue.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Odlično! Doći ćemo u sedam. Što ćemo donijeti?
lines.en: Excellent! We'll come at seven. What will we bring?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Donijet ćemo vino i salatu. A u nedjelju?
lines.en: We'll bring wine and a salad. And on Sunday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U nedjelju nećemo ništa raditi — odmarat ćemo se cijeli dan.
lines.en: On Sunday we won't do anything — we'll rest all day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Savršen plan! Hoćemo li gledati film navečer?
lines.en: A perfect plan! Shall we watch a film in the evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, gledat ćemo jedan dobar hrvatski film.
lines.en: Yes, we'll watch a good Croatian film.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dogovoreno! Javit ću Ani da dolazimo.
lines.en: Agreed! I'll let Ana know we're coming.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Super. Veselim se vikendu!
lines.en: Great. I'm looking forward to the weekend!

days.title: Pronouns as objects: me, te, ga, je
days.objective: Use accusative personal pronouns in stressed and unstressed forms.
days.paretoFocus: 'Vidiš li me? — Vidim te.' Object pronouns appear in almost every exchange; the short (clitic) forms are what natives use 95% of the time.
drills: Do Unit 8 of A2.hr.
Learn the pairs: mene/me, tebe/te, njega/ga, nju/ju (je), nas, vas, njih/ih.
Answer 6 questions replacing the noun with a pronoun (Vidiš li Marka? — Vidim ga.).
Note stressed forms for emphasis and after prepositions (za mene).

items: Replace the object with a pronoun in 4 sentences.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review weekend-plan phrases.

activities.title: me, te, ga, je — the object pronoun pairs
activities.intro: Every accusative pronoun has two forms: a stressed one (mene, tebe, njega) and a short clitic (me, te, ga). The clitic is the default — natives use it 95% of the time — and it always sits in second position. The stressed form appears after prepositions, for contrast, and as a stand-alone answer.

items.en: me (stressed / clitic)
you (stressed / clitic)
him, it (stressed / clitic)
her (stressed / clitic)
items.note: The everyday clitic is je; use ju when it would bump into the auxiliary je: Vidio JU JE jučer.

items.en: us
items.note: Same letters stressed and clitic — only the stress differs in speech.

items.en: you (plural/formal)
them (stressed / clitic)
for me, at his place
items.note: After a preposition: ALWAYS the stressed form. 'za me' is impossible in standard speech.

items.en: Who are you waiting for? — Him.
items.note: A one-word answer must be stressed: Njega, not 'Ga'.

items.en: I see you.
items.note: The clitic hugs second position: Vidim te. Sutra te vidim.

activities.title: Question and answer: replace the noun
activities.intro: The core move of natural Croatian: once a person or thing is named, it becomes a clitic. Read each mini-exchange aloud — question with the noun, answer with the pronoun.

items.en: Do you see Marko? — I see him.
Do you know Ana? — I know her.
Are you waiting for me? — I'm waiting for you!
items.note: The pronouns swap perspective: your me is my te.

items.en: Do you see us? — I see you.

items.hr: Gledaš li taj film? — Gledam ga.
items.en: Are you watching that film? — I'm watching it.
items.note: ga also means 'it' for masculine things like film.

items.hr: Voliš li ove pjesme? — Volim ih.
items.en: Do you like these songs? — I like them.
This is for you.
items.note: Preposition za → stressed tebe.

items.en: He saw her yesterday.
items.note: ju instead of je, because the auxiliary je follows immediately.

activities.title: Pronoun replacement drill
activities.intro: Answer with clitics, reach for stressed forms after prepositions and in one-word answers. Speed is the goal — these must become reflexes.

questions.prompt: Which is the CLITIC (short) form for 'him'?
questions.explanation: njega/ga — ga is the short everyday form. (mu is dative 'to him' — different case.)

questions.prompt: Vidiš li Marka? — The natural answer is:
questions.explanation: Clitic by default: Vidim ga. 'Vidim njega' adds heavy emphasis (HIM, not someone else), and a clitic can never start the sentence.

questions.prompt: 'This is for ___.' (me) — Ovo je za ___.
questions.explanation: After a preposition the stressed form is obligatory: za mene, kod mene, bez mene.

questions.prompt: Answer with a pronoun: 'Poznaješ li Anu?' — 'Poznajem ___.'
questions.explanation: Ana → je (the standard feminine accusative clitic). ju is reserved for when the auxiliary je follows.

questions.prompt: Answer with a pronoun: 'Vidiš li nas?' — 'Vidim ___.'
questions.explanation: They asked about 'us', you answer about 'you (plural)': Vidim vas. Perspective flips with pronouns.

questions.prompt: Answer with a pronoun: 'Voliš li ove pjesme?' — 'Volim ___.'
questions.explanation: Plural things → ih (them): Volim ih. Stressed version: njih.

questions.prompt: 'Koga čekaš?' You answer with ONE word:
questions.explanation: A clitic can't stand alone — a one-word answer needs the stressed form: Njega. (On is nominative — wrong case.)

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Vidio ___ je jučer.' (her)
questions.explanation: Right next to the auxiliary je, the feminine clitic switches to ju: Vidio ju je jučer — avoiding the awkward 'je je'.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I see him every day on the tram.'
questions.answer: Vidim ga svaki dan u tramvaju.
questions.explanation: The clitic ga takes second position, straight after the verb: Vidim ga...

days.title: Clitic order — the sentence skeleton
days.objective: Place enclitics in the fixed order: li → auxiliary → dative → accusative → se → je.
days.paretoFocus: Clitic order is THE grammar that separates A2 from A1 speech. One rule ('second position, fixed internal order') fixes hundreds of future sentences.
drills: Do Unit 9 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · clitic order (redoslijed enklitika).
Build: Jučer sam mu ga dao. / Dala mi je knjigu.
Reorder 6 scrambled sentences into correct clitic order.

items: Recite the clitic order chain from memory.
Say 'I gave it to him yesterday.'
Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: The clitic chain: li → aux → DAT → ACC → se → je
activities.intro: All the little unstressed words gather in SECOND position of the clause, in one fixed internal order: the question particle li, then the auxiliary (sam, ću, bih...), then the dative clitic, then the accusative one, then se — and the auxiliary je, uniquely, goes last of all. Memorize the chain as a template; every example below is the chain in action.

items.hr: li → sam/ću/bih → mi/ti/mu/joj → me/te/ga/je → se → je
items.en: the fixed chain — question word, auxiliary, dative, accusative, se, and je dead last
items.note: This order NEVER varies. One rule, hundreds of correct sentences.

items.en: Yesterday I gave it to him.
items.note: aux (sam) → dative (mu) → accusative (ga).

items.en: I gave it to him yesterday.
items.note: Different first word, same rule: the cluster sits right after whatever comes first.

items.en: She gave me the book.
items.note: je jumps BEHIND the dative: mi je, never 'je mi'.

items.en: She gave it to me.
items.note: The full chain: dative (mi) → accusative (ga) → je last.

items.en: Did you give it to him?
items.note: In questions with jesi, li comes first in the cluster: li → mu → ga.

items.en: Can you send it to me?
items.note: li right after the verb, then dative, then accusative.

items.en: I'll get in touch with you tomorrow.
items.note: aux (ću) → dative (ti) → se.

items.en: She smiled at him.
items.note: When se is present, the auxiliary je DROPS in the 3rd person: not 'mu se je nasmijala'.

items.en: He saw her yesterday.
items.note: Feminine ju before je — yesterday's rule feeding into today's chain.

activities.title: Clitic order under the microscope
activities.intro: Six scrambles and the logic behind them. Say each solved sentence aloud twice — rhythm is how natives store this rule.

questions.prompt: In the clitic chain, what comes immediately after li?
questions.explanation: The chain: li → aux → dative → accusative → se → je. li opens, je closes.

questions.prompt: Which is correct for 'She gave it to me'?
questions.explanation: dative (mi) before accusative (ga), and je always last. je can also never start the sentence.

questions.prompt: Which order is correct inside the cluster: dative or accusative first?
options: Dative first: mi ga, ti je, mu ga
Accusative first: ga mi, je ti
questions.answer: Dative first: mi ga, ti je, mu ga
questions.explanation: Always dative → accusative: Daj MI GA (give me it), Poslao TI JE pismo... the receiver comes before the thing.

questions.prompt: Unscramble: 'Yesterday I gave it to him.'
questions.explanation: Jučer takes first position; then the whole cluster in chain order: sam (aux) → mu (dat) → ga (acc).

questions.prompt: Unscramble: 'She gave me the book.'
questions.explanation: The participle opens, then mi (dative) BEFORE je — because je always goes last in the cluster.

questions.prompt: Unscramble the question: 'Did you give it to him?'
questions.explanation: Jesi carries the stress, then the chain: li → mu (dat) → ga (acc). The participle dao closes.

questions.prompt: Complete with TWO clitics in the right order: 'Možeš li ___ ___ poslati?' (to me + it)
questions.explanation: Dative before accusative: mi ga. Full sentence: Možeš li mi ga poslati? — Can you send it to me?

questions.prompt: Unscramble: 'I'll get in touch with you tomorrow.'
questions.explanation: aux (ću) → dative (ti) → se, exactly as the chain predicts. se comes after all the pronoun clitics.

questions.prompt: Unscramble the polite question: 'Can you send it to me?'
questions.explanation: li → mi → ga: the question particle leads the cluster, then dative, then accusative. This exact sentence is day 99's speed drill — bank it now.

questions.prompt: 'Ona mu se nasmijala.' Why is there no 'je'?
options: With se, the 3rd-person auxiliary je is dropped
It's a mistake — je is required
questions.answer: With se, the 3rd-person auxiliary je is dropped
questions.explanation: se + je collapses to just se in the 3rd person singular: Ona mu se nasmijala, Ona se vratila. Everywhere else the auxiliary stays: Nasmijala sam mu se.

days.title: New present class: krenem, dođem
days.objective: Conjugate -(n)uti verbs (krenuti → krenem) and doći → dođem.
days.paretoFocus: This class covers key motion and event verbs (krenuti, stignuti, doći). With Unit 10 done you finish Module 1 of 8 — the system is working.
drills: Do Unit 10 of A2.hr — Module 1 complete!
Study Grammar tab: A2 · more present-tense classes.
Conjugate krenuti and doći in full.
Make 5 sentences about arriving and setting off this weekend.

items: Conjugate doći cold.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review clitic order with one dative+accusative sentence.

activities.title: New present class: krenem, dođem
activities.intro: A new family of verbs joins your present tense today, and they all take the regular -em endings. Verbs in -nuti conjugate with -nem (krenuti → krenem), and doći with its whole family gives -đem (dođem, nađem, uđem). These are the key motion and event verbs of everyday plans — learn the 1st person singular and the rest of the paradigm follows automatically.

items.en: to set off → I set off
items.note: Full paradigm: krenem, kreneš, krene, krenemo, krenete, krenu. Kad krenemo ranije, ne žurimo. (When we set off earlier, we don't hurry.)

items.en: to come, to arrive → I come
items.note: dođem, dođeš, dođe, dođemo, dođete, dođu. Dođem po tebe u osam. (I'll come pick you up at eight.)

items.en: to arrive, to make it → I arrive
items.note: stignem, stigneš… stignu. Javi mi se kad stigneš. (Let me know when you arrive.)

items.en: to set out → I set out
items.note: pođem, pođeš… pođu. Ako pođemo sada, stižemo na vrijeme. (If we set out now, we arrive on time.)

items.en: to leave, to go away → I leave
items.note: Irregular stem: odem, odeš, ode, odemo, odete, odu. Moram otići prije šest. (I have to leave before six.)

items.en: to go out → I go out
items.note: izađem… izađu. Kad izađem s posla, nazvat ću te. (When I get out of work, I'll call you.)

items.en: to enter → I enter
items.note: uđem… uđu. Imperative: Uđi, vrata su otvorena! (Come in, the door is open!)

items.en: to find → I find
items.note: nađem… nađu. With se it means to meet up: Nađemo se u pet? (Shall we meet at five?) — the perfective present is normal in arrangements.

items.en: to turn (left/right) → I turn
items.note: skrenem… skrenu. Skreni desno kod pekare. (Turn right at the bakery.)

items.en: to take off; to download → I take off
items.note: skinem… skinu. Skinem jaknu čim uđem u stan. (I take my jacket off as soon as I enter the flat.)

items.en: to lift, to raise → I lift
items.note: dignem… dignu. With se, colloquially 'get up': Ujutro se teško dignem iz kreveta.

items.en: to begin → I begin
items.note: Not a -nuti verb, but the same -nem pattern: počnem, počneš… počnu. Čim počne kiša, idemo unutra. (As soon as the rain starts, we go inside.)

activities.title: krenem, dođem, stignem in action
activities.intro: Conjugate the new class and keep your clitic order intact while you do it.

questions.prompt: What is the present form of 'doći' for 'oni' (they)?
questions.explanation: doći follows the -em class: dođem, dođeš, dođe, dođemo, dođete, dođu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'stići' (ti): Javi mi se kad ______ kući.
questions.explanation: stići → stignem, stigneš… Javi mi se kad stigneš kući. (Let me know when you get home.)

questions.prompt: Choose the correct present form of 'krenuti' for 'mi' (we):
questions.explanation: krenuti → krenem, so 'mi' is krenemo. (krenimo is the imperative 'let's set off'.)

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'otići' (ona): Ona uvijek ______ prva s posla.
questions.explanation: otići has an irregular present stem: odem, odeš, ode… Ona uvijek ode prva. (She always leaves first.)

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Shall we meet in front of the cinema at eight?'
questions.answer: Nađemo se ispred kina u osam
questions.explanation: Nađemo se… is the standard way to propose meeting up; the clitic se sits in second position.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has correct clitic order? (I'll bring it to him tomorrow.)
questions.explanation: The clitic chain in second position: auxiliary (ću) → dative (mu) → accusative (ga).

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'doći' (vi): Kad ______ k nama?
questions.explanation: doći → dođem, and for 'vi' it is dođete: Kad dođete k nama? (When are you coming to our place?)

questions.prompt: Čim uđem u stan, ______ jaknu.
questions.explanation: skinuti → skinem (ja). As soon as I enter the flat, I take off my jacket.

days.title: Invitations & arrangements
days.objective: Invite, accept and politely decline in Croatian.
days.paretoFocus: Arranging to meet is a core A2 can-do; a handful of fixed patterns (Dođi k nama! / Može! / Žao mi je, ne mogu…) covers the whole social ritual.
drills: Do Unit 11 of A2.hr.
Learn: Dođi k nama u subotu! / Hoćemo li se naći…? / Odgovara li ti…?
Role-play one invitation accepted and one declined with a reason.
Practise pronoun clitics inside the invitations (Vidimo se! Javi mi se!).

items: Invite someone to coffee from memory.
Decline politely with 'jer' + reason.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Invitations & arrangements: Dođi k nama!
activities.intro: Arranging to meet is a core A2 skill, and a handful of fixed patterns covers the whole social ritual: inviting, proposing a time, accepting, and declining politely with a reason. Notice how pronoun clitics live inside almost every phrase (Javi mi se! Odgovara li ti…?).

items.en: Come to our place on Saturday!
items.note: k + dative = to someone's place. Formal/plural: Dođite k nama!

items.hr: Hoćemo li se naći u petak navečer?
items.en: Shall we meet on Friday evening?
items.note: li right after the verb, then the clitic se — the standard question for arranging.

items.en: Does Sunday work for you?
items.note: odgovarati + dative: the day 'answers to' you. Formal: Odgovara li Vam…?

items.en: When suits you?
items.note: The open version of the same question.

items.en: Sure! / That works!
items.note: The universal casual yes to any proposal.

items.en: Deal! / Agreed!
items.note: Seals an arrangement: Vidimo se u pet. — Vrijedi!

items.en: I'll gladly come.
items.note: rado = gladly; a warm way to accept.

items.en: I'm sorry, I can't.
items.note: The polite refusal opener — always soften with žao mi je.

items.hr: Ne mogu jer imam već dogovor.
items.en: I can't because I already have an arrangement.
items.note: Decline + jer + reason. dogovor = an arrangement/appointment.

items.en: Maybe another time.
items.note: Keeps the door open after declining.

items.en: Get in touch with me!
items.note: javiti se + dative. The clitics mi se follow the imperative directly.

items.en: See you!
items.note: Literally 'we see each other' — the default goodbye between friends.

activities.title: Invite, accept, decline
activities.intro: Run the whole invitation ritual: propose, check the time, say yes, say no politely — with every clitic in its place.

questions.prompt: Your friend asks: 'Hoćemo li se naći u petak?' How do you casually accept?
questions.explanation: Može! (literally 'it can') is the universal casual yes. Vrijedi! works too.

questions.prompt: Complete the invitation: Dođi ___ nama u subotu!
questions.explanation: k + dative = to someone's place: Dođi k nama! (Come to our place!)

questions.prompt: Which is the polite way to decline an invitation?
options: Žao mi je, ne mogu, imam već dogovor.
questions.answer: Žao mi je, ne mogu, imam već dogovor.
questions.explanation: Soften with žao mi je (I'm sorry), then give a reason — the standard polite refusal.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Shall we meet on Friday?'
questions.answer: Hoćemo li se naći u petak
questions.explanation: Verb first, then the clitics in fixed order: li before se — Hoćemo li se naći…?

questions.prompt: Complete with the missing clitic: Javi ___ se sutra!
questions.explanation: javiti se + dative: Javi mi se! = Get in touch with me! Dative clitic (mi) comes before se.

questions.prompt: Asking a friend if Saturday works: Odgovara li ___ subota?
questions.explanation: odgovarati takes the dative: Odgovara li ti subota? (Does Saturday suit you?)

questions.prompt: Decline with a reason: Ne mogu doći ______ imam previše posla.
questions.explanation: jer = because. Ne mogu doći jer imam previše posla. (I can't come because I have too much work.)

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Let's meet in front of the café at five.'
questions.answer: Nađimo se ispred kafića u pet
questions.explanation: Nađimo se = let's meet (imperative of naći se); ispred + genitive (kafića).

activities.title: Roštilj u subotu — sending the invitations
activities.intro: You and your wife are planning a Saturday barbecue and inviting friends. Read your lines ('Me') aloud — one friend accepts, one declines, and every phrase is from today's lesson.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dušo, hoćemo li pozvati Anu i Marka na roštilj u subotu?
lines.en: Honey, shall we invite Ana and Marko to a barbecue on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može! Ja ću im poslati poruku. U koliko sati?
lines.en: Sure! I'll send them a message. At what time?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Oko pet. Odgovara li ti to?
lines.en: Around five. Does that work for you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Odgovara. Napisao sam im: 'Dođite k nama u subotu oko pet!'
lines.en: It works. I wrote to them: 'Come to our place on Saturday around five!'

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. Što je Ana odgovorila?
lines.en: Great. What did Ana reply?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ana je napisala: 'Rado ću doći! Vidimo se u subotu!'
lines.en: Ana wrote: 'I'll gladly come! See you on Saturday!'

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično! A Marko? Može li on?
lines.en: Excellent! And Marko? Can he make it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Marko ne može. Žao mu je, ali već ima dogovor.
lines.en: Marko can't. He's sorry, but he already has an arrangement.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Šteta. Onda ćemo ga pozvati drugi put.
lines.en: A pity. Then we'll invite him another time.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vrijedi. Javit ću mu se sljedeći tjedan.
lines.en: Deal. I'll get in touch with him next week.

days.title: Write it: a weekend-plan message
days.objective: Write a natural 8-10 sentence message arranging the weekend.
days.paretoFocus: Writing forces you to place every clitic and pronoun deliberately — and a native correction from your partner is free tutoring.
drills: Do Unit 12 of A2.hr.
Write a message to a friend proposing a weekend plan (time, place, activity).
Check every enclitic sits in second position.
Read it to your partner and ask for corrections.

items: Read your message aloud for flow.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review invitation phrases.

activities.title: Building a weekend-plan message
activities.intro: Today you write a real message arranging the weekend. These are the building blocks natives use — greeting, plan, time, practical details, and sign-off. Watch the enclitics: in writing, every ću/mi/se must sit in second position, and a message is the perfect place to check each one deliberately.

items.en: Hi! What are you doing on Saturday?
items.note: The standard casual opener for making plans.

items.hr: Planiramo roštilj kod nas u dvorištu.
items.en: We're planning a barbecue at our place in the yard.
items.note: kod nas = at our place.

items.en: We start around five in the afternoon.
items.note: oko + genitive for approximate time: oko pet(a sata).

items.hr: Bit će i Ana i njezin muž.
items.en: Ana and her husband will be there too.
items.note: Bit će = futur I of biti; i… i… = both… and…

items.hr: Ponesi nešto za piće ako želiš.
items.en: Bring something to drink if you like.
items.note: ponijeti → imperative ponesi.

items.en: If it rains, we stay inside.
items.note: A simple real condition with ako + present.

items.en: Let me know whether you can come.
items.note: Indirect question with li: možeš li = whether you can.

items.hr: Možemo se naći i ranije ako ti odgovara.
items.en: We can also meet earlier if that suits you.
items.note: se in second position of its clause: Možemo se naći…

items.en: I'm looking forward to it!
items.note: veseliti se — the reflexive se is obligatory.

items.en: Say hi to Ivana!
items.note: pozdraviti + accusative — a warm closing touch.

items.en: See you on Saturday!
items.note: The classic sign-off for a plan message.

items.hr: P. S. Adresu ću ti poslati sutra.
items.en: P.S. I'll send you the address tomorrow.
items.note: Fronting Adresu pushes the clitics ću ti into second position — classic Croatian word order.

activities.title: Every clitic in second position
activities.intro: A written message is a clitic exam. Place ću, ti, se and li exactly where they belong.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I'll get in touch with you tomorrow.'
questions.explanation: Second position chain: auxiliary (ću) → dative (ti) → se. Javit ću ti se sutra.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has correct clitic order?
questions.explanation: After the first stressed word (Adresu), the clitics follow in order: ću (aux) → ti (dative).

questions.prompt: Complete the indirect question: Javi mi možeš ___ doći.
questions.explanation: možeš li = whether you can. li always attaches right after the verb.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Will you send me the address?'
questions.explanation: Question chain: verb + li + dative clitic (mi), then the infinitive and object.

questions.prompt: 'I'm looking forward to it!' = Veselim ___!
questions.explanation: veseliti se is reflexive — the se can never be dropped: Veselim se!

questions.prompt: Complete with the missing clitic: Naći ćemo ___ ispred ulaza.
questions.explanation: naći se = to meet up. The chain is ćemo (aux) → se: Naći ćemo se ispred ulaza.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
options: Sutra ćemo se naći u parku.
Sutra se ćemo naći u parku.
Ćemo se sutra naći u parku.
Sutra ćemo naći se u parku.
questions.answer: Sutra ćemo se naći u parku.
questions.explanation: Clitics in second position, auxiliary before se: Sutra ćemo se naći…

questions.prompt: Put the words in order (talking about 'broj' — the number): 'I'll send it to you tomorrow.'
questions.explanation: Full chain in action: ću (aux) → ti (dative) → ga (accusative). Poslat ću ti ga sutra.

days.title: Week 10 review + speaking: plan a real weekend
days.objective: Negotiate real weekend plans entirely in Croatian.
days.paretoFocus: Using this week's clitics and pronouns to plan something that actually happens is the strongest possible memory glue.
drills: 15-minute conversation: plan the coming weekend in Croatian (tutor, partner or spouse).
Re-do the trickiest exercise from Units 7-12.
Apply any corrections you received on your posted message.
Record a 60-second summary of the agreed plan.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say 3 sentences with perfect clitic order.
Recall the accusative pronoun pairs (mene/me…).

activities.title: Week 10 in ten lines
activities.intro: Everything from this week compressed into ten model sentences: weekend plans in futur I, object pronouns in both forms, and the clitic chain that holds Croatian sentences together. If you can produce all ten cold, week 10 is yours.

items.hr: Ići ću na izlet u subotu.
items.en: I'll go on a trip on Saturday.
items.note: Futur I: infinitive + ću. Fronted verb form: Ići ću…

items.en: We'll meet at five.
items.note: ćemo → se: auxiliary before the reflexive clitic.

items.en: me, you, him, her — stressed/clitic accusative pairs
items.note: The short (clitic) forms are what natives use 95% of the time; also: nas, vas, njih/ih.

items.en: Do you see me? — I see you.
items.note: Clitic pronouns in the most common exchange there is.

items.hr: Poznaješ li Marka? — Da, poznajem ga.
items.en: Do you know Marko? — Yes, I know him.
items.note: Replace the noun with the clitic pronoun in the answer.

items.en: This is for you.
items.note: After prepositions you must use the stressed form: za tebe, never za te.

items.en: I gave it to him yesterday.
items.note: The full chain: sam (aux) → mu (dative) → ga (accusative).

items.en: She gave me the book.
items.note: je (the 3rd person auxiliary) always goes last in the chain: mi → je.

items.hr: Krenut ćemo rano, a stići ćemo oko podneva.
items.en: We'll set off early and arrive around noon.
items.note: This week's new verbs (krenuti, stići) in futur I.

items.hr: Javit ću ti se kad stignem.
items.en: I'll get in touch with you when I arrive.
items.note: ću → ti → se, then the perfective present (stignem) in the kad-clause.

activities.title: Week 10 checkpoint — with listening
activities.intro: A mixed review of futur I, object pronouns and clitic order — including three listening tasks. Play each audio and catch the essentials, just like you would in a real voice message.

questions.prompt: Listen: what is the plan?
options: A trip to Samobor on Saturday at nine
A movie on Friday at nine
questions.answer: A trip to Samobor on Saturday at nine
questions.explanation: U subotu idemo na izlet u Samobor. Krećemo u devet ujutro. = On Saturday we're going on a trip to Samobor. We set off at nine in the morning.
questions.audioText: U subotu idemo na izlet u Samobor. Krećemo u devet ujutro.

questions.prompt: Listen: what does the speaker propose?
options: Meeting on Sunday instead of Friday
questions.answer: Meeting on Sunday instead of Friday
questions.explanation: Žao mi je, ne mogu doći u petak. Imam već dogovor. Može li u nedjelju? = Sorry, I can't come on Friday — I already have an arrangement. How about Sunday?
questions.audioText: Žao mi je, ne mogu doći u petak. Imam već dogovor. Može li u nedjelju?

questions.prompt: Listen and type the missing clitic: Javit ću ___ se kad stignem.
questions.explanation: Javit ću ti se kad stignem. — the dative clitic ti comes between ću and se.
questions.audioText: Javit ću ti se kad stignem.

questions.prompt: Vidiš li Anu? — Da, vidim ___.
questions.explanation: The accusative clitic for 'her' is je: Vidim je. (Next to the auxiliary je it switches to ju: Vidio ju je.)

questions.prompt: Replace the noun with a pronoun: Poznaješ li Marka? — Da, poznajem ___.
questions.explanation: njega/ga — the clitic ga stands for Marka: Poznajem ga. (I know him.)

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I gave it to him yesterday.'
questions.explanation: Second position after Jučer: sam (aux) → mu (dative) → ga (accusative), then the participle.

questions.prompt: Što ćeš raditi u subotu? — ______ na roštilj kod prijatelja.
questions.explanation: ću can't stand first, so the infinitive fronts it: Ići ću na roštilj. (I'll go to a barbecue.)

questions.prompt: How do you say 'Can you send it to me?' (it = paket)
questions.explanation: The chain: li → dative (mi) → accusative (ga). Možeš li mi ga poslati?

questions.prompt: Complete with the missing preposition: Ovo je ___ tebe.
questions.explanation: za tebe = for you. After a preposition the stressed form (tebe) is obligatory.

activities.title: Planning the real weekend
activities.intro: Saturday is coming and this plan is actually happening. Negotiate the whole weekend with your wife — market, barbecue at Ana's, and a quiet Sunday — using this week's futur I, pronouns and clitics.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dušo, kakav nam je plan za vikend?
lines.en: Honey, what's our plan for the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U subotu ujutro idemo na plac, a poslije možemo na kavu.
lines.en: On Saturday morning we're going to the market, and afterwards we can go for coffee.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Može. A poslijepodne? Ana nas je pozvala na roštilj.
lines.en: Sure. And in the afternoon? Ana invited us to a barbecue.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Znam, odgovorio sam joj jučer. Rekao sam da dolazimo oko pet.
lines.en: I know, I replied to her yesterday. I said we're coming around five.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično. Hoćemo li joj nešto ponijeti?
lines.en: Excellent. Shall we bring her something?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ponijet ćemo vino. Kupit ću ga u subotu ujutro na placu.
lines.en: We'll bring wine. I'll buy it on Saturday morning at the market.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Savršeno. A u nedjelju? Ja se želim odmoriti.
lines.en: Perfect. And on Sunday? I want to rest.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Naravno. Ujutro ćemo dugo spavati, a onda idemo u šetnju uz rijeku.
lines.en: Of course. In the morning we'll sleep in, and then we're going for a walk along the river.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Baš lijepo. Kad krećemo u subotu?
lines.en: How nice. When do we set off on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Krenut ćemo u devet. Javit ću ti se s placa ako nešto trebamo.
lines.en: We'll set off at nine. I'll get in touch with you from the market if we need anything.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dogovoreno. Veselim se vikendu!
lines.en: Agreed. I'm looking forward to the weekend!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: I ja. Napokon slobodni dani!
lines.en: Me too. Free days at last!

days.title: Kvaliteta života — how you live
days.objective: Describe your home, neighbourhood and daily habits.
days.paretoFocus: Quality-of-life vocabulary (stan, kvart, navike, slobodno vrijeme) powers the conversations natives most like to have — comparing how people live.
drills: Do Unit 13 of A2.hr.
Learn 15 home-and-lifestyle words: stan, kvart, susjed, navika, odmor, buka, mir…
Describe your home in 5 sentences.
Say one habit you love and one you want to change.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Describe your neighbourhood in 3 sentences.
Review weekend-plan vocabulary.

activities.title: Kvaliteta života — home, neighbourhood, habits
activities.intro: Quality-of-life vocabulary powers the conversations natives most like to have: how people live, what their neighbourhood is like, which habits they love. These fourteen words are the core kit — each comes with a sentence you can reuse about your own life.

items.en: flat, apartment
items.note: Živim u stanu u centru. (I live in a flat in the centre.)

items.en: neighbourhood
items.note: Naš kvart je miran i zelen. (Our neighbourhood is quiet and green.)

items.en: neighbour (m/f)
items.note: Naši susjedi su jako ljubazni. (Our neighbours are very kind.)

items.en: building
items.note: Stanujemo u staroj zgradi bez lifta. (We live in an old building without a lift.)

items.en: yard, courtyard
items.note: Djeca se igraju u dvorištu. (The children play in the yard.)

items.en: habit
items.note: Jutarnja kava je moja najdraža navika. (Morning coffee is my favourite habit.)

items.en: rest; vacation
items.note: Trebam odmor od posla. (I need a rest from work.)

items.en: noise
items.note: Ne mogu spavati od buke. (I can't sleep because of the noise.)

items.en: peace, quiet
items.note: Navečer je u kvartu potpuni mir. (In the evening the neighbourhood is completely quiet.)

items.en: nature
items.note: Vikendom volimo biti u prirodi. (On weekends we love being in nature.)

items.en: a walk
items.note: Idemo u šetnju poslije večere. (We go for a walk after dinner.)

items.en: free time
items.note: U slobodno vrijeme čitam i kuham. (In my free time I read and cook.)

items.en: costs, expenses
items.note: Troškovi života u centru su visoki. (The cost of living in the centre is high.)

items.en: closeness, vicinity
items.note: U blizini je park i tržnica. (There's a park and a market nearby.)

activities.title: How you live: vocabulary in context
activities.intro: Use the new lifestyle words in real sentences about homes, habits and neighbourhoods.

questions.prompt: Naš ______ je jako tih — nema puno prometa.
questions.explanation: kvart = neighbourhood. Naš kvart je jako tih. (Our neighbourhood is very quiet.)

questions.prompt: Complete (buka, after 'od'): Ne mogu spavati od ______ .
questions.explanation: od + genitive: buka → buke. Ne mogu spavati od buke. (I can't sleep because of the noise.)

questions.prompt: What does 'susjed' mean?
questions.explanation: susjed = neighbour (male); susjeda = female neighbour.

questions.prompt: Complete: Svako jutro pijem kavu na balkonu — to je moja ______ .
questions.explanation: navika = habit. A daily routine you repeat is your navika.

questions.prompt: U slobodno vrijeme volim ______ u prirodi.
questions.explanation: After voljeti you need the infinitive: volim šetati (I love walking). šetnja is the noun 'a walk'.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I live in a quiet neighbourhood near the park.'
questions.answer: Živim u mirnom kvartu blizu parka
questions.explanation: u + locative (mirnom kvartu), blizu + genitive (parka).

questions.prompt: U centru je uvijek buka, a u našem kvartu je ______ .
questions.explanation: The classic contrast: buka (noise) vs mir (peace and quiet).

questions.prompt: Complete (blizina, after 'u'): Volim svoj stan jer je sve u ______ .
questions.explanation: u blizini = nearby (locative of blizina). Sve je u blizini. (Everything is close by.)

questions.prompt: Želim promijeniti jednu ______ : previše gledam mobitel.
questions.explanation: Direct object of promijeniti → accusative: navika → naviku.

days.title: Locative across the whole phrase
days.objective: Decline adjectives, demonstratives and possessives in the locative.
days.paretoFocus: 'U ovom malom stanu', 'u svojoj sobi' — full locative phrases let you say precisely where things happen, the backbone of describing your life.
drills: Do Unit 14 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · adjective declension (locative).
Decline 6 phrases: taj veliki grad → u tom velikom gradu.
Write 5 sentences about where you live, work and relax.

items: Say 'in my own room' (u svojoj sobi).
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Contrast accusative (motion) vs locative (location) in one pair.

activities.title: Locative across the whole phrase
activities.intro: You already put nouns in the locative; now the whole phrase agrees. Masculine and neuter modifiers take -om(e) (u tom velikom gradu), soft stems take -em (na trećem katu, u našem dvorištu), and feminine takes -oj (u našoj novoj kući). Bonus: these adjective endings are identical in the dative, so you're learning two cases at once.

items.hr: taj veliki grad → u tom velikom gradu
items.en: that big city → in that big city
items.note: Masculine locative: both modifiers take -om.

items.hr: moj mali stan → u mom malom stanu
items.en: my small flat → in my small flat
items.note: moj → mom (also mome/mojem). Živim u mom malom stanu.

items.hr: naša nova kuća → u našoj novoj kući
items.en: our new house → in our new house
items.note: Feminine: modifiers take -oj, the noun takes -i.

items.en: one's own room → in one's own room
items.note: svoj declines like moj: Radim u svojoj sobi. (I work in my own room.)

items.hr: ovaj tihi kvart → u ovom tihom kvartu
items.en: this quiet neighbourhood → in this quiet neighbourhood
items.note: ovaj → ovom, tihi → tihom.

items.hr: to lijepo selo → u tom lijepom selu
items.en: that pretty village → in that pretty village
items.note: Neuter takes the same -om endings as masculine.

items.en: third floor → on the third floor
items.note: Soft stem ć → -em: trećem, not trećom. Stan je na trećem katu.

items.hr: naše veliko dvorište → u našem velikom dvorištu
items.en: our big yard → in our big yard
items.note: naš is a soft stem too: našem.

items.hr: jedna mala kuhinja → u jednoj maloj kuhinji
items.en: one small kitchen → in one small kitchen
items.note: Feminine chain: jednoj maloj kuhinji.

items.hr: tvoj stari kvart → u tvom starom kvartu
items.en: your old neighbourhood → in your old neighbourhood
items.note: tvoj → tvom, stari → starom.

items.en: the main square → on the main square
items.note: Squares and open spaces take na: na glavnom trgu.

activities.title: u tom velikom gradu — full phrase practice
activities.intro: Decline whole phrases in the locative and keep the accusative-vs-locative contrast (motion vs location) sharp.

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of 'moj': Živim u ______ malom stanu.
questions.explanation: moj → mom (mome/mojem) in the locative: u mom malom stanu.

questions.prompt: Radim u ______ velikom gradu. (taj)
questions.explanation: taj → tom in the locative: u tom velikom gradu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of 'svoj': Radim u ______ sobi.
questions.explanation: Feminine locative -oj: u svojoj sobi = in my own room (the owner is the subject, so svoj).

questions.prompt: Stan je na ______ katu.
questions.explanation: treći has a soft stem (ć), so the locative is trećem: na trećem katu.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I live in this quiet neighbourhood.'
questions.explanation: The whole phrase agrees in the locative: ovom tihom kvartu.

questions.prompt: Motion or location? Svaki dan idem ______ .
questions.explanation: ići expresses motion → accusative: idem u veliki grad. Location (living there) would be locative.

questions.prompt: Motion or location? Cijeli život živim ______ .
questions.explanation: živjeti expresses location → locative: živim u velikom gradu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of 'naš': Odmaramo se u ______ novoj kući.
questions.explanation: Feminine locative -oj: u našoj novoj kući.

questions.prompt: Baka živi ______ .
questions.explanation: selo is neuter and takes the same locative endings as masculine: u tom lijepom selu.

days.title: Talking about where you live
days.objective: Give a rich, connected description of your living situation.
days.paretoFocus: This is the A2 can-do 'describing home and surroundings' — and it forces yesterday's locative phrases into real use.
drills: Do Unit 15 of A2.hr.
Describe your flat room by room, with adjectives agreeing in the locative.
Compare where you live now with where you grew up (2-3 sentences).
