Ask and answer: U kakvom stanu živiš? Što ti se sviđa u svom kvartu?

items: Deliver a 60-second home tour aloud.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review locative phrase endings.

activities.title: Talking about where you live
activities.intro: Time to describe your home like a native: which flat, which floor, what you love and what bothers you. Notice two key constructions — sviđati se and smetati both put YOU in the dative (sviđa mi se, smeta mi) while the thing itself is the subject.

items.en: I live in a two-room flat.
items.note: dvosoban = two-room; jednosoban, trosoban follow the same pattern.

items.hr: Stan je na trećem katu, u mirnoj zgradi.
items.en: The flat is on the third floor, in a quiet building.
items.note: Yesterday's locative phrases doing real work.

items.hr: Imamo velik balkon s pogledom na park.
items.en: We have a big balcony with a view of the park.
items.note: s pogledom na + accusative = with a view of.

items.hr: U mom kvartu ima puno kafića i mala tržnica.
items.en: In my neighbourhood there are lots of cafés and a small market.
items.note: ima = there is/are.

items.en: I love my own study most of all.
items.note: svoju because the owner (I) is the subject; radna soba = study/workroom.

items.hr: Sviđa mi se što je sve blizu.
items.en: I like that everything is close.
items.note: sviđati se + dative: the liker is mi, the liked thing is the subject.

items.en: The noise from the street bothers me.
items.note: smetati works the same way: smeta mi = it bothers me.

items.hr: Odrastao sam u maloj kući s dvorištem.
items.en: I grew up in a small house with a yard.
items.note: odrasti → odrastao sam (masculine participle); s + instrumental: s dvorištem.

items.hr: Prije sam živio u kući, a sada živim u stanu.
items.en: Before, I lived in a house, and now I live in a flat.
items.note: The then-vs-now frame you'll reuse constantly.

items.en: What kind of flat do you live in?
items.note: kakav declines too: u kakvom stanu (locative).

items.hr: Što ti se sviđa u svom kvartu?
items.en: What do you like about your neighbourhood?
items.note: The question that starts every good conversation about how people live.

activities.title: Describe your home
activities.intro: Practise the home-description toolkit: dative constructions with sviđati se and smetati, plus locative phrases about rooms and floors.

questions.prompt: Sviđa ___ se tvoj stan. (I like your flat.)
questions.explanation: sviđati se puts the liker in the dative: Sviđa mi se tvoj stan.

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of 'kakav': U ______ stanu živiš?
questions.explanation: kakav → kakvom in the locative: U kakvom stanu živiš? (What kind of flat do you live in?)

questions.prompt: Smeta mi ______ s ulice.
questions.explanation: With smetati, the annoying thing is the SUBJECT (nominative buka); the person is dative (mi).

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I love my own study most of all.'
questions.explanation: svoju (not moju) because the owner is the subject; the whole phrase agrees in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of 'kuća': Odrastao sam u maloj ______ .
questions.explanation: Feminine locative: kuća → kući. Odrastao sam u maloj kući. (I grew up in a small house.)

questions.prompt: Što ti se sviđa u ______ kvartu?
questions.explanation: svoj → svom in the locative: u svom kvartu (in your own neighbourhood — the owner is the subject 'you').

questions.prompt: Imamo velik balkon s ______ na park.
questions.explanation: s + instrumental: s pogledom na park = with a view of the park.

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of 'dvosoban': Živim u ______ stanu.
questions.explanation: dvosoban → dvosobnom (locative, with fleeting a dropped): u dvosobnom stanu.

activities.title: Naš stan, naš kvart — a home conversation
activities.intro: An evening chat with your wife about your flat, your neighbourhood, and where each of you grew up. Her lines use feminine past forms (odrasla, živjela) — a perfect model of participle agreement.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Znaš, jako volim naš stan. A ti?
lines.en: You know, I really love our flat. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: I ja. Najviše volim našu dnevnu sobu jer je svijetla.
lines.en: Me too. I love our living room most because it's bright.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Meni se najviše sviđa kuhinja. U njoj pijemo jutarnju kavu.
lines.en: I like the kitchen most. We drink our morning coffee in it.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Istina. A sviđa mi se i kvart — sve je u blizini.
lines.en: True. And I like the neighbourhood too — everything is close by.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Da, ali ponekad mi smeta buka s ulice.
lines.en: Yes, but sometimes the noise from the street bothers me.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Meni ne smeta. Odrastao sam u velikom gradu, navikao sam.
lines.en: It doesn't bother me. I grew up in a big city, I'm used to it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja sam odrasla u malom selu, kod bake. Tamo je bio potpuni mir.
lines.en: I grew up in a small village, at my grandma's. It was completely peaceful there.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U kakvoj kući si živjela?
lines.en: What kind of house did you live in?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: U staroj kamenoj kući s velikim dvorištem. A ti?
lines.en: In an old stone house with a big yard. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja sam živio u stanu na desetom katu. Pogled je bio super, ali dvorište nismo imali.
lines.en: I lived in a flat on the tenth floor. The view was great, but we didn't have a yard.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hoćemo li jednog dana kupiti kuću s dvorištem?
lines.en: Shall we buy a house with a yard one day?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Rado! Ali samo ako je blizu dobrog kafića.
lines.en: Gladly! But only if it's near a good café.

days.title: Verb aspect — one idea, two verbs
days.objective: Understand the perfective/imperfective pair system properly.
days.paretoFocus: Aspect is the heart of the Slavic verb. Grasping that pisati/napisati are one meaning with two viewpoints unlocks how natives think about every action.
drills: Do Unit 16 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · verbal aspect (glagolski vid).
Sort 10 verbs into imperfective/perfective: pisati/napisati, kupovati/kupiti, piti/popiti…
For 5 daily actions, decide: process or completed result?

items: Explain aspect in one sentence, out loud.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Give both members of 5 aspect pairs.

activities.title: Verb aspect — one idea, two verbs
activities.intro: Almost every Croatian verb comes in a pair: an IMPERFECTIVE verb for ongoing, repeated or unfinished actions, and a PERFECTIVE verb for one completed event. English hides this in tenses ('was writing' vs 'wrote'); Croatian builds it into the verb itself. One key rule: perfective verbs can't describe what's happening right now — for 'I am writing' you must say Pišem, never Napišem. Learn these twelve pairs as single vocabulary items.

items.en: to write (process / completed)
items.note: Pišem mail. (I'm writing an email.) → Napisao sam mail. (I wrote it — it's done.)

items.en: to read (process / read through)
items.note: Čitam knjigu. → Pročitao sam knjigu. (I finished the book.)

items.en: to buy (habitually / once)
items.note: Kupujem namirnice svaki dan. → Jučer sam kupio vino.

items.en: to drink (process / drink up)
items.note: Pijem kavu. → Popio sam kavu i krenuo na posao.

items.en: to eat (process / eat up)
items.note: Jedem ručak. → Pojeo sam sve. (I ate everything.)

items.en: to watch (process / take a look)
items.note: Gledam film. → Pogledaj ovo! (Take a look at this!)

items.en: to study / to learn (master)
items.note: Učim hrvatski. → Naučio sam padeže. (I've learned the cases.)

items.en: to come (repeatedly) / to arrive (once)
items.note: Dolazi svaki dan. → Došao je u pet. (He arrived at five.)

items.en: to give (repeatedly) / to give (once)
items.note: Baka nam daje kolače. → Dala mi je knjigu.

items.en: to begin (be starting) / to begin (once)
items.note: Film počinje u osam. → Film je počeo prije pet minuta.

items.en: to pay (regularly) / to pay (once)
items.note: Plaćam račune svaki mjesec. → Platio sam račun i izašao.

items.en: to call (be calling) / to call up (once)
items.note: Zvao me cijelo jutro. → Nazvao me jučer navečer.

activities.title: Process or completed result?
activities.intro: For every action decide: is it a process, habit or duration (imperfective), or one whole completed event (perfective)?

questions.prompt: Which of these verbs is PERFECTIVE?
questions.explanation: napisati = to write and finish (one completed act). pisati, čitati and kupovati are all imperfective.

questions.prompt: Right now: 'I am writing an email.' = ?
questions.explanation: A perfective verb can't describe an action in progress — for right now you need the imperfective: Pišem.

questions.prompt: Choose the right verb and form (piti/popiti, ja): Svaki dan ______ kavu poslije ručka.
questions.explanation: svaki dan = a habit → imperfective: pijem. (popijem would stress finishing the whole cup each time — pijem is the neutral habit choice.)

questions.prompt: Jučer sam ______ cijelu knjigu. (I read the whole book.)
questions.explanation: cijelu knjigu = the whole book, completed → perfective: pročitao sam.

questions.prompt: Choose the right infinitive (učiti/naučiti): Učim hrvatski već godinu dana i želim ga dobro ______ .
questions.explanation: The goal is the completed result (to master it) → perfective: naučiti.

questions.prompt: The verb 'kupovati' is…
questions.explanation: kupovati = to be buying / buy habitually. Its perfective partner is kupiti.

questions.prompt: Ona ______ namirnice svaki dan.
questions.explanation: svaki dan = habit → imperfective present: kupuje (from kupovati).

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I wrote the email and sent it.'
questions.answer: Napisao sam mail i poslao ga
questions.explanation: Two completed events in sequence → both perfective: napisao… i poslao.

questions.prompt: What is the perfective partner of 'davati'?
questions.explanation: davati (give repeatedly) / dati (give once) — one of the highest-frequency pairs.

days.title: Aspect in the past
days.objective: Choose the right aspect when narrating: background vs event.
days.paretoFocus: 'Dok sam pisao pismo, nazvala me sestra.' Imperfective paints the scene, perfective moves the story — the rule behind every anecdote you'll ever tell.
drills: Do Unit 17 of A2.hr.
Rewrite 5 past sentences switching aspect and observe the meaning change.
Tell a 4-sentence story: background imperfective, events perfective.
Underline every verb in the story and justify its aspect.

items: Re-tell your mini-story from memory.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall 5 aspect pairs cold.

activities.title: Aspect in the past: background vs event
activities.intro: In a story, the imperfective paints the scene (what was going on) and the perfective moves the plot (what happened). 'Dok sam pisao pismo, nazvala me je sestra' — the writing is the background, the call is the event. This is the rule behind every anecdote you'll ever tell in Croatian.

items.hr: Pisao sam pismo. / Napisao sam pismo.
items.en: I was writing the letter. / I wrote (finished) the letter.
items.note: Same verb pair, completely different claim: in progress vs done.

items.hr: Dok sam pisao pismo, nazvala me je sestra.
items.en: While I was writing the letter, my sister called me.
items.note: dok + imperfective (background), perfective for the event that interrupts.

items.hr: Dok smo večerali, netko je pozvonio na vrata.
items.en: While we were having dinner, someone rang the doorbell.
items.note: večerati sets the scene; pozvoniti is the single event.

items.en: I was cooking lunch all morning.
items.note: Duration (cijelo jutro) → imperfective kuhati.

items.hr: Skuhao sam ručak i pozvao ženu za stol.
items.en: I cooked lunch (it's ready) and called my wife to the table.
items.note: kuhati/skuhati — a sequence of completed events is perfective + perfective.

items.hr: Svake subote smo išli na plac.
items.en: Every Saturday we went (used to go) to the market.
items.note: Repeated habit in the past → imperfective ići.

items.hr: Jedne subote smo otišli na izlet u Samobor.
items.en: One Saturday we went on a trip to Samobor.
items.note: One specific event → perfective otići.

items.hr: Gledao sam film kad je stigla tvoja poruka.
items.en: I was watching a film when your message arrived.
items.note: Background gledati, event stići.

items.hr: Pogledao sam film i otišao spavati.
items.en: I watched the film (to the end) and went to bed.
items.note: Both completed, one after the other → both perfective.

items.hr: Dugo sam učio hrvatski i napokon sam naučio padeže.
items.en: I studied Croatian for a long time and finally learned the cases.
items.note: The pair in one sentence: process (učio) then achieved result (naučio).

activities.title: Tell it right: aspect in past narration
activities.intro: Choose the aspect that matches the story: imperfective for the scene, perfective for the events.

questions.prompt: Dok sam ______ pismo, nazvala me je sestra.
questions.explanation: dok = while → background action in progress → imperfective: Dok sam pisao pismo…

questions.prompt: ______ sam pismo i poslao ga.
questions.explanation: A completed event followed by another → perfective: Napisao sam pismo i poslao ga.

questions.prompt: Choose the right participle (ići/otići, mi): Svake subote smo ______ na plac.
questions.explanation: svake subote = repeated habit → imperfective: išli smo.

questions.prompt: Choose the right participle (ići/otići, mi): Jučer smo ______ na izlet.
questions.explanation: One specific completed trip → perfective: otišli smo na izlet.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means 'I cooked lunch (and it's done)'?
questions.explanation: Perfective skuhati claims the result exists: lunch is ready. Kuhao sam only says I was cooking.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I was watching a film when the message arrived.'
questions.answer: Gledao sam film kad je stigla poruka
questions.explanation: Imperfective background (gledao sam) + perfective event (je stigla) — the classic narrative frame.

questions.prompt: Ona je ______ knjigu cijelo popodne.
questions.explanation: cijelo popodne = duration → imperfective: čitala je knjigu cijelo popodne.

questions.prompt: Na kraju je ______ zadnju stranicu i zatvorila knjigu.
questions.explanation: The final completed event → perfective: pročitala je zadnju stranicu.

questions.prompt: Complete (naučiti, ja m.): Učio sam hrvatski dvije godine i napokon sam ______ padeže.
questions.explanation: napokon = finally, the achieved result → perfective: naučio sam padeže.

days.title: Output: my quality of life
days.objective: Speak for two minutes about how you live and how you'd like to live.
days.paretoFocus: Combining home vocabulary, locative phrases and past-tense aspect in one talk is exactly the integrated performance A2 demands.
drills: Do Unit 18 of A2.hr.
Prepare and record a 2-minute talk: your home, your habits, what you'd change.
Read the matching Kvaliteta života texts in Razgovarajte s nama! if you have it.
Listen back and note 3 errors to fix.

items: Re-record the weakest 30 seconds.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review aspect-in-the-past with one example.

activities.title: Scaffolding your talk: Moja kvaliteta života
activities.intro: Today you record a two-minute talk about how you live. Here is a complete skeleton — opener, home, habits, likes, complaints, plans, closer — that weaves together this week's locative phrases, dative constructions and aspect. Swap in your own details and the talk writes itself.

items.en: I'll tell you how I live.
items.note: A clean opener; ispričati = to tell (a story), perfective.

items.hr: Živim sa ženom u dvosobnom stanu u mirnom kvartu.
items.en: I live with my wife in a two-room flat in a quiet neighbourhood.
items.note: Two locative phrases in a row — yesterday's grammar at work.

items.hr: Prije sam živio u velikom gradu, a sada uživam u miru.
items.en: Before, I lived in a big city, and now I enjoy the peace.
items.note: uživati u + locative = to enjoy something.

items.hr: Svako jutro prošetam do pekare i popijem kavu na balkonu.
items.en: Every morning I take a walk to the bakery and drink my coffee on the balcony.
items.note: Perfective present for a complete little routine — very natural for habits seen as whole acts.

items.hr: Najviše mi se sviđa što je sve u blizini.
items.en: What I like most is that everything is close by.
items.note: sviđa mi se što… = I like (the fact) that…

items.en: Only the weekend noise bothers me.
items.note: vikendom = on weekends (instrumental of time).

items.hr: Imam jednu lošu naviku: predugo gledam mobitel.
items.en: I have one bad habit: I look at my phone too long.
items.note: predugo = for too long; honest confessions make great talks.

items.hr: Želim promijeniti tu naviku i svaki dan šetati u prirodi.
items.en: I want to change that habit and walk in nature every day.
items.note: promijeniti (perfective — one change) + šetati (imperfective — the new routine).

items.hr: Prošle godine sam počeo trčati i to je postala moja nova navika.
items.en: Last year I started running and it became my new habit.
items.note: početi + imperfective infinitive: počeo sam trčati.

items.hr: Ukratko, moj život je dobar i miran.
items.en: In short, my life is good and peaceful.
items.note: Ukratko = in short — the perfect closer for any talk.

activities.title: Kvaliteta života — the integrated check
activities.intro: One exercise, the whole week: listen to people describing their lives, then produce the locative phrases, dative constructions and aspect choices yourself.

questions.prompt: Listen: what bothers the speaker?
questions.explanation: Smeta mi samo buka. = Only the noise bothers me. (He loves his neighbourhood because everything is close.)
questions.audioText: Živim u malom stanu u centru grada. Najviše mi se sviđa moj kvart jer je sve blizu. Smeta mi samo buka.

questions.prompt: Listen: why did she move to Zagreb?
options: Because of the peace and quiet
questions.explanation: Tamo je bio mir, ali nije bilo posla. Sada živim u Zagrebu i imam dobar posao. = There was peace there, but no work. Now she lives in Zagreb and has a good job.
questions.audioText: Prije sam živjela u selu kod roditelja. Tamo je bio mir, ali nije bilo posla. Sada živim u Zagrebu i imam dobar posao.

questions.prompt: Listen and complete: Svako jutro ______ kavu na balkonu.
questions.explanation: Svako jutro popijem kavu na balkonu. — perfective present for a small complete routine.
questions.audioText: Svako jutro popijem kavu na balkonu.

questions.prompt: Živim u ______ . (miran kvart)
questions.explanation: Location → locative, whole phrase agrees: u mirnom kvartu.

questions.prompt: Prošle godine sam ______ trčati.
questions.explanation: One event that happened once (last year) → perfective: počeo sam trčati.

questions.prompt: Complete with the missing clitic: Najviše ___ se sviđa što je sve u blizini.
questions.explanation: sviđati se + dative: Najviše mi se sviđa… (What I like most…)

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I want to change one bad habit.'
questions.explanation: promijeniti (perfective — one change) + the whole phrase in the accusative: jednu lošu naviku.

questions.prompt: Dok sam ______ u velikom gradu, nisam imao mira.
questions.explanation: dok + ongoing background state → imperfective past: Dok sam živio u velikom gradu…

questions.prompt: Complete (buka): Smeta mi ______ vikendom.
questions.explanation: With smetati the annoying thing is the subject → nominative: Smeta mi buka vikendom.

days.title: Week 11 review + speaking
days.objective: Consolidate locative phrases and aspect in conversation.
days.paretoFocus: Aspect only becomes automatic through live use — a conversation about daily life forces dozens of aspect decisions in real time.
drills: 15-minute conversation about homes, habits and daily life.
Ask your partner to flag every aspect mistake.
Re-do the hardest exercise from Units 13-18.
Note your personal top-3 aspect confusions.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say one background+event story sentence perfectly.
Review locative phrase agreement.

activities.title: Week 11 in one place — locative phrases and aspect
activities.intro: Today you talk, so load the week's two weapons first: full locative phrases (u ovom malom stanu) and the aspect switch (kuhao vs skuhao). Read every line aloud — they are the exact sentences you'll need in the conversation.

items.hr: Živim u ovom malom stanu na drugom katu.
items.en: I live in this small flat on the second floor.
items.note: The whole phrase declines in the locative: ovaj mali stan → u ovom malom stanu.

items.hr: U svojoj sobi imam veliki radni stol.
items.en: In my own room I have a big desk.
items.note: svoj because the owner is the subject: svoja soba → u svojoj sobi.

items.hr: Radim u velikom uredu, a odmaram se u mirnom kvartu.
items.en: I work in a big office and I relax in a quiet neighbourhood.
items.note: Masculine/neuter locative adjectives end in -om: velikom, mirnom.

items.en: I was writing the email all morning.
items.note: Imperfective pisati = the process, no claim that it got finished.

items.hr: Napisao sam mail i poslao ga.
items.en: I wrote the email and sent it.
items.note: Perfective napisati = done, complete. One event, result achieved.

items.hr: Dok sam kuhao večeru, nazvala me sestra.
items.en: While I was cooking dinner, my sister called me.
items.note: The storytelling formula: imperfective background (kuhao) + perfective event (nazvala).

items.hr: Svaki dan kupujem kruh, a jučer sam kupio i vino.
items.en: Every day I buy bread, and yesterday I also bought wine.
items.note: Habit → imperfective kupovati; single completed purchase → perfective kupiti.

items.hr: Idemo u novi restoran. — Već smo u novom restoranu!
items.en: We're going to the new restaurant. — We're already in the new restaurant!
items.note: Motion = u + accusative (novi restoran); location = u + locative (novom restoranu).

items.hr: Što ti se sviđa u svom kvartu?
items.en: What do you like about your neighbourhood?
items.note: The question your partner will ask today — prepare two answers.

items.hr: Pio sam kavu i čitao novine — tipično jutro.
items.en: I was drinking coffee and reading the paper — a typical morning.
items.note: Describing how a morning looked = all imperfective.

activities.title: Week 11 mixed drill
activities.intro: A cold re-test before you speak: locative agreement across the whole phrase, motion vs location, and aspect choices in the past.

questions.prompt: Živim u ___ stanu. (this small — full agreement)
questions.explanation: Locative masculine singular: ovaj → ovom, mali → malom. The whole phrase agrees with stanu.

questions.prompt: Odmaram se u svojoj ___. (soba — put it in the locative)
questions.explanation: Feminine -a nouns take -i in the locative: soba → sobi. And svojoj already shows you the case.

questions.prompt: Jučer sam ___ pismo i poslao ga. (I finished writing it)
questions.explanation: The letter got written and sent — a completed event needs the perfective napisati.

questions.prompt: Dok sam ___ večeru, nazvala me sestra.
questions.explanation: After dok the background action is imperfective: I was in the middle of cooking when the call (perfective event) arrived.

questions.prompt: Svaki dan ___ kruh u pekari. (buy — a daily habit, ja)
questions.explanation: Habits take the imperfective: kupovati → kupujem. Kupiti would be one single purchase.

questions.prompt: Say: I live in a quiet neighbourhood.
questions.explanation: Location with u + locative: mirni kvart → u mirnom kvartu, adjective and noun both in the locative.

questions.prompt: Idemo u ___ restoran. (motion!)
questions.explanation: Motion takes u + accusative. Restoran is inanimate, so the accusative looks like the nominative: novi restoran.

questions.prompt: Kupovala je namirnice svaki dan, a jučer je ___ i vino. (kupiti — perfekt, feminine)
questions.explanation: Yesterday's single completed purchase: perfective kupiti → kupila (feminine participle).

questions.prompt: What does 'Pio sam kavu cijelo jutro.' mean?
options: I was drinking coffee all morning.
I drank one coffee this morning.
I will drink coffee in the morning.
I never drink coffee in the morning.
questions.answer: I was drinking coffee all morning.
questions.explanation: Imperfective piti + cijelo jutro = an ongoing process. One finished coffee would be popio sam kavu.

activities.title: Speaking day: our flat and our habits
activities.intro: Your weekly conversation, at the kitchen table. Act it out with your wife, then improvise with your real flat and habits — and let her flag every aspect slip, just like in the script.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Danas pričamo o našem stanu i o navikama. Spreman?
lines.en: Today we're talking about our flat and our habits. Ready?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Spreman! Volim naš stan — u ovom mirnom kvartu imamo sve.
lines.en: Ready! I love our flat — in this quiet neighbourhood we have everything.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što ti se najviše sviđa u svom kvartu?
lines.en: What do you like most about your neighbourhood?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sviđa mi se mala pekara iza ugla. Svaki dan kupujem kruh tamo.
lines.en: I like the little bakery around the corner. I buy bread there every day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A jučer? Što si radio poslije posla?
lines.en: And yesterday? What did you do after work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jučer sam kuhao večeru. Dok sam kuhao, ti si me zvala!
lines.en: Yesterday I was cooking dinner. While I was cooking, you called me!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Točno! I jesi li na kraju skuhao nešto fino?
lines.en: Right! And did you cook something nice in the end?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jesam, skuhao sam rižoto. Kuhao sam ga skoro sat vremena.
lines.en: I did, I cooked a risotto. I was cooking it for almost an hour.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Vidiš — kuhao za proces, skuhao za rezultat.
lines.en: Well done! See — kuhao for the process, skuhao for the result.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Polako razumijem. A ti? Što si radila jutros?
lines.en: I'm slowly getting it. And you? What did you do this morning?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Čitala sam knjigu na balkonu i popila dvije kave.
lines.en: I was reading a book on the balcony and drank two coffees.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U našem malom stanu balkon je najbolje mjesto.
lines.en: In our little flat the balcony is the best spot.

days.title: Aspect with future and modals
days.objective: Pick the right aspect after ću/moram/želim.
days.paretoFocus: 'Sutra ću napisati izvještaj' (finish it) vs 'Sutra ću pisati' (spend time on it) — aspect after modals and futur I is where learners most often sound off.
drills: Do Unit 19 of A2.hr.
Make 6 future sentences, three per aspect, and explain each choice.
Combine modals with the right aspect: Moram kupiti… / Volim kuhati…
Spot-fix 4 sentences with the wrong aspect.

items: Say 'Tomorrow I'll write (and finish) the email.'
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall your top-3 aspect confusions from yesterday.

activities.title: Aspect after ću, moram, volim
activities.intro: Futur I and modal verbs don't remove the aspect decision — they sharpen it. Perfective infinitive = you'll get it done; imperfective = you'll spend time on it or do it habitually. Ten patterns cover almost everything.

items.en: Tomorrow I'll write the report (and finish it).
items.note: Perfective napisati after ću = the result is promised.

items.hr: Sutra ću pisati izvještaj cijelo poslijepodne.
items.en: Tomorrow I'll be writing the report all afternoon.
items.note: Imperfective pisati = you're announcing time spent, not completion.

items.en: I have to buy bread before dinner.
items.note: One concrete purchase → perfective kupiti after morati.

items.en: I love cooking on Sundays.
items.note: voljeti describes a habit, so its infinitive is almost always imperfective.

items.en: I want to learn Croatian by summer.
items.note: naučiti (perfective) = master it. Učiti would just mean spending time studying.

items.en: I start working at eight.
items.note: Golden rule: after početi/počinjati and prestati only the IMPERFECTIVE infinitive is possible.

items.en: I'll stop drinking coffee in the evening.
items.note: prestati + imperfective piti. Also note the futur spelling: prestati → prestat ću.

items.hr: Ne mogu doći večeras, ali mogu dolaziti svaki petak.
items.en: I can't come tonight, but I can come every Friday.
items.note: One occasion → doći; a repeated arrangement → dolaziti.

items.hr: Hoćeš li pročitati ovu knjigu do petka?
items.en: Will you read this book (to the end) by Friday?
items.note: A deadline (do petka) pulls the perfective: pročitati.

items.hr: Idući tjedan učit ću svaki dan i naučit ću sve padeže.
items.en: Next week I'll study every day and I'll learn all the cases.
items.note: Both aspects in one plan: učiti = the daily work, naučiti = the promised result. Infinitives drop -i before ću: učit ću.

activities.title: Future and modals: pick the aspect
activities.intro: Every question is one decision: process or result? Say the full sentence aloud after each answer.

questions.prompt: Sutra ću ___ izvještaj i poslati ga šefu. (finish it)
questions.explanation: You're promising a finished report — perfective napisati. After ću the verb stays in the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Cijelo poslijepodne ću ___ izvještaj. (spend the afternoon on it)
questions.explanation: cijelo poslijepodne measures duration — that's the imperfective's job: pisati.

questions.prompt: Moram ___ kruh prije večere. (kupiti or kupovati? — one completed purchase)
questions.explanation: One errand with a result: perfective kupiti. Kupovati would suggest a shopping habit or process.

questions.prompt: Volim ___ nedjeljom.
questions.explanation: voljeti + a habitual activity takes the imperfective infinitive: volim kuhati.

questions.prompt: Počinjem ___ u osam.
questions.explanation: After počinjati/početi (and prestati) only an imperfective infinitive can follow: počinjem raditi.

questions.prompt: Želim ___ hrvatski do ljeta. (learn = master it, perfective)
questions.explanation: The deadline do ljeta asks for a result: naučiti. Učiti is only the ongoing studying.

questions.prompt: Say: Tomorrow I'll write (and finish) the email.
questions.explanation: ću is a clitic and stands in second position: Sutra ću napisati mail. Perfective napisati = it will get done.

questions.prompt: Prestat ću ___ kavu navečer.
questions.explanation: prestati = stop doing an activity, so it takes the imperfective: prestat ću piti.

questions.prompt: ___ ću ti se sutra. (javiti se — I'll get in touch; mind the futur spelling)
questions.explanation: When the infinitive comes before ću, -ti verbs drop the final -i: javiti → Javit ću ti se sutra.

days.title: Fifteen aspect pairs you'll actually use
days.objective: Memorise the highest-frequency aspect pairs as single vocabulary items.
days.paretoFocus: Learning pairs together (davati/dati, počinjati/početi, viđati/vidjeti) means you never have to derive them again. Unit 20 closes Module 2 — a quarter done!
drills: Do Unit 20 of A2.hr — Module 2 complete!
Drill 15 core pairs both directions (English → both verbs).
Write 5 sentence pairs showing the same action in both aspects.
Add the pairs to your Words tab queue.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recite 10 pairs cold.
Review aspect after modals with one example.

activities.title: Aspect pairs I: daily actions
activities.intro: Module 2 closes today. From now on, learn every verb as a pair — one meaning, two viewpoints. First eight: the verbs of an ordinary day. Read each pair aloud: imperfective, then perfective.

items.en: to write (be writing / finish writing)
items.note: Pišem poruku. → Napisao sam poruku.

items.en: to read (be reading / read through)
items.note: Čitam roman. → Pročitao sam ga za tri dana.

items.en: to drink (be drinking / drink up)
items.note: Pijem kavu. → Popio sam kavu i krenuo.

items.en: to eat (be eating / eat up)
items.note: Jedem polako. → Pojeo sam sve.

items.en: to buy (be buying, buy regularly / buy once)
items.note: Kupujem kruh svaki dan. → Jučer sam kupio i vino.

items.en: to watch (be watching / take a look, watch to the end)
items.note: Gledam film. → Pogledaj ovo!

items.en: to study / to learn (master)
items.note: Učim hrvatski. → Naučio sam padeže.

items.en: to pay (be paying, pay regularly / pay once)
items.note: Račune plaćam online. → Platio sam večeru.

activities.title: Aspect pairs II: giving, starting, coming and going
activities.intro: Eight more pairs — the ones that glue plans and stories together. Several change their stem completely (davati/dati, dolaziti/doći), so drill them as single vocabulary items.

items.en: to give (be giving, give regularly / give once)
items.note: Baka nam je uvijek davala kolače. → Dala mi je ključ.

items.en: to take (be taking, take regularly / take once)
items.note: Uzimam tramvaj svaki dan. → Uzeo sam taksi.

items.en: to begin (be beginning / begin)
items.note: Film počinje u osam. → Film je već počeo!

items.en: to finish (be finishing / finish)
items.note: Obično završavam posao u pet. → Danas sam završio ranije.

items.en: to see (see regularly / see, catch sight of)
items.note: Viđamo se svaki tjedan. → Jučer sam ga vidio u gradu.

items.en: to come (be coming, come regularly / arrive once)
items.note: Dolazim! → Doći ću za pet minuta.

items.en: to send (be sending, send regularly / send once)
items.note: Šaljem ti fotografije. → Poslao sam ti je jučer.

items.en: to return (be returning, return regularly / return once)
items.note: Vraćam se kući oko šest. → Vratio sam se kasno sinoć.

activities.title: Your sixteen pairs under fire
activities.intro: Both directions: from one member of the pair to the other, and from English straight to the right verb in the right form.

questions.prompt: The perfective partner of davati is:
questions.explanation: davati/dati — one of the pairs where the perfective is the shorter verb.

questions.prompt: The imperfective partner of početi is:
questions.explanation: počinjati/početi = to begin. Film počinje u osam (schedule) vs Film je počeo (it has started).

questions.prompt: Baka mi je često ___ kolače. (davati — perfekt, a repeated habit, feminine)
questions.explanation: A habit in the past stays imperfective: davati → davala. Dala would be one single occasion.

questions.prompt: Film ___ u osam, požuri!
questions.explanation: Timetables and scheduled starts use the imperfective present: film počinje u osam.

questions.prompt: Jučer sam ___ starog prijatelja u gradu. (vidjeti — perfekt, masculine)
questions.explanation: One concrete sighting: perfective vidjeti → vidio sam. Viđao sam would mean I used to see him regularly.

questions.prompt: Rijetko se ___ otkad je odselio. (we rarely see each other)
questions.explanation: Regular (here: rare but repeated) contact uses viđati se: rijetko se viđamo.

questions.prompt: Say: I read the book (to the end) and returned it to Ana.
questions.answer: Pročitao sam knjigu i vratio je Ani
questions.explanation: Two completed events, both perfective: pročitati and vratiti. In the second clause je is the accusative clitic (= the book).

questions.prompt: Svaki dan ___ vodu, a danas sam popio i sok.
questions.explanation: The daily habit takes the imperfective present pijem; the one finished juice takes perfective popio.

questions.prompt: Obično ___ posao u pet. (završavati or završiti? — present, a routine, ja)
questions.explanation: obično = routine → imperfective završavati: obično završavam posao u pet.

days.title: Planovi, želje, mogućnosti
days.objective: Talk about plans, wishes and possibilities with verb + infinitive patterns.
days.paretoFocus: planirati/željeti/namjeravati/moći + infinitive is a tiny pattern with huge range — everything you intend, hope or can do fits in it.
drills: Do Unit 21 of A2.hr.
Learn: planirati, namjeravati, željeti, nadati se, uspjeti.
State 3 plans, 3 wishes and 3 possibilities for this year.
Mind the aspect of each infinitive you choose.

items: Say one plan, one wish, one possibility.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review 5 aspect pairs.

activities.title: Planovi, želje, mogućnosti — verb + infinitive
activities.intro: One small pattern, huge range: planirati, namjeravati, željeti, uspjeti, pokušavati + infinitive covers everything you intend, hope or manage to do. Watch the aspect of every infinitive you attach.

items.en: I plan to learn Croatian this year.
items.note: planirati + infinitive; the goal is a result, so perfective naučiti.

items.en: I intend to sign up for a course.
items.note: namjeravati = to intend. The clitic se sits in second position: Namjeravam se prijaviti.

items.en: I want to visit Dubrovnik in September.
items.note: željeti + infinitive — the polite cousin of htjeti.

items.hr: Nadam se da ćemo ljetovati na moru.
items.en: I hope we'll spend the summer at the seaside.
items.note: nadati se takes da + a clause, not an infinitive: nadam se da ću/ćemo…

items.hr: Uspio sam rezervirati stol za subotu.
items.en: I managed to reserve a table for Saturday.
items.note: uspjeti + infinitive = to manage to; the achieved result invites a perfective.

items.en: I'm trying to work less at the weekend.
items.note: pokušavati (impf.) = ongoing effort; pokušati (pf.) = one attempt.

items.hr: Mogu ti pomoći sutra poslije posla.
items.en: I can help you tomorrow after work.
items.note: moći + infinitive = possibility; ti is a dative clitic in second position.

items.en: We have to plan the trip in advance.
items.note: isplanirati = perfective of planirati: plan it fully, once.

items.hr: Jednog dana želimo kupiti kuću uz more.
items.en: One day we want to buy a house by the sea.
items.note: jednog dana = temporal genitive; the dream purchase is perfective kupiti.

items.en: I don't plan to change anything.
items.note: Double negation is obligatory: ne + ništa. Open-ended changing → imperfective mijenjati.

activities.title: Plans and wishes drill
activities.intro: Conjugate the plan verbs, pick the right infinitive aspect, and keep se and da where they belong.

questions.prompt: ___ ove godine naučiti hrvatski. (I plan)
questions.explanation: planirati is a regular -am verb: planiram, planiraš, planira…

questions.prompt: Nadam se ___ ćemo ljetovati na moru. (the little connecting word)
questions.explanation: nadati se takes a da-clause: Nadam se da ćemo… — not an infinitive.

questions.prompt: Uspio sam ___ karte za koncert. (managed to buy them)
questions.explanation: uspjeti reports an achieved result, so the perfective infinitive follows: uspio sam kupiti.

questions.prompt: Say: I intend to sign up for the course. (mind where se goes)
questions.explanation: The clitic se takes second position in the clause, right after Namjeravam — not after the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Pokušavam ___ manje. (work — an ongoing effort)
questions.explanation: After a modal-like verb the infinitive follows, and an ongoing activity is imperfective: pokušavam raditi manje.

questions.prompt: Jednog dana želimo ___ kuću uz more. (buy — perfective)
questions.explanation: One dream purchase, complete: kupiti. Kupovati kuću would mean being in the process of buying it.

questions.prompt: What does 'Namjeravam' mean?
questions.explanation: namjeravati = to intend; nadati se = to hope; uspjeti = to manage.

questions.prompt: Nadamo se da ___ sve biti u redu.
questions.explanation: The subject of the da-clause is sve (it all), 3rd person singular: da će sve biti u redu.

days.title: The conditional: bih, bi, bismo
days.objective: Form kondicional I with and without the pronoun.
days.paretoFocus: The conditional is how Croatian does politeness, wishes and hypotheticals — three conversational superpowers from one small paradigm.
drills: Do Unit 22 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · the conditional (kondicional prvi).
Learn bih, bi, bi, bismo, biste, bi + participle: Rado bih putovao.
Transform 6 present sentences into conditional wishes.

items: Conjugate the conditional of ići cold.
Say 'I would gladly travel.'
Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Kondicional I: bih, bi, bi, bismo, biste, bi
activities.intro: Six little words plus the participle you already know from the perfekt — and suddenly you can wish, suggest and speculate. In careful standard Croatian mi = bismo and vi = biste; colloquial speech levels everything to bi, but you should learn the full paradigm.

items.en: I would gladly travel.
items.note: The formula: bih/bi/bismo… + past participle. bih is a clitic in second position.

items.hr: Ja bih došao, ali ne mogu.
items.en: I would come, but I can't.
items.note: With the pronoun: Ja bih došao. Without it: Došao bih.

items.en: You would surely know that. (to a woman)
items.note: 2nd person singular: bi. The participle agrees with the person spoken to: znala.

items.hr: On bi došao ranije, ali radi do pet.
items.en: He would come earlier, but he works until five.
items.note: 3rd person singular: bi.

items.en: We would go to the seaside.
items.note: 1st person plural: bismo — the form learners forget first. Drill it.

items.en: You would love this film.
items.note: 2nd person plural (and polite Vi): biste.

items.en: They would stay longer.
items.note: 3rd person plural: bi again — three persons share the form bi.

items.en: I would like a glass of water.
items.note: Htio bih… = the polite way to want something. Compare blunt Hoću…

items.hr: Kupio bih taj stan da imam novca.
items.en: I would buy that flat if I had the money.
items.note: The conditional also builds hypotheticals — tomorrow's topic in full.

items.en: Tomorrow I would gladly sleep longer.
items.note: Second position rule: Sutra counts as first, so bih comes right after it.

activities.title: Conditional forms drill
activities.intro: The paradigm, cold: bih, bi, bi, bismo, biste, bi — and the clitic in second position every single time.

questions.prompt: Mi ___ išli na more. (conditional auxiliary)
questions.explanation: 1st person plural: bismo. Colloquial speech says mi bi, but the standard form is bismo.

questions.prompt: Vi ___ voljeli ovaj film. (conditional auxiliary)
questions.explanation: 2nd person plural and polite Vi: biste.

questions.prompt: Ja ___ došao, ali ne mogu.
questions.explanation: 1st person singular: bih — ja bih došao.

questions.prompt: Say: I would gladly travel.
questions.explanation: bih is a clitic: Rado takes first position, bih must come second, the participle follows.

questions.prompt: Oni ___ ostali dulje.
questions.explanation: 3rd person plural is bi — the same form as ti, on and ona.

questions.prompt: Htio ___ čašu vode, molim. (I would like)
questions.explanation: Htio bih… = I would like… — your new default for polite wanting.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the clitic in the right place?
options: Sutra rado bih spavao dulje bih.
questions.explanation: bih can never start a sentence and must sit in second position: Sutra | bih | rado spavao dulje.

questions.prompt: Kupio ___ taj stan da imam novca. (ja)
questions.explanation: Kupio bih = I would buy. The participle can take first position, with bih right behind it.

questions.prompt: In careful standard Croatian, 'we would' is:
questions.explanation: Standard: mi bismo, vi biste. You'll hear mi bi everywhere in speech, but write and learn bismo.

days.title: Polite Croatian: Biste li…?
days.objective: Use negative and question forms of the conditional for polite requests.
days.paretoFocus: 'Biste li mogli…?' and 'Ne bih htio smetati…' are the politeness formulas of adult Croatian — instantly upgrading how you sound in shops, offices and emails.
drills: Do Unit 23 of A2.hr.
Form questions: Biste li mi pomogli? Bi li došla?
Form negatives: Ne bih rekao. Ne bismo mogli.
Role-play 3 polite requests: at the post office, to a neighbour, in an email.

items: Make a polite request from memory.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall where the clitic 'bih' sits in the sentence.

activities.title: Polite Croatian: Biste li…? Ne bih…
activities.intro: The question and negative forms of the conditional are the politeness engine of adult Croatian. Ten formulas that instantly upgrade how you sound at the counter, on the phone and in email.

items.en: Could you help me? (formal)
items.note: The politest possible request: Biste li + mogli + infinitive.

items.hr: Biste li htjeli kavu ili čaj?
items.en: Would you like coffee or tea? (formal)
items.note: Inversion with li: auxiliary first, then li.

items.hr: Bi li došla s nama u kino?
items.en: Would you come to the cinema with us? (to a woman, informal)
items.note: Informal singular: Bi li + participle agreeing with the person asked.

items.en: I wouldn't say so.
items.note: Soft disagreement — much gentler than Ne! or Nije točno.

items.hr: Ne bismo mogli doći u subotu.
items.en: We wouldn't be able to come on Saturday.
items.note: Negative: ne directly before the auxiliary — ne bih, ne bi, ne bismo.

items.en: I wouldn't want to disturb you.
items.note: The classic polite opener before asking for something.

items.en: Excuse me, would you repeat that?
items.note: Your conversation-repair phrase, upgraded to conditional politeness.

items.hr: Htio bih poslati ovaj paket u Njemačku.
items.en: I would like to send this parcel to Germany.
items.note: State your business politely: Htio bih + perfective infinitive.

items.hr: Biste li mi rekli gdje je šalter tri?
items.en: Would you tell me where counter three is?
items.note: The dative clitic mi slips in right after li: Biste li mi…

items.hr: Možete li mi pomoći? — Biste li mi mogli pomoći?
items.en: Can you help me? — Could you (possibly) help me?
items.note: Both are fine; the conditional version is a degree warmer and more formal.

activities.title: Politeness drill
activities.intro: Build questions with Biste li, negatives with ne bih, and keep the clitics in line: li first, then bih/biste, then mi/ti.

questions.prompt: Ask politely: Could you help me?
questions.explanation: Clitic chain: auxiliary biste + li, then the dative clitic mi, then the participle and infinitive.

questions.prompt: Ne ___ htio smetati. (I wouldn't want to disturb)
questions.explanation: The negative conditional: ne + bih + participle — ne bih htio.

questions.prompt: ___ li došla s nama u kino? (asking your friend Ana)
questions.explanation: Informal singular takes bi: Bi li došla? Biste is for vi — plural or polite.

questions.prompt: Which request is the most polite?
questions.explanation: Conditional + vi-form + moći = maximum politeness. The imperative Pomozi mi! is for family and emergencies.

questions.prompt: Ne ___ mogli doći u subotu. (we)
questions.explanation: Negative 1st person plural: ne bismo mogli — a polite way to decline.

questions.prompt: Htio ___ poslati ovaj paket u Njemačku.
questions.explanation: You're speaking about yourself: htio bih = I would like.

questions.prompt: What does 'Ne bih rekao.' mean?
questions.explanation: Conditional ne bih rekao = polite, softened disagreement — very common in conversation.

questions.prompt: Biste li ___ rekli gdje je šalter tri? (to me — dative clitic)
questions.explanation: The dative clitic mi follows li in the clitic chain: Biste li mi rekli…

activities.title: Role-play: at the post office
activities.intro: Your wife plays the post-office clerk — a favourite living-room game. Every request goes through the conditional. Act it out, then swap: you play the clerk.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Idemo vježbati! Ja sam službenica na pošti. Dobar dan, izvolite?
lines.en: Let's practise! I'm the clerk at the post office. Good afternoon, how can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan. Htio bih poslati ovaj paket u Ameriku.
lines.en: Good afternoon. I would like to send this parcel to America.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Naravno. Biste li ga stavili na vagu, molim Vas?
lines.en: Of course. Would you put it on the scale, please?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Evo, izvolite. Biste li mi rekli koliko košta slanje?
lines.en: Here you are. Would you tell me how much shipping costs?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Obično slanje košta dvanaest eura, a brzo dvadeset pet.
lines.en: Regular shipping costs twelve euros, and express twenty-five.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne bih htio platiti toliko. Može obično, molim.
lines.en: I wouldn't want to pay that much. Regular is fine, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: U redu. Biste li ispunili ovaj obrazac?
lines.en: All right. Would you fill in this form?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Naravno. Oprostite, biste li mi posudili kemijsku?
lines.en: Of course. Excuse me, would you lend me a pen?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Izvolite. Trebate li još nešto?
lines.en: Here you are. Do you need anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne, hvala Vam lijepa. Doviđenja!
lines.en: No, thank you very much. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Doviđenja! … I bravo — zvučao si vrlo pristojno!
lines.en: Goodbye! … And bravo — you sounded very polite!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala! Kondicional stvarno pomaže.
lines.en: Thanks! The conditional really helps.

days.title: Wishes and unreal conditions
days.objective: Express wishes, possibilities and unreal conditions with kad + conditional.
days.paretoFocus: 'Kad bih imao više vremena, putovao bih.' The four meanings of the conditional (wish, possibility, condition, impossible condition) cover most of what 'would' does in English.
drills: Do Unit 24 of A2.hr.
Build 4 sentences, one per conditional meaning: želja, mogućnost, uvjet, nemogući uvjet.
Answer: Što bi radio da ne moraš raditi?
Translate 4 English 'would' sentences into Croatian.

items: Say a 'kad bih… , … bih' sentence cold.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review polite Biste li…? requests.

activities.title: The four jobs of the conditional
activities.intro: One paradigm, four meanings: wish (želja), possibility (mogućnost), condition (uvjet) and impossible condition (nemogući uvjet). Together they cover most of what English 'would' does.

items.en: I would like more free time.
items.note: Job 1 — wish (želja). Htio bih / htjela bih + what you want.

items.hr: Rado bih otputovao na neko toplo mjesto.
items.en: I would gladly go off somewhere warm.
items.note: rado + conditional = a warm, dreamy wish.

items.en: That could be true.
items.note: Job 2 — possibility (mogućnost). moglo bi biti = it might be.

items.hr: Ana bi mogla stići oko osam.
items.en: Ana could arrive around eight.
items.note: bi + mogla + infinitive: speculating about what's possible.

items.hr: Kad bih imao više vremena, putovao bih.
items.en: If I had more time, I would travel.
items.note: Job 3 — condition (uvjet): kad + conditional in BOTH halves.

items.hr: Kad bismo živjeli uz more, plivali bismo svaki dan.
items.en: If we lived by the sea, we would swim every day.
items.note: Same pattern in the plural: kad bismo…, … bismo.

items.hr: Kad ne bih morao raditi, spavao bih do deset.
items.en: If I didn't have to work, I would sleep until ten.
items.note: Negative condition: kad ne bih + participle.

items.hr: Što bi radio da ne moraš raditi?
items.en: What would you do if you didn't have to work?
items.note: da + present is the everyday alternative to kad + conditional in the if-half.

items.hr: Kad bih dobio slobodnu godinu, proputovao bih Europu.
items.en: If I got a year off, I would travel all over Europe.
items.note: Job 4 — an unlikely or impossible condition uses exactly the same form. Context decides.

items.hr: Bi li mi pomogla oko ovoga?
items.en: Would you help me with this? (to a woman)
items.note: And the bonus job you already know: politeness.

activities.title: What would you do? — hypotheticals drill
activities.intro: Build kad bih sentences, spot the four meanings, and translate English 'would' without flinching.

questions.prompt: Rado ___ putovao svijetom. (ja — kondicional)
questions.explanation: First-person conditional auxiliary is bih: Rado bih putovao. (In a full if-clause it repeats: Kad bih imao vremena, putovao bih.)

questions.prompt: Ask: What would you do if you didn't have to work? (to a man)
questions.answer: Što bi radio da ne moraš raditi
questions.explanation: Main clause in the conditional (Što bi radio), the if-half with da + present (da ne moraš raditi).

questions.prompt: Kad bismo živjeli uz more, ___ bismo svaki dan.
questions.explanation: The conditional needs the past participle: plivali bismo = we would swim.

questions.prompt: What does 'To bi moglo biti točno.' mean?
questions.explanation: bi moglo biti = could be / might be — the possibility job of the conditional.

questions.prompt: Rado ___ živjeli uz more. (mi — kondicional)
questions.explanation: First-person plural conditional is bismo: Rado bismo živjeli. (In an if-clause both halves take it: kad bismo mogli…, živjeli bismo.)

questions.prompt: Which sentence expresses a WISH (želja)?
options: Kad bih imao vremena, putovao bih.
questions.explanation: Htjela bih… states a wish. The others are possibility, condition and politeness — the other three jobs.

questions.prompt: Rado ___ otputovao na neko toplo mjesto. (ja)
questions.explanation: Rado bih otputovao — rado takes first position, the clitic bih second.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'I would sleep until ten.'
questions.explanation: English 'would' for a hypothetical = the Croatian conditional: spavao bih.

questions.prompt: Ana ___ mogla stići oko osam.
questions.explanation: 3rd person singular bi: Ana bi mogla stići — speculating about a possibility.

days.title: Week 12 review + speaking: dreams & plans
days.objective: Hold a conversation about plans and dreams driven by the conditional.
days.paretoFocus: Talking about what you would do is the most natural possible drill for this week's grammar — and among the most engaging conversations to have.
drills: 15-minute conversation: plans, wishes and what-ifs (tutor or conversation partner).
Use at least 5 conditionals and 3 different aspect pairs.
Re-do the hardest exercise from Units 19-24.
Note recurring errors for next week's focus.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say your best 'kad bih…' sentence.
Recite the conditional paradigm once.

activities.title: Week 12 in one place — your dreams-and-plans toolkit
activities.intro: Before today's conversation, one pass through the whole week: aspect after ću and modals, the plan verbs, and every job of the conditional. These are the exact lines you'll want mid-conversation.

items.en: Tomorrow I'll write the whole report (and finish it).
items.note: Perfective after ću = promised result.

items.hr: Volim kuhati, ali danas moram skuhati večeru za goste.
items.en: I love cooking, but today I have to cook dinner for guests.
items.note: Habit imperfective, tonight's concrete task perfective — both in one breath.

items.en: I plan to finish the course by summer.
items.note: Plan verb + perfective infinitive with a deadline.

items.hr: Nadam se da ćemo ljetos ići u Hrvatsku.
items.en: I hope we'll go to Croatia this summer.
items.note: nadati se + da-clause; ljetos = this summer.

items.hr: Kad ne bih morao raditi, putovao bih po cijelom svijetu.
items.en: If I didn't have to work, I would travel all over the world.
items.note: The kad bih…, … bih frame — today's conversation lives here.

items.hr: Kad bismo imali više novca, kupili bismo kuću uz more.
items.en: If we had more money, we would buy a house by the sea.
items.note: Plural hypothetical: bismo in both halves.

items.hr: Što bi radio da ne moraš raditi?
items.en: What would you do if you didn't have to work?
items.note: The question to ask your partner today — and to answer yourself.

items.hr: Kome bi prvo javio dobre vijesti?
items.en: Who would you tell the good news first?
items.note: javiti komu što — the dative asks 'to whom'. Full dative phrases arrive tomorrow.

items.en: That would be wonderful!
items.note: The all-purpose enthusiastic reply to any dream or plan.

activities.title: Week 12 mixed drill
activities.intro: Aspect, plan verbs and conditionals in one cold re-test. Aim for a clean sweep before you start dreaming out loud.

questions.prompt: Sutra ću ___ cijeli izvještaj. (finish it)
questions.explanation: A promised finished report takes the perfective infinitive after ću: napisati.

questions.prompt: Mi ___ rado došli. (conditional — we would gladly come)
questions.explanation: Standard 1st person plural: bismo — mi bismo rado došli.

questions.prompt: The perfective partner of počinjati is:
questions.explanation: počinjati/početi — Module 2's pair for beginning. Don't confuse it with počistiti (to clean up).

questions.prompt: Ask: Who would you tell the good news first? (to a man)
questions.answer: Kome bi prvo javio dobre vijesti
questions.explanation: Kome takes first position, the clitic bi second, then the participle: Kome bi prvo javio…

questions.prompt: Uspio sam ___ stol za subotu. (reserve)
questions.explanation: uspjeti + infinitive: uspio sam rezervirati = I managed to reserve.

questions.prompt: Kad bih dobio slobodnu godinu, ___ bih Europu.
questions.explanation: The conditional needs the participle: proputovao bih = I would travel all over.

questions.prompt: Volim ___, ali danas moram skuhati večeru za goste.
questions.explanation: voljeti + habit = imperfective kuhati; the one concrete dinner tonight is skuhati.

questions.prompt: Vi ___ voljeli ovaj plan. (careful standard form)
questions.explanation: vi = biste in standard Croatian: Vi biste voljeli ovaj plan.

activities.title: Dreams and what-ifs
activities.intro: The week's grammar becomes a real conversation: what you would do, where you would go, what you actually plan. Act it out, then continue past the script with your own dreams.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Danas pričamo o snovima. Što bi radio kad ne bi morao raditi?
lines.en: Today we're talking about dreams. What would you do if you didn't have to work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad ne bih morao raditi, putovao bih po cijelom svijetu.
lines.en: If I didn't have to work, I would travel all over the world.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kamo bi prvo otputovao?
lines.en: Where would you go first?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Prvo bih otputovao u Japan. Uvijek sam želio vidjeti Tokio.
lines.en: First I would go to Japan. I've always wanted to see Tokyo.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A što bismo napravili kad bismo imali više novca?
lines.en: And what would we do if we had more money?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad bismo imali više novca, kupili bismo kuću uz more.
lines.en: If we had more money, we would buy a house by the sea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: To bi bilo divno! Ja bih plivala svaki dan.
lines.en: That would be wonderful! I would swim every day.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A ja bih naučio roniti. I lovio bih ribu!
lines.en: And I would learn to dive. And I would go fishing!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro, a sada ozbiljno — što planiraš sljedeće godine?
lines.en: OK, and now seriously — what are you planning next year?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Planiram završiti tečaj i želim položiti ispit A2.
lines.en: I plan to finish the course and I want to pass the A2 exam.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Uspjet ćeš! A ja se nadam da ćemo ljetos ići u Hrvatsku.
lines.en: You'll manage it! And I hope we'll go to Croatia this summer.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad bismo išli u rujnu, bilo bi jeftinije.
lines.en: If we went in September, it would be cheaper.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vidiš? Kondicional ti već ide odlično.
lines.en: See? Your conditional is already excellent.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala! Sad bih rado popio kavu — zaslužio sam je.
lines.en: Thanks! Now I would gladly drink a coffee — I've earned it.

days.title: Dative across the whole phrase
days.objective: Decline adjectives, demonstratives and possessives in the dative.
days.paretoFocus: 'Pomažem svojoj mlađoj sestri' — full dative phrases complete the recipient side of your sentences and mirror the locative forms you already know.
drills: Do Unit 25 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · adjective declension (dative).
Decline 6 phrases: moja dobra prijateljica → mojoj dobroj prijateljici.
Write 5 giving/helping/writing-to sentences with full phrases.

items: Say 'to my younger sister' with full agreement.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Note: dative and locative adjective endings match.

activities.title: Dative across the whole phrase
activities.intro: The recipient side of your sentences gets its full endings: masculine/neuter adjectives take -om(u/e), feminine -oj — exactly the endings you already know from the locative. Same forms, new job.

items.hr: moja dobra prijateljica → Pišem mojoj dobroj prijateljici.
items.en: my good friend (f.) → I'm writing to my good friend.
items.note: Feminine dative: -oj on adjective and possessive, -i on the noun.

items.hr: taj stari čovjek → Dajem tom starom čovjeku novine.
items.en: that old man → I give that old man the newspaper.
items.note: Masculine dative: taj → tom, stari → starom, čovjek → čovjeku.

items.en: I'm helping my (own) younger sister.
items.note: svoj declines too: svojoj mlađoj sestri. Comparatives take normal adjective endings.

items.hr: Pišem svom mlađem bratu svaki mjesec.
items.en: I write to my younger brother every month.
items.note: Soft-stem comparative mlađi takes -em, not -om: mlađem bratu.

items.en: I'm giving that small child an apple.
items.note: Neuter dijete extends its stem: dijete → djetetu.

items.en: I'm sending a message to our dear grandma.
items.note: baka keeps its k in the dative: baki (kinship words resist the k→c change).

items.en: I bought a present for her mother.
items.note: But majka DOES change: majka → majci (k → c before -i).

items.en: I trust my best friend.
items.note: Superlatives decline like any adjective: najbolji → najboljem (soft stem, -em).

items.hr: u tom velikom gradu — tom velikom gradu
items.en: in that big city (locative) — to that big city (dative)
items.note: For adjectives, demonstratives and possessives, dative and locative endings are identical.

items.hr: Čestitao sam svojoj dragoj ženi na novom poslu.
items.en: I congratulated my dear wife on her new job.
items.note: čestitati komu na čemu — dative person + na + locative thing.

activities.title: Full dative phrases drill
activities.intro: Decline whole phrases, not just nouns: -om/-em for masculine and neuter, -oj for feminine, and watch the consonant changes in majci.

questions.prompt: Pomažem ___ mlađoj sestri. (one's own — the owner is the subject)
questions.explanation: Subject = owner → svoj, in the feminine dative: svojoj mlađoj sestri.

questions.prompt: moja dobra prijateljica → Pišem mojoj dobroj ___.
questions.explanation: Feminine -a nouns take -i in the dative: prijateljica → prijateljici.

questions.prompt: Dajem ___ starom čovjeku novine. (that)
questions.explanation: Masculine dative singular of taj is tom(u): tom starom čovjeku.

questions.prompt: Pišem ___ bratu. (to my own younger brother — soft stem!)
questions.explanation: After soft consonants like đ the dative takes -em: mlađem. So: svom mlađem bratu.

questions.prompt: Šaljem poruku našoj dragoj ___. (baka)
questions.explanation: baka → baki: kinship and pet-name nouns keep their k in the dative.

questions.prompt: Kupio sam poklon njezinoj ___. (majka — watch the consonant!)
questions.explanation: majka → majci: k changes to c before the dative -i. Compare baka → baki, which resists the change.

questions.prompt: 'To my good friend (a woman)' is:
questions.explanation: The dative of the whole phrase: mojoj (-oj) dobroj (-oj) prijateljici (-i).

questions.prompt: Say: I trust my best friend.
questions.explanation: vjerovati takes the dative; the whole phrase declines: svom najboljem prijatelju.

questions.prompt: Dajem ___ malom djetetu jabuku.
questions.explanation: dijete is neuter; neuter dative matches the masculine: tom malom djetetu.

days.title: Dative verbs & svoj in the dative
days.objective: Use the common dative-governing verbs with correct phrases.
days.paretoFocus: A short verb list (pomoći, javiti se, vjerovati, sviđati se, čestitati) triggers the dative constantly — knowing the list means never guessing the case.
drills: Do Unit 26 of A2.hr.
Learn 8 dative verbs and make one sentence with each.
Practise sviđati se with clitics: Sviđa mi se tvoj stan.
Use svoj in the dative: Javio sam se svojoj obitelji.

items: List 6 dative verbs from memory.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'I like your flat' the Croatian way (dative + sviđati se).

activities.title: Verbs that demand the dative
activities.intro: A short list of verbs triggers the dative every time — know the list and you never guess the case again. The stars are sviđati se (Croatian 'like') and nedostajati ('miss'), which flip the English sentence around.

items.en: I'm helping my sister with the move.
items.note: pomagati/pomoći + dative: pomažem komu.

items.hr: Javio sam se svojoj obitelji čim sam stigao.
items.en: I got in touch with my family as soon as I arrived.
items.note: javiti se + dative — and svoj because the family is the subject's own.

items.en: I trust you.
items.note: vjerovati + dative: vjerujem ti, ne vjerujem mu.

items.en: I like your flat.
items.note: The Croatian 'like': the liked thing is the SUBJECT, the liker is in the dative — literally 'your flat pleases me'.

items.en: I like these photos.
items.note: Plural subject → plural verb: sviđaju. The dative clitic mi doesn't change.

items.hr: Čestitao sam bratu na novom poslu.
items.en: I congratulated my brother on his new job.
items.note: čestitati komu na čemu.

items.en: I thanked my neighbour for his help.
items.note: zahvaliti komu na čemu — same skeleton as čestitati.

items.en: Does the music bother you?
items.note: smetati + dative: smeta mi, smeta li ti…

items.en: I miss you.
items.note: Flipped like sviđati se: the missed person is the subject — 'you are lacking to me'.

items.hr: Odgovara li ti termin u pet?
items.en: Does five o'clock suit you?
items.note: odgovarati + dative = to suit. Your invitation vocabulary from week 10, now explained.

items.en: This coat belongs to my wife.
items.note: pripadati + dative — full phrase: mojoj ženi.

activities.title: Dative verbs drill
activities.intro: The flipped verbs get most of the attention: sviđati se agreement, nedostajati direction, and the dative clitics mi, ti, mu, joj in second position.

questions.prompt: Say it the Croatian way: I like your flat.
questions.explanation: The flat is the subject, you are in the dative. Clitic order: dative mi before se.

questions.prompt: ___ mi se ove fotografije. (plural agreement!)
questions.explanation: The subject is fotografije (plural), so the verb is plural: sviđaju mi se.

questions.prompt: Javio sam se ___ obitelji. (one's own)
questions.explanation: javiti se + dative, and the subject owns the family: svojoj obitelji.

questions.prompt: Čestitao sam ___ na novom poslu.
questions.explanation: čestitati komu: the congratulated person goes in the dative — bratu.

questions.prompt: Nedostaješ ___. (I miss you — the dative clitic)
questions.explanation: Flipped construction: YOU are the subject (nedostaješ), I am the dative — nedostaješ mi.

questions.prompt: What does 'Nedostaješ mi.' mean?
questions.explanation: The classic trap: the missed person is the subject. Nedostaješ mi = literally 'you are lacking to me' = I miss you.

questions.prompt: Vjerujem ___. (I trust you — informal singular clitic)
questions.explanation: vjerovati + dative: vjerujem ti. For 'him': vjerujem mu.

questions.prompt: Smeta li ___ glazba? (you — formal)
questions.explanation: smetati + dative; the clitic vam follows li: Smeta li vam glazba?

questions.prompt: What does 'Odgovara li ti termin u pet?' mean?
options: Do you have an appointment at five?
questions.explanation: odgovarati + dative = to suit someone. Odgovarati na pitanje = to answer — same verb, different construction.

days.title: Što bi bilo kad bi… — hypothetical day
days.objective: Sustain hypothetical talk combining conditional, dative and aspect.
days.paretoFocus: Free hypothetical conversation is where the conditional stops being a paradigm and becomes an instinct.
drills: Do Unit 27 of A2.hr.
Answer aloud: Što bi radio kad bi dobio slobodnu godinu? Kome bi prvo javio dobre vijesti?
Write 5 chained hypotheticals (Kad bih…, … bih, a onda bih…).
Open the Grammar tab → A2 · the conditional; read the examples aloud.

items: Deliver one 30-second hypothetical monologue.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review dative verbs.

activities.title: Kad bih… — the hypothetical engine
activities.intro: Today the conditional stops being a table and becomes conversation. The pattern: kad + conditional in the condition, conditional again in the result — and you chain results with 'a onda bih…' (and then I would…). Say every line aloud; the goal is that 'bih' comes out before you think about it.

items.hr: Kad bih imao vremena, učio bih hrvatski svaki dan.
items.en: If I had time, I would study Croatian every day.
items.note: Both halves take the conditional: kad bih imao…, učio bih.

items.hr: Kad bih dobio slobodnu godinu, otputovao bih u Dalmaciju.
items.en: If I got a year off, I would travel to Dalmatia.
items.note: dobio → otputovao: perfective participles for one-off events.

items.en: …and then I would learn to surf.
items.note: Chain hypotheticals with a onda bih… — this is how you sustain a monologue.

items.hr: Kad bih dobio dobre vijesti, prvo bih javio ženi.
items.en: If I got good news, I would tell my wife first.
items.note: javiti komu — dative! One of your dative-rection verbs.

items.hr: Kome bi prvo javio dobre vijesti?
items.en: Who would you tell the good news first?
items.note: kome = dative of tko. The answer needs a dative too.

items.hr: Što bi radio kad bi imao milijun eura?
items.en: What would you do if you had a million euros?
items.note: bi for ti — the full paradigm: bih, bi, bi, bismo, biste, bi.

items.hr: Kupio bih stan blizu mora i pomogao bih roditeljima.
items.en: I would buy a flat near the sea and help my parents.
items.note: pomoći komu — dative again: pomogao bih roditeljima.

items.hr: Kad bismo imali više vremena, putovali bismo češće.
items.en: If we had more time, we would travel more often.
items.note: bismo — the careful standard form for 'we'; colloquial speech flattens everything to bi.

items.hr: Pisao bih svaki dan. / Napisao bih knjigu.
items.en: I would write every day. / I would write a book (and finish it).
items.note: Aspect lives on in the conditional: imperfective for habit, perfective for one completed act.

items.en: I wouldn't change anything.
items.note: ne + bih; ništa stays with the negated verb — Croatian requires the double negative.

items.hr: Bi li i ti otišao sa mnom?
items.en: Would you leave with me too?
items.note: Question: bi + li, then the participle. (sa mnom = with me.)

items.hr: Javio bih ti se čim bih stigao.
items.en: I would get in touch with you as soon as I arrived.
items.note: Clitic chain in action: bih (aux) → ti (dative) → se.

activities.title: Hypothetical workout
activities.intro: Auxiliaries, datives, aspect and clitic order — everything a free hypothetical needs, drilled in isolation and then combined.

questions.prompt: Kad ___ imao vremena, putovao bih. (ja)
questions.explanation: First person singular takes bih: kad bih imao. Colloquial speech says bi, but the standard — and this course — keeps bih.

questions.prompt: The conditional auxiliary for mi (we) is:
questions.explanation: mi bismo, vi biste — the two forms that mark careful speech. bihmo doesn't exist; biste is for vi.

questions.prompt: Kome bi prvo javio dobre vijesti? — ___
options: Javio bih ih od svoje žene.
questions.explanation: javiti komu takes the dative: svojoj ženi. And it's svoj because the subject (I) is the possessor.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Kad bih imao milijun eura, ___ bih stan.' (buy — one completed purchase)
questions.explanation: One completed act → perfective kupiti → participle kupio. Kupovao bih would mean 'I would keep buying'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the right auxiliary: 'Mi ___ putovali češće.'
questions.explanation: mi → bismo: Mi bismo putovali češće. This is exactly the form colloquial speech loses — using it correctly is an instant level-up.

questions.prompt: 'I would write to you every day' — which aspect?
questions.explanation: svaki dan = a repeated habit → imperfective pisati → pisao bih. Napisao bih is for one finished piece of writing.

questions.prompt: Build the chained hypothetical: 'If I got a year off, I would travel to Dalmatia.'
questions.answer: Kad bih dobio slobodnu godinu, otputovao bih u Dalmaciju.
questions.explanation: Both clauses carry the conditional: Kad bih dobio…, otputovao bih… In the second clause bih sits in second position, right after the participle.

questions.prompt: Which clitic order is correct for 'I would get in touch with you tomorrow'?
questions.explanation: The chain: auxiliary (bih) → dative (ti) → se. Javio bih ti se — the same template as with sam/ću: aux before dative, se last.

activities.title: Što bi bilo kad bi… — dream out loud
activities.intro: A whole conversation held together by the conditional. Read your lines aloud, then swap roles — you'll need her feminine participles (radila, dobila) when you ask the questions.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Igrajmo igru: što bi radila kad bi dobila slobodnu godinu?
lines.en: Let's play a game: what would you do if you got a year off?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hm, kad bih dobila slobodnu godinu, otputovala bih na Havaje. A ti?
lines.en: Hm, if I got a year off, I would travel to Hawaii. And you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja bih kupio brod i plovio bih po Jadranu.
lines.en: I would buy a boat and sail around the Adriatic.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ti ne znaš ploviti!
lines.en: You don't know how to sail!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Zato bih prvo naučio! A onda bih te odveo na Hvar.
lines.en: That's why I would learn first! And then I would take you to Hvar.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro. A kome bi prvo javio da si dobio slobodnu godinu?
lines.en: Fine. And who would you tell first that you got a year off?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tebi, naravno. Odmah bih ti javio.
lines.en: You, of course. I would tell you right away.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Točno! 'Javio bih ti' — dativ. Bravo.
lines.en: Correct! 'Javio bih ti' — dative. Bravo.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A kad bismo imali milijun eura, kupili bismo kuću uz more.
lines.en: And if we had a million euros, we would buy a house by the sea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kad bismo… kupili bismo — super, nisi rekao 'bi'!
lines.en: Kad bismo… kupili bismo — great, you didn't say 'bi'!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Polako postajem pravi Hrvat.
lines.en: I'm slowly becoming a real Croat.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ne bih išla tako daleko, ali govoriš sve bolje!
lines.en: I wouldn't go that far, but you're speaking better and better!

days.title: Write it: Moji planovi i želje
days.objective: Write a 120-word text on your plans and wishes and get it corrected.
days.paretoFocus: This text is the week's grammar exam disguised as a diary entry — conditionals, datives and aspect all have to land correctly.
drills: Do Unit 28 of A2.hr.
Write ~120 words: real plans (futur I), wishes (conditional), people involved (dative).
Self-check every conditional clitic and svoj.
Read it to your partner for native corrections.

items: Read your text aloud for flow.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Note 3 constructions you weren't sure about.

activities.title: Model text: Moji planovi i želje
activities.intro: Before you write your 120 words, dissect a model. Each sentence shows one tool: futur I for real plans, the conditional for wishes, the dative for the people involved, and svoj wherever the subject owns. Read it aloud as one connected text, then write yours by swapping in your own life.

items.en: This year I have big plans.
items.note: A plain present opener — no need to start fancy.

items.hr: U rujnu ću položiti ispit iz hrvatskoga.
items.en: In September I will pass my Croatian exam.
items.note: Real plan → futur I. položiti = perfective: pass it, not just sit it.

items.hr: Tražit ću novi posao u Zagrebu.
items.en: I will look for a new job in Zagreb.
items.note: Infinitive-first futur: tražiti drops the final -i in writing → Tražit ću.

items.hr: Svaki dan ću učiti trideset minuta.
items.en: I will study thirty minutes every day.
items.note: Something else opens the sentence → the clitic ću slots into second position.

items.en: I would like to speak Croatian fluently.
items.note: Wish → conditional. htio for a man, htjela for a woman.

items.hr: Najviše bih volio raditi od kuće.
items.en: Most of all I would like to work from home.
items.note: najviše bih volio — the superlative wish, a lovely A2 flex.

items.hr: Želim pomoći svojim roditeljima oko kuće.
items.en: I want to help my (own) parents around the house.
items.note: pomoći komu → dative plural: svojim roditeljima. Subject = owner → svoj.

items.hr: Ženi ću kupiti poklon za godišnjicu.
items.en: I will buy my wife a present for our anniversary.
items.note: Recipient in the dative (ženi), thing in the accusative (poklon).

items.hr: Kad bih imao više slobodnog vremena, češće bih zvao brata.
items.en: If I had more free time, I would call my brother more often.
items.note: zvao (imperfective) — regular calls, a habit.

items.hr: Vjerujem da će sve moje želje postati planovi.
items.en: I believe that all my wishes will become plans.
items.note: A neat closing line — steal it for your own text.

activities.title: Self-check: the traps in your text
activities.intro: Before you read your text to your partner, hunt these errors yourself. Each item shows the classic wrong version and the fix — exactly the checklist a Croatian teacher runs through learner writing.

items.hr: ✗ Ja bi došao → ✓ Ja bih došao
items.en: I would come
items.note: First person needs bih. 'Ja bi' is the single most common conditional slip.

items.hr: ✗ Mi bi putovali → ✓ Mi bismo putovali
items.en: we would travel
items.note: bismo for mi, biste for vi — the exam favourites.

items.hr: ✗ Pomažem moje roditelje → ✓ Pomažem svojim roditeljima
items.en: I help my parents
items.note: Two fixes at once: pomagati takes the dative, and subject = owner → svoj.

items.hr: ✗ Bih htio kavu → ✓ Htio bih kavu
items.en: I would like a coffee
items.note: bih is a clitic — it can never stand first in the sentence.

items.hr: ✗ Sutra ću da radim → ✓ Sutra ću raditi
items.en: Tomorrow I will work
items.note: Croatian futur I takes the infinitive; 'da + present' here is Serbian, not Croatian.

items.hr: ✗ Kupit ću poklon mojoj ženi → ✓ Kupit ću poklon svojoj ženi
items.en: I'll buy my (own) wife a present
items.note: The subject owns → svoj beats moj every time.

items.hr: ✗ Javit ću se ti sutra → ✓ Javit ću ti se sutra
items.en: I'll get in touch with you tomorrow
items.note: Clitic order: ću (aux) → ti (dative) → se.

items.hr: ✗ Kad bih ću imati vremena… → ✓ Kad budem imao vremena… / Kad bih imao vremena…
items.en: when / if I have time
