activities.title: Genitive workout: quantities and descriptions
activities.intro: From a bare glass of water to full descriptive phrases — every question is a genitive with a different disguise.

questions.prompt: 'A glass of water' is: čaša ___
questions.explanation: Quantity nouns take the genitive: voda → vode. Čaša vode, šalica kave, boca vina — always the same move.

questions.prompt: šalica ___ kave (strong)
questions.explanation: The adjective declines with its noun: jaka kava → jake kave. Feminine genitive singular ends in -e for both.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'kilogram ___ sira' (fresh)
questions.explanation: Masculine genitive of svjež is svježeg(a) — after soft consonants (ž, č, ć, š, j, nj, lj) the ending is -eg, not -og.

questions.prompt: Imam puno ___. (a lot of work)
questions.explanation: puno + genitive. posao loses its -ao outside the nominative: posao → posla, just like the participle išao → išla.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Imam malo slobodnog ___.' (time)
questions.explanation: vrijeme grows a stem in the genitive: vrijeme → vremena. Note the ije shrinks to e in the longer form.

questions.prompt: nekoliko ___ (a few good friends)
questions.explanation: With countable nouns, nekoliko/puno/malo demand the genitive PLURAL: adjective -ih, noun -a.

questions.prompt: Describe her with two explanatory genitives: 'She is a woman of cheerful spirit and good will.'
questions.answer: Ona je žena vedrog duha i dobre volje.
questions.explanation: Two explanatory genitives chained with i: vedrog duha (m.) + dobre volje (f.). The genitive phrase follows the noun it describes.

questions.prompt: Which phrase is the TEMPORAL genitive?
questions.explanation: The three uses: prošle godine = time (no preposition!), auto moga brata = possession, čaša vode / osjećaj sreće = quantity and description. Name all three aloud, one example each.

days.title: Write it: apply for a job
days.objective: Write a short job application and mini-CV in Croatian.
days.paretoFocus: The application letter is formal writing with immediate real-world value — and it audits your genitives, conditionals and vi-forms in one page.
drills: Do Unit 55 of A2.hr.
Write 10 sentences: Javljam se na natječaj… + experience, strengths, availability.
Attach a 6-line CV: education, work history with temporal genitives (prošle godine…).
Read it to your partner and fix what they flag.

items: Read the letter aloud, formal register.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review the genitive uses once more.

activities.title: Javljam se na natječaj — the job application
activities.intro: Formal Croatian with immediate real-world value. These are the fixed phrases of every application letter — openers, body lines, closers — plus the mini-CV vocabulary. Notice how the letter quietly audits everything: genitives of quantity (pet godina iskustva), temporal genitives (prošle godine), svoj, and the capital-V Vi of written courtesy.

items.en: Dear Sir/Madam, (to a man or unknown / to a woman)
items.note: The only correct opener for a formal letter. Never 'Dragi' in an application.

items.hr: Javljam se na natječaj za radno mjesto konobara.
items.en: I am applying for the advertised position of waiter.
items.note: The key sentence: javiti se na natječaj + za radno mjesto + genitive of the job.

items.en: job competition/opening, advertisement
items.note: Vidio sam Vaš oglas… = I saw your ad… — capital V for written politeness.

items.en: I have three years of work experience.
items.note: Genitive of description: iskustvo čega? — radnog iskustva.

items.hr: Prošle godine završio sam tečaj hrvatskog jezika.
items.en: Last year I completed a Croatian language course.
items.note: Temporal genitive opens the CV line; tečaj čega? — another genitive.

items.hr: Radio sam kao prodavač u velikoj trgovini.
items.en: I worked as a salesman in a large shop.
items.note: raditi kao + nominative of the job.

items.hr: Moje su prednosti odgovornost i strpljivost.
items.en: My strengths are responsibility and patience.
items.note: prednost = advantage/strength; abstract nouns in -ost are feminine (i-declension).

items.hr: Tečno govorim engleski, a hrvatski učim već dvije godine.
items.en: I speak English fluently, and I have been learning Croatian for two years.
items.note: već + present = 'have been …ing' — Croatian uses the present for ongoing duration.

items.en: I am available from the first of September.
items.note: od + genitive; the ordinal declines too: prvi rujna → od prvog rujna.

items.en: I am sending my CV attached.
items.note: svoj, not moj — the subject (ja) is the owner. u prilogu = attached, enclosed.

items.hr: Stojim Vam na raspolaganju za razgovor.
items.en: I am at your disposal for an interview.
items.note: The classic pre-closing line. razgovor (za posao) = job interview.

items.en: Yours faithfully / Respectfully,
items.note: The only closer you need. s + instrumental: poštovanje → s poštovanjem.

items.en: CV: education, work experience, skills
items.note: The three section headings of your six-line CV.

activities.title: The application audit
activities.intro: Register, prepositions, genitives, svoj — everything an employer's eye would catch, one trap at a time.

questions.prompt: You are writing to an unknown woman at a company. The letter starts:
questions.explanation: Formal letters open with Poštovani / Poštovana gospođo. Everything else on that list would end your application in the bin.

questions.prompt: Javljam se ___ natječaj za radno mjesto konobara.
questions.explanation: The fixed rection: javiti se NA natječaj. The za comes later — za radno mjesto.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Imam pet godina radnog ___.' (experience)
questions.explanation: Genitive of description: radno iskustvo → radnog iskustva. Yesterday's pattern earning its salary.

questions.prompt: 'I am available from the first of September':
questions.explanation: od + genitive, and the ordinal declines with the month: od prvog rujna.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'U prilogu šaljem ___ životopis.' (my — the subject owns it)
questions.explanation: When the subject is the possessor, Croatian requires svoj: šaljem svoj životopis. 'Moj' is understandable but marked — the standard wants svoj.

questions.prompt: Which closing line keeps the formal Vi-register correctly?
options: Stojim Vam na raspolaganju za razgovor.
Stojim ti na raspolaganju za razgovor.
Stojim Vas na raspolaganju za razgovor.
Stojiš mi na raspolaganju za razgovor.
questions.answer: Stojim Vam na raspolaganju za razgovor.
questions.explanation: stajati komu na raspolaganju — dative Vam, with the courtesy capital V that written Croatian expects in letters.

questions.prompt: Build the CV line: 'Last year I completed a Croatian language course.'
questions.answer: Prošle godine završio sam tečaj hrvatskog jezika.
questions.explanation: Temporal genitive first (Prošle godine), then the clitic sam in second position after the participle: završio sam.

questions.prompt: Which line is TOO informal for an application letter?
options: Javljam se na natječaj za radno mjesto prodavača.
questions.explanation: Čujemo se ('talk soon!') is for friends and texts. A letter closes with S poštovanjem — register is part of the grammar here.

days.title: Week 17 speaking day (+ Unit 56)
days.objective: Talk about work and ambitions live, then bank a lighter unit.
days.paretoFocus: Explaining your work in Croatian to a real listener is the strongest retrieval practice this module allows — everything from Unit 49 onward feeds it.
drills: 15-minute conversation: your work, your ideal job, workplace stories.
Do Unit 56 of A2.hr.
Work in 3 temporal genitives and 2 comparatives naturally.
Note corrections; queue unknown words in the Words tab.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say your 3 best corrected sentences.
Recall: three uses of the genitive.

activities.title: Talking shop — your work in ten lines
activities.intro: Today's conversation fuel: ten lines about your job, your boss and your ambitions. Each one smuggles in a pattern from the last two weeks — temporal genitives, comparatives, a conditional, futur II. Rehearse them aloud now; in the conversation you'll need them without looking.

items.hr: Radim kao programer u maloj tvrtki.
items.en: I work as a programmer in a small company.
items.note: raditi kao + nominative; u maloj tvrtki = locative.

items.hr: Na poslu najviše volim raditi s ljudima.
items.en: At work I most enjoy working with people.
items.note: s + instrumental plural: ljudi → s ljudima.

items.hr: Moj idealan posao bio bi bliže moru.
items.en: My ideal job would be closer to the sea.
items.note: Conditional bio bi + comparative bliže + dative moru.

items.hr: Prije dvije godine promijenio sam posao.
items.en: Two years ago I changed jobs.
items.note: prije + genitive for 'ago'; promijeniti = perfective, one completed change.

items.hr: Prošlog mjeseca imao sam previše sastanaka.
items.en: Last month I had too many meetings.
items.note: Temporal genitive prošlog mjeseca + quantity genitive previše sastanaka — two genitives, one complaint.

items.hr: Kolege su mi drage, ali šef zna biti strog.
items.en: I like my colleagues, but the boss can be strict.
items.note: znati + infinitive = 'tends to / can be' — a very native turn of phrase.

items.hr: Jednog dana želim voditi svoj tim.
items.en: One day I want to lead my own team.
items.note: Temporal genitive jednog dana; svoj because the subject will own the team.

items.hr: Posao mi je zanimljiviji nego prije.
items.en: My job is more interesting than before.
items.note: Comparative in -iji + nego. mi je = 'my … is' with the dative of the experiencer.

items.hr: Radim od devet do pet, ponekad i duže.
items.en: I work nine to five, sometimes even longer.
items.note: od/do + genitive; duže = comparative adverb of dugo.

items.hr: Ako sve bude u redu, sljedeće godine tražit ću povišicu.
items.en: If all goes well, next year I'll ask for a raise.
items.note: Futur II (bude) in the ako-clause, futur I in the result — plus one more temporal genitive.

activities.title: Warm-up before the conversation
activities.intro: Quick reps before the 15-minute talk: two listening checks, the patterns you must work in live (three temporal genitives, two comparatives), and the lines you'll actually say.

questions.prompt: 'Radim kao konobar u restoranu' means:
options: I work as a waiter in a restaurant.
I want to be a waiter in a restaurant.
I worked as a cook in a restaurant.
I am looking for a waiter for my restaurant.
questions.answer: I work as a waiter in a restaurant.
questions.explanation: raditi kao + job in the nominative = to work as. Present tense radim = I work.

questions.prompt: Listen: what happened last month?
questions.explanation: Prošlog mjeseca = last month (temporal genitive), previše sastanaka = too many meetings (quantity genitive).
questions.audioText: Prošlog mjeseca imao sam previše sastanaka.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Radim od devet ___ pet.' (from nine TO five)
questions.explanation: The pair od … do … + genitive frames any span: od devet do pet, od ponedjeljka do petka.

questions.prompt: Listen: what would his ideal job be like?
questions.explanation: bliže moru = closer to the sea — comparative adverb bliže + dative. bio bi marks it as a daydream, not a plan.
questions.audioText: Moj idealan posao bio bi bliže moru.

questions.prompt: Work in a temporal genitive: '___ godine promijenio sam posao.' (last)
questions.explanation: prošla godina → prošle godine, no preposition. That's one of the three temporal genitives you owe the conversation.

questions.prompt: Say your ambition: 'One day I want to lead my own team.'
questions.answer: Jednog dana želim voditi svoj tim.
questions.explanation: Temporal genitive up front (jednog dana), and svoj tim because the subject is the future owner.

questions.prompt: One of your two required comparatives: 'Posao mi je ___ nego prije.'
questions.explanation: nego prije demands a comparative: zanimljiv → zanimljiviji. The superlative najzanimljiviji would need 'of all', not 'than before'.

activities.title: Razgovor o poslu — the interview at home
activities.intro: Your speaking day script — a model for the real 15-minute conversation. She interviews you about work and ambitions; your job is to land three temporal genitives and two comparatives without her noticing the effort. (She notices.)

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danas vježbamo razgovor o poslu. Pitaj me nešto!
lines.en: Today we're practising a conversation about work. Ask me something!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobro. Što najviše voliš na svom poslu?
lines.en: Okay. What do you like most about your job?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Najviše volim raditi s ljudima. Prošle godine dobio sam novi projekt i sad je posao zanimljiviji nego prije.
lines.en: I most enjoy working with people. Last year I got a new project and now the job is more interesting than before.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: 'Zanimljiviji nego prije' — odličan komparativ! A kakav bi bio tvoj idealan posao?
lines.en: 'More interesting than before' — excellent comparative! And what would your ideal job be like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Moj idealan posao bio bi bliže moru. Radio bih manje, a plivao bih više.
lines.en: My ideal job would be closer to the sea. I would work less and swim more.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! A šef? Kakav je tvoj šef?
lines.en: Ha! And the boss? What's your boss like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Šef mi je dobar, ali zna biti strog. Prošlog mjeseca imao sam previše sastanaka.
lines.en: My boss is good, but he can be strict. Last month I had too many meetings.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: 'Prošlog mjeseca' — vremenski genitiv, bravo. Imaš li kakvu ambiciju?
lines.en: 'Last month' — temporal genitive, bravo. Do you have any ambition?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jednog dana želim voditi svoj tim. Zato svakog jutra učim nešto novo.
lines.en: One day I want to lead my own team. That's why I learn something new every morning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tri vremenska genitiva u jednom razgovoru! Napredniji si nego prošlog tjedna.
lines.en: Three temporal genitives in one conversation! You're more advanced than last week.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala! Ako sve bude u redu, sljedeće godine tražit ću povišicu.
lines.en: Thanks! If all goes well, next year I'll ask for a raise.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Samo hrabro. Tvoj hrvatski već zvuči profesionalno.
lines.en: Go for it. Your Croatian already sounds professional.

days.title: Sjećanja — childhood memories
days.objective: Open the memories theme: childhood vocabulary and first recollections.
days.paretoFocus: djetinjstvo, uspomena, sjećati se — memories are the A2 storytelling theme, and everything you know about past aspect finally gets a stage.
drills: Do Unit 57 of A2.hr.
Learn 12 memory words: djetinjstvo, uspomena, sjećati se, odrastati, igračka, susjedstvo…
Answer: Čega se najradije sjećaš iz djetinjstva? (genitive after sjećati se!)
Sketch 5 bullet points of a childhood memory for tomorrow's story.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'I remember my grandmother's garden.'
Review temporal genitives for storytelling.

activities.title: Sjećanja — the childhood word kit
activities.intro: The memories theme opens here. Twelve-plus words for talking about childhood — and one crucial rection hiding among them: sjećati se takes the GENITIVE. Čega se sjećaš? What do you remember? Every answer this week will start from that question.

items.en: childhood
items.note: sretno djetinjstvo = a happy childhood; iz djetinjstva = from childhood (iz + G).

items.en: memory, keepsake
items.note: najljepša uspomena = the loveliest memory. Also a physical memento.

items.en: to remember
items.note: The key rection: Sjećam se svog prvog bicikla — genitive, not accusative!

items.en: memory (the mental act, a recollection)
items.note: uspomena is the treasured thing; sjećanje is the act or trace of remembering.

items.en: to grow up (imperfective / perfective)
items.note: Odrastao sam u malom gradu. = I grew up in a small town.

items.en: toy
items.note: Plural igračke. moja najdraža igračka = my favourite toy.

items.en: neighbourhood
items.note: djeca iz susjedstva = the neighbourhood kids.

items.en: grandpa and grandma
items.note: Ljeta kod djeda i bake — kod + genitive, the classic Croatian childhood.

items.en: grandson / granddaughter
items.note: Bio sam njihov najstariji unuk.

items.en: to play hide-and-seek
items.note: igrati se + genitive of the game: igrali smo se skrivača.

items.en: swing
items.note: ljuljati se = to swing, to rock — hear the lj twice.

items.en: to climb a tree
items.note: Present: penjem se. na + accusative for the direction of the climb.

items.hr: Čega se najradije sjećaš iz djetinjstva?
items.en: What do you remember most fondly from childhood?
items.note: čega = genitive of što — the question matches the verb's case.

items.en: I most fondly remember grandma's garden.
items.note: bakin vrt → bakina vrta: the possessive adjective declines through the genitive too.

activities.title: Remembering in the genitive
activities.intro: Vocabulary first, then the rection that rules the week: sjećati se + genitive, igrati se + genitive, and a first taste of habitual past for tomorrow.

questions.prompt: 'Childhood' is:
questions.explanation: djetinjstvo = childhood — built on dijete (child). susjedstvo = neighbourhood, uspomena = keepsake/memory.

questions.prompt: The verb sjećati se takes which case?
questions.explanation: sjećati se + genitive: Sjećam se mora, sjećam se bake. Never the accusative — this rection defines the whole memories theme.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Sjećam se svog prvog ___.' (bicycle)
questions.explanation: Genitive after sjećati se: bicikl → bicikla, and the whole phrase declines: svog prvog bicikla.

questions.prompt: Which question word asks 'WHAT do you remember?'
questions.explanation: što declines: čega (G), čemu (D), čime (I). Because sjećati se takes the genitive, the question must too: Čega se sjećaš?

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ljeta sam provodio kod ___ i bake.' (grandpa)
questions.explanation: kod + genitive: djed → djeda. Kod djeda i bake — where half of Croatia spent every summer.

questions.prompt: 'Every summer we went (used to go) to the seaside':
options: Svakog ljeta išli smo na more.
Svakog ljeta otišli smo na more.
Svakog ljeta otišao sam na more.
questions.answer: Svakog ljeta išli smo na more.
questions.explanation: A repeated habit needs the imperfective: išli smo. Otišli (perfective) would mean one single departure — the full contrast arrives on day 126.

questions.prompt: Ask the theme question: 'What do you remember most fondly from childhood?'
questions.answer: Čega se najradije sjećaš iz djetinjstva?
questions.explanation: Čega leads (genitive of što), the clitic se sits in second position, and iz + genitive closes: iz djetinjstva.

questions.prompt: 'We played hide-and-seek': Igrali smo se ___
questions.explanation: igrati se + genitive of the game: skrivač → skrivača. Same logic as sjećati se — the se-verb reaches for the genitive.

days.title: Sequencing a story
days.objective: Chain events with najprije, zatim, potom, kasnije, na kraju.
days.paretoFocus: Sequencers plus temporal genitives are the skeleton of narrative — the exact linking skill the A2 writing descriptors name.
drills: Do Unit 58 of A2.hr.
Expand yesterday's bullet points into a sequenced 8-sentence story.
Timestamp it: jednog ljeta, svakog jutra, prošle godine…
Tell it aloud twice; second time from memory.

items: Re-tell the story in 60 seconds.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
List the 5 sequencers cold.

activities.title: Najprije, zatim, na kraju — the story skeleton
activities.intro: Five sequencers turn loose sentences into a story: najprije → zatim → potom → kasnije → na kraju. Add timestamps in the temporal genitive (jednog ljeta, svakog jutra) and you have the full narrative skeleton. The last four items walk through a model story — the same shape your 8-sentence memory should take today.

items.en: first (of all)
items.note: Najprije smo se probudili rano. — opens the chain of events.

items.en: then, next
items.note: The workhorse connector: zatim smo doručkovali.

items.en: after that
items.note: A slightly more formal twin of zatim — good for written stories.

items.en: later
items.note: Comparative of kasno — literally 'more late'.

items.en: in the end, finally
items.note: Also 'at the end of' + genitive: na kraju dana, na kraju ljeta.

items.en: before that / after that
items.note: prije and nakon take the genitive: toga. Handy for jumping around the timeline.

items.hr: jednog ljeta, svakog jutra, prošle godine
items.en: one summer, every morning, last year
items.note: The temporal genitive timestamps — no preposition, straight into the story.

items.hr: Jednog ljeta otišli smo kod bake na otok.
items.en: One summer we went to grandma's on the island.
items.note: Model story, sentence 1: timestamp + perfective otići = the story's opening event.

items.hr: Najprije smo se kupali, zatim smo ručali kod bake.
items.en: First we swam, then we had lunch at grandma's.
items.note: Two sequencers, one sentence — the clitics (smo se, smo) stay in second position of each clause.

items.hr: Kasnije smo lovili ribu s djedom.
items.en: Later we fished with grandpa.
items.note: loviti ribu = to fish; s djedom = instrumental of company.

items.hr: Na kraju ljeta nisam htio ići kući.
items.en: At the end of the summer I didn't want to go home.
items.note: na kraju + genitive (ljeta); kući = homeward, the special adverbial form.

activities.title: Chain the events
activities.intro: Sequencers in order, timestamps in the genitive, and two full story sentences to rebuild — the exact moves your 8-sentence story needs.

questions.prompt: Which sequencer opens a story?
questions.explanation: The canonical chain: najprije → zatim/potom → kasnije → na kraju. Start at the start.

questions.prompt: Which sequencer closes the story?
questions.explanation: na kraju = in the end / finally. Bonus: with a genitive it means 'at the end of' — na kraju ljeta.

questions.prompt: Timestamp the opening: '___ ljeta otišli smo na otok.' (one)
questions.explanation: Temporal genitive, no preposition: jedno ljeto → jednog ljeta = one summer.

questions.prompt: nakon + which form? 'after lunch' =
questions.explanation: nakon takes the genitive: ručak → ručka (fleeting a). Same for prije: prije ručka.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Svakog ___ smo plivali prije doručka.' (morning)
questions.explanation: svako jutro → svakog jutra — the habitual timestamp. And prije doručka: genitive after prije.

questions.prompt: Rebuild: 'First we swam, then we had lunch.'
questions.answer: Najprije smo se kupali, zatim smo ručali.
questions.explanation: Each clause carries its own second-position clitics: Najprije SMO SE kupali, zatim SMO ručali.

questions.prompt: Narrative aspect check: 'Zatim sam ___ pismo i poslao ga.'
questions.explanation: In a sequenced chain each event is completed before the next — perfective napisao. Pisao sam would leave the letter unfinished forever.

questions.prompt: Close the story: 'At the end of the summer I didn't want to go home.'
questions.answer: Na kraju ljeta nisam htio ići kući.
questions.explanation: na kraju + genitive ljeta; nisam htio = negated perfekt of htjeti; kući = the adverb 'homeward', no preposition.

days.title: Kad sam bio dijete…
days.objective: Narrate habitual past (imperfective) against one-time events (perfective).
days.paretoFocus: 'Kad sam bio dijete, svakog ljeta smo išli na more. Jedne godine naučio sam plivati.' — habitual vs punctual is aspect's finest distinction, mastered through memories.
drills: Do Unit 59 of A2.hr.
Write 4 habitual-past sentences (imperfective) and 4 one-time events (perfective).
Merge them into one childhood paragraph.
Note the noun dijete and its odd genitive djeteta — full pattern comes tomorrow.

items: Say one habitual + one one-time memory pair.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review story sequencers.

activities.title: Kad sam bio dijete… — habitual vs one-time
activities.intro: Aspect's finest distinction, staged as memories. Habits and repeated summers take the imperfective (išli smo, budio sam se, pekla je); single events that happened once take the perfective (naučio sam, probudio sam se, ispekla je). Learn the pairs, then the signal words that give the choice away.

items.hr: Kad sam bio dijete, svakog ljeta smo išli na more.
items.en: When I was a child, we went to the seaside every summer.
items.note: Habit → imperfective ići. This is THE model sentence of the day.

items.en: One year I learned to swim.
items.note: One completed breakthrough → perfective naučiti.

items.hr: Svakog jutra budio sam se rano.
items.en: Every morning I woke up early.
items.note: buditi se (imperfective) — the daily routine.

items.hr: Jednog jutra probudio sam se prije svih.
items.en: One morning I woke up before everyone.
items.note: probuditi se (perfective) — that one particular morning.

items.hr: Baka je svake nedjelje pekla kolače.
items.en: Grandma baked cakes every Sunday.
items.note: peći (imperfective) for the weekly ritual; svake nedjelje = temporal genitive.

items.hr: Jednom je ispekla tortu za pedeset ljudi.
items.en: Once she baked a cake for fifty people.
items.note: ispeći (perfective): one legendary cake, finished and eaten.

items.en: I often fell off my bike.
items.note: padati (imperfective) — falling as a recurring childhood condition.

items.hr: Jednom sam pao i slomio ruku.
items.en: Once I fell and broke my arm.
items.note: pasti + slomiti (both perfective): one fall, one broken arm, one story.

items.en: We watched cartoons every day.
items.note: gledati (imperfective); crtić = cartoon.

items.hr: Jedne večeri pogledali smo cijeli film do kraja.
items.en: One evening we watched a whole film to the end.
items.note: pogledati (perfective): do kraja = to the end — completion made explicit.

items.hr: svaki dan, često, obično, uvijek → nesvršeni
items.en: every day, often, usually, always → imperfective
items.note: These signal words announce a habit — reach for the imperfective.

items.en: once, one year, suddenly → perfective
items.note: These pin one moment to the timeline — reach for the perfective.

items.en: child → of the child
items.note: A teaser: dijete grows a -t- in the genitive. The full shape-shifter pattern comes tomorrow.

activities.title: Habit or event?
activities.intro: Every question is the same decision in a new costume: is this a repeated summer or a single moment? Signal words are your compass.

questions.prompt: 'Svakog ljeta' (every summer) signals which aspect?
options: neither — it blocks the past tense
questions.explanation: svaki/svaka/svako + time = repetition = imperfective. jedne godine / jednom point the other way.

questions.prompt: Kad sam bio dijete, svakog ljeta smo ___ na more.
questions.explanation: Habit → imperfective ići → išli smo. Otišli would report one single departure.

questions.prompt: One-time breakthrough: 'Jedne godine ___ sam plivati.' (learned)
questions.explanation: jedne godine = one specific year → perfective naučiti → naučio sam. Učio sam would mean you were still practising.

questions.prompt: Svakog jutra ___ sam se rano.
questions.explanation: Daily routine → imperfective buditi se → budio sam se. Probudio sam se belongs to ONE morning.

questions.prompt: The accident: 'Jednom sam ___ s bicikla i slomio ruku.' (fell)
questions.explanation: jednom = once → perfective pasti → pao. The habitual padao sam pairs with često, not jednom.

questions.prompt: Baka je svake nedjelje ___ kolače.
questions.explanation: svake nedjelje = weekly ritual → imperfective peći → pekla je. Ispekla je is for that one heroic fifty-person cake.

questions.prompt: One particular morning: 'One morning I woke up before everyone.'
questions.answer: Jednog jutra probudio sam se prije svih.
questions.explanation: jednog jutra pins one morning → perfective probuditi se. Clitic order: sam before se. prije + genitive: svih.

questions.prompt: Which pair contrasts habit and event CORRECTLY?
options: Često sam padao s bicikla, a jednom sam slomio ruku.
Često sam pao s bicikla, a jednom sam lomio ruku.
Često sam padao s bicikla, a jednom sam lomio ruku.
Često sam pao s bicikla, a jednom sam slomio ruku.
questions.answer: Često sam padao s bicikla, a jednom sam slomio ruku.
questions.explanation: često → imperfective padao; jednom → perfective slomio. The wrong versions break exactly one half each — that's how subtle this gets.

days.title: Shape-shifting nouns: ime, vrijeme, dijete
days.objective: Decline nouns with -(e)n-/-(e)t- stem extensions.
days.paretoFocus: ime → imena, vrijeme → vremena, dijete → djeteta — a tiny class of very frequent nouns that grows a syllable outside the nominative. Unit 60 closes Module 6.
drills: Do Unit 60 of A2.hr — Module 6 complete!
Study Grammar tab: A2 · special noun patterns (stem extensions).
Decline ime, vrijeme and dijete through the singular.
Use each in a sentence: Nemam vremena. Kako je ime tvog djeteta?

items: Give the genitive of ime, vrijeme, dijete.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Note: 60 units down, 20 to go.

activities.title: Shape-shifters: ime, vrijeme, dijete
activities.intro: A tiny class of very frequent neuter nouns grows a syllable the moment it leaves the nominative: ime → imena, vrijeme → vremena, dijete → djeteta. Learn the singular of all three, the trick plural of dijete (djeca!), and the everyday sentences these nouns live in. Module 6 closes today — 60 units down.

items.en: name → of the name, to the name, with the name
items.note: The stem grows -(e)n-: imen-. G imena, D/L imenu, I imenom.

items.hr: Kako se piše vaše ime i prezime?
items.en: How do you spell your first and last name?
items.note: The form-filling question you will hear at every counter in Croatia.

items.en: My name isn't on the list.
items.note: nema + genitive: imena. The extension appears the moment the case changes.

items.en: time/weather → of time, to time, with time
items.note: Watch the ije shrink to e in every extended form: vrEmena, not 'vrijemena'.

items.en: I don't have time.
items.note: Probably the most frequent shape-shifted form in the language.

items.en: How much time do we have?
items.note: koliko + genitive — quantity genitive meets the stem extension.

items.en: child → of the child, to the child, with the child
items.note: The -(e)t- extension: djetet-. Same ije → je shortening: dIJEte → dJEteta.

items.en: What is your child's name?
items.note: Yesterday's teaser resolved: tvog djeteta, genitive with the -t- stem.

items.en: Plural: names, times — but 'children'!
items.note: dijete has no regular plural: djeca (a collective) does the job instead.

items.en: The children are playing in the park.
items.note: djeca looks singular but takes a plural verb: djeca SE IGRAJU.

items.en: shoulder → shoulders; calf → of the calf
items.note: The rest of the little family: rame goes the -(e)n- way, tele the -(e)t- way like dijete.

items.en: With time, everything gets easier.
items.note: Instrumental vremenom — a proverb-grade sentence and today's motto.

activities.title: Grow the stem
activities.intro: Three nouns, one habit: add the syllable before the ending. If you can clear these eight, the whole shape-shifter class is yours.

questions.prompt: The genitive of ime is:
questions.explanation: The stem grows -(e)n-: ime → imen- + a = imena. 'Ima' would be the verb 'has'!

questions.prompt: Nemam ___! (I don't have time)
questions.explanation: nemati + genitive → vremena. Note the ije shortens to e in the extended stem — 'vrijemena' does not exist.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Kako je ime tvog ___?' (child)
questions.explanation: dijete takes the -(e)t- extension: djetet- + a = djeteta. And dIJEte → dJEteta — the ije shortens too.

questions.prompt: The plural of dijete is:
questions.explanation: dijete has no regular plural — the collective djeca steps in. (dijeta means 'diet', a different word entirely.)

questions.prompt: Complete the proverb: 'S ___ sve postaje lakše.' (with time)
questions.explanation: Instrumental of vrijeme: vremen- + om = vremenom. Stem extension plus the regular neuter -om.

questions.prompt: Which noun declines like ime (rame → ?)
questions.explanation: rame belongs to the same -(e)n- family: rame → ramena. more, selo and pismo are ordinary neuters: mora, sela, pisma.

questions.prompt: Ask the counter question: 'How do you spell your first and last name?'
questions.answer: Kako se piše vaše ime i prezime?
questions.explanation: The se sits in second position after kako. In the nominative, ime keeps its short shape — the extension only appears in other cases.

questions.prompt: Djeca ___ u parku.
questions.explanation: djeca is a collective that ends like a feminine singular but takes a PLURAL verb: djeca se igraju, djeca su bila…

days.title: Instrumental across the whole phrase
days.objective: Decline adjectives, demonstratives, possessives and svoj in the instrumental.
days.paretoFocus: 'sa svojom najboljom prijateljicom', 'ovim brzim vlakom' — the instrumental is the last case to get full-phrase treatment. After today, the whole declension table is yours.
drills: Do Unit 61 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · adjective declension (instrumental).
Decline 6 phrases: moja stara prijateljica → s mojom starom prijateljicom.
Write 5 sentences: with whom and by what means you did things last week.

items: Say 'with my best friend' with full agreement.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Celebrate: every case now declines the full phrase.

activities.title: Sa svojom najboljom prijateljicom — the full instrumental phrase
activities.intro: The last case gets the full-phrase treatment. Masculine and neuter modifiers take -im (ovim brzim vlakom), feminine takes -om (s mojom starom prijateljicom). One boundary to guard: company takes s/sa, means of transport takes nothing. After today the entire declension table — every case, every word in the phrase — is yours.

items.en: with my old friend (f.)
items.note: Feminine instrumental all the way down: -om, -om, -om.

items.en: with my (own) best friend
items.note: svoj because the subject is the friend's 'owner'; sa (not s) before another s-.

items.en: by this fast train
items.note: Masculine: demonstrative and adjective in -im, noun in -om. NO preposition — it's a means.

items.en: with that nice neighbour
items.note: Company → s. taj → tim, simpatičan → simpatičnim (fleeting a out, -im in).

items.en: by one's own new car
items.note: The foreign noun auto declines as masculine: auto → autom. Means → no preposition.

items.en: with our dear grandma
items.note: naša → našom, draga → dragom, baka → bakom — a clean feminine row.

items.en: I travel by train. / I travel with my brother.
items.note: The rule of the day: means → bare instrumental; company → s + instrumental.

items.hr: Prošlog tjedna išao sam u kino sa svojim mlađim bratom.
items.en: Last week I went to the cinema with my younger brother.
items.note: Temporal genitive + full instrumental phrase: sa svojim mlađim bratom.

items.en: I write with a black pen.
items.note: Instrument → no preposition. kemijska (olovka) = ballpoint pen.

items.hr: Vozim se na posao gradskim autobusom.
items.en: I ride to work on the city bus.
items.note: gradski → gradskim; relational -ski adjectives decline like definites.

items.en: I talked with her mother.
items.note: njezina → njezinom — possessives take the same -om as adjectives here.

items.hr: sa mnom, s tobom, s njom
items.en: with me, with you, with her
items.note: The pronoun instrumentals — and the fixed exception sa mnom (never 's mnom').

activities.title: Full-phrase instrumental drill
activities.intro: Agreement across the whole phrase, the company-vs-means boundary, and the s/sa spelling rule — the instrumental's final exam.

questions.prompt: 'By train' is:
questions.explanation: Means of transport = bare instrumental, no preposition. S vlakom would mean the train is your travel companion.

questions.prompt: Idem u kino s ___ prijateljicom.
questions.explanation: Feminine instrumental singular: moja → mojom, matching prijateljicom. -oj would be dative/locative.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Putujem ovim brzim ___.' (train)
questions.explanation: The noun closes the agreement chain: ovim (dem.) + brzim (adj.) + vlakom (noun) — im, -im, -om.

questions.prompt: Which one needs the preposition s?
questions.explanation: Company gets s: s bratom. Means of transport travel bare: vlakom, autom, biciklom.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Išao sam u kino sa ___ mlađim bratom.' (my own)
questions.explanation: Subject = possessor → svoj; masculine instrumental → svojim. And sa before the s- of svojim.

questions.prompt: 'With my best friend (f.)', full agreement:
questions.explanation: Every word wears feminine instrumental -om: svojom najboljom prijateljicom. The -oj row is dative/locative, not instrumental.

questions.prompt: Build: 'I ride to work on the city bus.'
questions.answer: Vozim se na posao gradskim autobusom.
questions.explanation: voziti se + bare instrumental of means: gradskim autobusom. The se clings to second position after Vozim.

questions.prompt: 'With me' is:
questions.explanation: The fixed form: sa mnom — sa is obligatory before the consonant cluster mn. Elsewhere the rule is sa before s, š, z, ž.

days.title: Write it: a memory
days.objective: Write a 120-word childhood memory with instrumental company and genitive time.
days.paretoFocus: This text is the memories module in miniature: temporal genitives, aspect contrast, instrumental companions — narrative writing at true A2 level.
drills: Do Unit 62 of A2.hr.
Write ~120 words about one childhood summer or friendship.
Require of yourself: 2 temporal genitives, 2 instrumental phrases, both aspects.
Read it to your partner and fix what they flag.

items: Read the memory aloud with feeling.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review instrumental phrase endings.

activities.title: The memory text — building blocks
activities.intro: Today you write ~120 words about one childhood summer. Here is a model text, sentence by sentence, with its requirements ticked off as you go: two temporal genitives, two instrumental phrases, both aspects. Borrow the skeleton, replace the details with your own memory.

items.hr: Jednog ljeta, kad mi je bilo osam godina, otišli smo kod bake na otok.
items.en: One summer, when I was eight, we went to grandma's on the island.
items.note: Temporal genitive #1 (jednog ljeta) + the age formula: kad mi je bilo osam godina.

items.hr: Svakog jutra budio sam se uz miris kave i mora.
items.en: Every morning I woke up to the smell of coffee and the sea.
items.note: Temporal genitive #2 + habitual imperfective (budio sam se) + explanatory genitive (miris kave).

items.hr: S djedom sam svakog popodneva lovio ribu.
items.en: Every afternoon I fished with grandpa.
items.note: Instrumental phrase #1: s djedom (company). Still the habitual imperfective: lovio sam.

items.hr: Najprije smo plivali, zatim smo jeli sladoled na rivi.
items.en: First we swam, then we ate ice cream on the seafront.
items.note: Yesterday's sequencers stitching the routine together.

items.hr: Jedne večeri djed me je naučio voziti barku.
items.en: One evening grandpa taught me to drive the boat.
items.note: The perfective turn: jedne večeri + naučio — one event, completed. Aspect contrast achieved.

items.hr: Nikad neću zaboraviti miris bakina kruha.
items.en: I will never forget the smell of grandma's bread.
items.note: zaboraviti + accusative; bakin kruh → bakina kruha, the possessive adjective declining.

items.hr: Na kraju ljeta uvijek mi je bilo teško otići.
items.en: At the end of the summer it was always hard for me to leave.
items.note: na kraju + genitive; uvijek + imperfective bilo — the recurring goodbye.

items.hr: Danas se tog ljeta sjećam sa smiješkom.
items.en: Today I remember that summer with a smile.
items.note: sjećati se + genitive (tog ljeta) and instrumental phrase #2: sa smiješkom.

items.hr: Sa svojom sestrom još uvijek razgovaram o tim ljetima.
items.en: I still talk about those summers with my sister.
items.note: A closing option: full instrumental phrase + o + locative plural (o tim ljetima).

items.hr: Checklist: 2× vremenski genitiv, 2× instrumental, oba vida
items.en: Checklist: 2 temporal genitives, 2 instrumental phrases, both aspects
items.note: Before reading to your partner, tick each requirement in your own text.

activities.title: Audit the memory
activities.intro: The same audit your partner will run on your text — requirement by requirement. If you can grade the model, you can grade your own.

questions.prompt: Which phrase is a temporal genitive?
questions.explanation: jednog ljeta = one summer, no preposition — pure temporal genitive. s djedom and sa smiješkom are instrumentals, na rivi is locative.

questions.prompt: The age formula: 'Kad mi je ___ osam godina…' (when I was eight)
questions.explanation: Age is impersonal in the past: kad mi je BILO osam godina — neuter bilo, with the dative mi.

questions.prompt: Instrumental of company: 'S ___ sam lovio ribu.'
questions.explanation: s + instrumental: djed → djedom. That's instrumental requirement #1 of your text.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Danas se tog ljeta ___ sa smiješkom.' (remember)
questions.explanation: sjećati se + genitive: sjećam se tog ljeta. The se already sits in second position, so only the verb is missing.

questions.prompt: Aspect check: 'Jedne večeri djed me je ___ voziti barku.'
questions.explanation: jedne večeri = one completed event → perfective naučiti → naučio. Učio would mean the lesson never quite ended.

questions.prompt: The recurring goodbye: 'At the end of the summer it was hard for me to leave.'
questions.answer: Na kraju ljeta bilo mi je teško otići.
questions.explanation: Impersonal bilo + dative mi; the clitic chain mi je follows the participle. na kraju + genitive ljeta.

questions.prompt: Which sentence fulfils the 'instrumental phrase with full agreement' requirement?
options: Sa svojom sestrom razgovaram o tim ljetima.
Sa svoja sestra razgovaram o tim ljetima.
Sa svojoj sestri razgovaram o tim ljetima.
Sa svoju sestru razgovaram o tim ljetima.
questions.answer: Sa svojom sestrom razgovaram o tim ljetima.
questions.explanation: sa + instrumental: svojom sestrom, both words in -om. The -oj/-i row is dative/locative, -u is accusative — close, but the audit catches them.

activities.title: Čitanje uspomene — reading it to her
activities.intro: The final drill of the day, staged: you read your memory text aloud, she ticks off the requirements — and catches the one slip you didn't see. Read your lines with feeling; the text deserves it.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Napisao sam tekst o jednom ljetu iz djetinjstva. Mogu li ti ga pročitati?
lines.en: I wrote a text about one summer from my childhood. Can I read it to you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Naravno. Čitaj polako, slušam.
lines.en: Of course. Read slowly, I'm listening.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jednog ljeta otišli smo kod bake na otok. Svakog jutra budio sam se uz miris kave i mora.
lines.en: One summer we went to grandma's on the island. Every morning I woke up to the smell of coffee and the sea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Lijepo! Dva vremenska genitiva odmah na početku.
lines.en: Lovely! Two temporal genitives right at the start.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sa djedom sam svakog popodneva lovio ribu. Jedne večeri me je naučio voziti barku.
lines.en: With grandpa I fished every afternoon. One evening he taught me to drive the boat.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: 'Naučio' — svršeni glagol za jedan događaj, točno. Ali čekaj, nešto sam čula…
lines.en: 'Naučio' — perfective for a single event, correct. But wait, I heard something…

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Na kraju ljeta uvijek mi je bilo teško otići. Danas se tog ljeta sjećam sa smiješkom.
lines.en: At the end of the summer it was always hard for me to leave. Today I remember that summer with a smile.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: 'Sjećam se tog ljeta' — genitiv poslije sjećati se, odlično. Sad ispravka: rekao si 'sa djedom', a treba 's djedom'.
lines.en: 'I remember that summer' — genitive after sjećati se, excellent. Now the correction: you said 'sa djedom', but it should be 's djedom'.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ah, da — 'sa' ide samo ispred s, š, z, ž… i ispred 'mnom'. Ispravit ću u tekstu.
lines.en: Ah, right — 'sa' only goes before s, š, z, ž… and before 'mnom'. I'll fix it in the text.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Točno tako. Sve ostalo je bilo izvrsno — sutra mi ga ispričaj bez papira!
lines.en: Exactly. Everything else was excellent — tomorrow, tell it to me without the paper!

days.title: Week 18 review + speaking: a 3-minute memory
days.objective: Tell a complete 3-minute memory story to a live listener.
days.paretoFocus: Swapping childhood stories is intimacy in any language — and if your spouse is Croatian, trading memories over dinner is the best tutor session money can't buy.
drills: Tell your memory story for 3 minutes; then ask for theirs and follow up with questions.
Apply corrections from your posted text.
Re-do the weakest exercise from Units 57-62.
Record the story once more and compare with week 11's recordings — hear the progress.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Re-say 3 corrected sentences.
Recall: ime/vrijeme/dijete genitives + instrumental phrase endings.

activities.title: Week 18 in ten lines
activities.intro: The whole week compressed into ten sentences — one per pattern. Read each aloud and name the grammar it carries before peeking at the note. If all ten come back instantly, you're ready for the three-minute story.

items.hr: Prošle godine — auto moga brata — čaša hladne vode.
items.en: Last year — my brother's car — a glass of cold water.
items.note: The three genitive uses: time, possession, quantity/description. Say them as a mantra.

items.hr: Popio sam šalicu jake kave i pojeo komad domaćeg kruha.
items.en: I drank a cup of strong coffee and ate a piece of homemade bread.
items.note: Quantity genitives in the wild — plus two perfective verbs for two finished snacks.

items.hr: najprije → zatim → potom → kasnije → na kraju
items.en: first → then → after that → later → in the end
items.note: The five sequencers, cold. This is your story skeleton.

items.hr: Kad sam bio dijete, svakog ljeta smo išli na more.
items.en: When I was a child, we went to the seaside every summer.
items.note: Habitual past: imperfective išli + temporal genitive svakog ljeta.

items.en: One year I learned to swim.
items.note: One-time event: perfective naučio + temporal genitive jedne godine.

items.hr: ime → imena, vrijeme → vremena, dijete → djeteta
items.en: name, time, child — and their genitives
items.note: The shape-shifters. If these three come out clean, the pattern is banked.

items.hr: Djeca se igraju u parku, a ja nemam vremena.
items.en: The children are playing in the park, and I have no time.
items.note: Collective djeca + plural verb; nemati + genitive vremena.

items.hr: Sa svojom najboljom prijateljicom idem na kavu.
items.en: I'm going for coffee with my best friend.
items.note: Full feminine instrumental phrase: svojom najboljom prijateljicom.

items.hr: Vozim se ovim brzim vlakom, a ne svojim starim autom.
items.en: I'm taking this fast train, not my old car.
items.note: Means → bare instrumental, full agreement in -im/-om across both phrases.

items.hr: Čega se najradije sjećaš? — Sjećam se mirisa bakina kruha.
items.en: What do you remember most fondly? — I remember the smell of grandma's bread.
items.note: sjećati se + genitive, twice over: mirisa (čega?) bakina kruha (čijeg?).

activities.title: The week 18 gauntlet
activities.intro: Mixed review across days 121-129, with two listening checks. Whatever you miss here is the 'weakest exercise' to re-do from Units 57-62.

questions.prompt: puno ___ (a lot of work)
questions.explanation: Quantity + genitive: posao → posla, the -ao collapsing as always.

questions.prompt: The genitive of vrijeme is:
questions.explanation: Stem extension plus ije → e: vrijeme → vremena. Nemam vremena!

questions.prompt: Listen: which two things happened?
options: Every summer they went to the seaside; one year he learned to swim.
One summer they went to the seaside; every year he swam.
Every summer he swam; last year he stopped.
One year they moved to the seaside; he taught swimming.
questions.answer: Every summer they went to the seaside; one year he learned to swim.
questions.explanation: svakog ljeta + imperfective išli = the habit; jedne godine + perfective naučio = the single breakthrough.
questions.audioText: Svakog ljeta išli smo na more, a jedne godine naučio sam plivati.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Kako je ime tvog ___?' (child)
questions.explanation: The -(e)t- extension one more time: dijete → djeteta. If this came out instantly, day 127 is banked.

questions.prompt: Listen: how does she remember, and what does she remember?
options: With a smile — the smell of grandma's bread.
With a smile — the sound of the sea.
questions.answer: With a smile — the smell of grandma's bread.
questions.explanation: sa smiješkom = with a smile (instrumental); sjećam se mirisa bakina kruha = genitive after sjećati se.
questions.audioText: Sa smiješkom se sjećam mirisa bakina kruha.

questions.prompt: Habit vs event: 'Često sam ___ s bicikla, a jednom sam slomio ruku.' (fell, repeatedly)
questions.explanation: često → imperfective padati → padao sam. The perfective pao belongs with jednom, in the second half.

questions.prompt: Full instrumental phrase: 'I'm going for coffee with my best friend.'
questions.answer: Sa svojom najboljom prijateljicom idem na kavu.
questions.explanation: Every word in feminine instrumental -om, sa before the s- of svojom, and na kavu (accusative) for the destination.

questions.prompt: The final boss — which sentence is flawless?
options: Jednog ljeta naučio sam voziti barku sa svojim djedom.
Jedno ljeto naučio sam voziti barku sa svojim djedom.
Jednog ljeta učio sam voziti barku s svojim djedom.
Jednog ljeta naučio sam voziti barku sa svojem djedom.
questions.answer: Jednog ljeta naučio sam voziti barku sa svojim djedom.
questions.explanation: Temporal genitive jednog ljeta, perfective naučio for the one-time event, and sa svojim djedom — sa before s-, svojim in -im. The others each break exactly one of this week's rules.

activities.title: Tri minute uspomena — the memory swap
activities.intro: The week's finale: you tell your three-minute memory, then ask for hers and follow up. This model shows the shape — your story, her corrections, her memory, your questions. Compare with week 11's recordings afterwards and hear how far you've come.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Spreman sam. Tri minute, moja najljepša uspomena. Slušaš?
lines.en: I'm ready. Three minutes, my loveliest memory. Are you listening?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Slušam. Kreni!
lines.en: I'm listening. Go!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad sam bio dijete, svakog ljeta smo išli na more. Najprije vlakom do obale, zatim trajektom na otok.
lines.en: When I was a child, we went to the seaside every summer. First by train to the coast, then by ferry to the island.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: 'Vlakom, trajektom' — instrumental bez prijedloga. Nastavi!
lines.en: 'By train, by ferry' — instrumental without a preposition. Continue!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jedne godine naučio sam plivati. Sjećam se tog dana kao da je bio jučer.
lines.en: One year I learned to swim. I remember that day as if it were yesterday.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: 'Sjećam se tog dana' — genitiv! Bravo. Što još?
lines.en: 'I remember that day' — genitive! Bravo. What else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Svakog jutra igrao sam se skrivača s djecom iz susjedstva. Jednom sam pao s drveta i slomio ruku.
lines.en: Every morning I played hide-and-seek with the neighbourhood kids. Once I fell from a tree and broke my arm.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Joj! A ja sam kao dijete svakog ljeta bila kod svoje bake u Slavoniji.
lines.en: Ouch! And as a child I spent every summer at my grandma's in Slavonia.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Čega se najradije sjećaš iz tog vremena?
lines.en: What do you remember most fondly from that time?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Najradije se sjećam mirisa bakina kruha. S vremenom takve uspomene postaju samo ljepše.
lines.en: I most fondly remember the smell of grandma's bread. With time, memories like that only get more beautiful.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: 'Iz tog vremena', 's vremenom'… vrijeme, vremena — te me riječi više ne mogu iznenaditi.
lines.en: 'From that time', 'with time'… vrijeme, vremena — those words can't surprise me any more.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Točno! Ime, vrijeme, dijete — sve si savladao. Prije godinu dana znao si reći samo 'bok'.
lines.en: Exactly! Ime, vrijeme, dijete — you've mastered them all. A year ago you only knew how to say 'hi'.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A sada ti pričam svoje uspomene na hrvatskom. Nije loše, ha?
lines.en: And now I'm telling you my memories in Croatian. Not bad, huh?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nije loše — izvrsno je! Bravo, ljubavi.
lines.en: Not bad — it's excellent! Bravo, my love.

days.title: Ime je znak — names & customs
days.objective: Talk about names, nicknames and Croatian naming customs.
days.paretoFocus: imendan, nadimak, prezime — names open culture: why half of Croatia celebrates name days and what surnames in -ić actually mean.
drills: Do Unit 63 of A2.hr.
Learn: ime, prezime, nadimak, imendan, kum/kuma, krštenje, naslijediti ime.
Explain your own name: origin, meaning, who chose it.
Learn 5 common Croatian names and their name-day customs.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Say 'My name means…' or 'I was named after…'.
Decline ime once more (ime, imena, imenu…).

activities.title: Ime je znak: names, name days and godparents
activities.intro: Names open Croatian culture: half the country celebrates name days, surnames in -ić hide little family histories, and every name has a story. Today's words let you ask about names, explain your own, and understand why your wife's family calendar is full of saints.

items.en: first name and surname
items.note: On every form: ime i prezime. Remember ime declines with the -en- extension: imena, imenu.

items.en: nickname
items.note: G sg. nadimka — the fleeting a drops. Kakav ti je nadimak?

items.en: name day
items.note: The feast day of the saint you are named after — many Croats celebrate it like a second birthday.

items.en: Happy name day!
items.note: Say it to every Ana on 26 July (sveta Ana).

items.en: godfather and godmother
items.note: Also best man / maid of honour at a wedding — kumovi are practically family.

items.en: baptism, christening
items.note: Na krštenju dijete dobiva ime — at the baptism the child receives its name.

items.en: I was named after my grandfather.
items.note: dobiti ime po + locative. A woman says: Dobila sam ime po baki.

items.en: I inherited my grandfather's name.
items.note: naslijediti = to inherit; djedovo — possessive adjective from djed.

items.en: My name means 'victory'.
items.note: značiti = to mean. Get this sentence ready with your own name's meaning.

items.hr: Svi ga zovu Ivo, ali u putovnici piše Ivan.
items.en: Everyone calls him Ivo, but his passport says Ivan.
items.note: Croatian loves short forms: Ivan → Ivo, Katarina → Kata, Josip → Joso.

items.en: Surnames in -ić often mean 'son (of)'.
items.note: Kovačić = the smith's son; Petrić = Petar's son. The -ić is a diminutive.

items.en: Who chose your name?
items.note: izabrati = to choose (pf.). The question that opens every name story.

activities.title: Names and customs in practice
activities.intro: Lock in the name-day vocabulary and the little grammar around it: po + locative, the ime declension and the fleeting a in nadimak.

questions.prompt: 'I was named after my grandfather.' — Dobio sam ime ______ djedu.
questions.explanation: dobiti ime po + locative: Dobio sam ime po djedu.

questions.prompt: Complete the greeting to Ana on 26 July: Sretan ______!
questions.explanation: Sretan imendan! — Happy name day! The name-day equivalent of Sretan rođendan.

questions.prompt: Pick the genitive: Ne znam podrijetlo tvog ______.
questions.explanation: ime extends its stem outside the nominative: G sg. imena (podrijetlo tvog imena = the origin of your name).

questions.prompt: What does the surname Kovačić literally suggest?
questions.explanation: kovač = smith + the diminutive -ić = the smith's little one / son. Most -ić surnames work this way.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Everyone calls me Ivo.'
questions.explanation: The clitic me sits in second position: Svi me zovu Ivo.

questions.prompt: Complete: On je ______ ime po ocu. (dobiti, past, male)
questions.explanation: dobiti → dobio (m. sg. participle): On je dobio ime po ocu.

questions.prompt: Who are 'kum i kuma'?
questions.explanation: kum and kuma are godparents at a baptism and the witnesses at a wedding — a close, almost family bond.

questions.prompt: Ask about a nickname: Kakav ti je ______?
questions.explanation: nadimak = nickname. Watch the fleeting a in other cases: G sg. nadimka.

days.title: Declining foreign words: taksi, hobi, intervju
days.objective: Decline foreign nouns ending in -y, -i, -u, -o and -e.
days.paretoFocus: taksi → taksija, hobi → hobija, intervju → intervjua, auto → auta — loanwords flood modern Croatian, and they all obey learnable insertion rules.
drills: Do Unit 64 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · special noun patterns (foreign nouns).
Decline taksi, hobi, intervju and auto through the singular.
Write 4 sentences: Idem taksijem. Nemam hobija…

items: Give the genitive of taksi, hobi, intervju.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review name-day vocabulary.

activities.title: Loanwords that decline: taksi, hobi, intervju, auto
activities.intro: Modern Croatian is full of loanwords ending in -i, -u, -o and -e — and they all decline as masculine nouns with tiny, learnable adjustments: nouns in -i insert a -j-, the rest just add the endings. Learn the pattern once and taksi, hobi, intervju and auto behave forever.

items.en: taxi → of / to / by taxi
items.note: Nouns in -i insert -j- before every ending.

items.en: I'm going to the airport by taxi.
items.note: Instrumental of means: taksijem — like vlakom, autobusom.

items.hr: Zvao sam taksi, ali taksija nema.
items.en: I called a taxi, but there's no taxi around.
items.note: nema + genitive: taksija.

items.en: hobby → of a hobby
items.note: Same -j- insertion: hobija, hobiju, hobijem.

items.en: Do you have any hobby?
items.note: Inanimate masculine: A sg. = N sg., so imati hobi.

items.en: I have no time for hobbies.
items.note: Plural hobiji; za + A: za hobije.

items.en: interview → of / in an interview
items.note: Nouns in -u simply add the endings: intervjua, intervjuu, intervjuom.

items.hr: Dao je dugi intervju za radio.
items.en: He gave a long interview for the radio.
items.note: za + A. Radio itself declines tomorrow: radija.

items.en: car → of / to / by car
items.note: auto is masculine although it ends in -o: moj auto, G sg. auta.

items.hr: Auto je na servisu, pa idem tramvajem.
items.en: The car is being serviced, so I'm going by tram.
items.note: na servisu = at the garage; pa = so.

items.en: final (match) → of the final
items.note: Nouns in -e take the endings on the full form: finala, finalu.

items.en: Who is playing in the final?
items.note: u + locative: u finalu.

activities.title: Loanwords through the cases
activities.intro: Run today's four model nouns through the singular: genitives after nema and bez, instrumentals of transport, locatives of place.

questions.prompt: Complete the genitive: Nema ______ na stanici. (taksi)
questions.explanation: nema + genitive; taksi inserts -j-: taksija.

questions.prompt: 'I'm going to work by taxi.' — Idem na posao ______.
questions.explanation: Instrumental of means with -em after the soft j: taksijem.

questions.prompt: Complete: Nemam ______, samo posao i obitelj. (hobi, genitive)
questions.explanation: nemati + genitive: Nemam hobija — hobi inserts -j- exactly like taksi.

questions.prompt: 'part of the interview' — dio ______
questions.explanation: dio + genitive; intervju just adds -a: dio intervjua.

questions.prompt: Pick the correct form: Vozim se starim ______.
questions.explanation: voziti se + instrumental: starim autom. auto declines like a masculine noun.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'The car is being serviced.'
questions.explanation: na servisu (locative) = at the garage; je in second position.

questions.prompt: Complete: Tko igra u ______? (finale, locative)
questions.explanation: finale adds endings to the full form: u finalu.

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns is masculine despite ending in -o?
questions.explanation: Loanwords like auto (and radio) are masculine: moj auto, taj radio. Selo, pivo and jutro are ordinary neuters.

days.title: radio → radija, Mario → Marija
days.objective: Decline nouns and names in -io with the j-insertion.
days.paretoFocus: The -io pattern (radio, studio, Mario) inserts -j- in every case but the nominative — tiny rule, constant payoff with names and media words.
drills: Do Unit 65 of A2.hr.
Decline radio and Mario: radija, radiju, radijem; Marija, Mariju, s Marijem.
Write 4 sentences mixing -io nouns and yesterday's loanwords.
Say a sentence about a friend named Mario or Dario in three different cases.

items: Say 'on the radio' (na radiju) and 'with Mario' (s Marijem).
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recall the foreign-noun genitives from yesterday.

activities.title: radio → radija, Mario → Marija: the -io pattern
activities.intro: Nouns and names ending in -io insert a -j- in every case except the nominative: radio → radija, Mario → Marija, studio → studija. It's a tiny rule with constant payoff — Croatian media words and half the men on the Dalmatian coast follow it.

items.en: radio → of / on / with the radio
items.note: The -j- appears everywhere except the nominative.

items.en: I listen to the news on the radio.
items.note: na + locative: na radiju.

items.hr: Čuo sam tu pjesmu na radiju.
items.en: I heard that song on the radio.
items.note: čuti → čuo (m. sg. participle).

items.en: studio → of / in the studio
items.note: Snimamo u studiju. — We're recording in the studio.

items.en: Mario → of / to / with Mario
items.note: Names in -io follow exactly the same rule.

items.hr: Razgovarao sam s Marijem o poslu.
items.en: I talked with Mario about work.
items.note: s + instrumental: s Marijem.

items.en: I'm going to Dario's for coffee.
items.note: kod + genitive: Darija — same pattern as Mario.

items.en: I saw Mario in town.
items.note: Animate masculine: A = G, Marija. Careful — it looks like the female name Marija!

items.hr: Marija sam vidio, a Mariju nisam.
items.en: I saw Mario, but I didn't see Marija.
items.note: Case endings keep them apart: Marija (A of Mario) vs Mariju (A of Marija, the woman).

items.en: Dario works at the radio station.
items.note: raditi na radiju — a fixed phrase.

items.en: That is Mario's car.
items.note: Possessive adjective: Mario → Marijev, with the same -j-.

items.hr: Ne mogu naći radio u autu.
items.en: I can't find the radio in the car.
items.note: Yesterday's auto meets today's radio: u autu, and radio as inanimate A = N.

activities.title: The j-insertion in action
activities.intro: Decline radio, studio and the names Mario and Dario through real sentences — and learn to tell Marija (of Mario) from Marija (the woman).

questions.prompt: Complete: Slušam vijesti na ______. (radio)
questions.explanation: na + locative with the -j- insertion: na radiju.

questions.prompt: 'with Mario' — Razgovarao sam ______.
questions.explanation: Mario → instrumental Marijem. Careful: s Marijom would mean with Marija, the woman!

questions.prompt: Complete: Idem kod ______ na kavu. (Dario)
questions.explanation: kod + genitive: Dario → Darija, with the inserted -j-.

questions.prompt: Complete: Snimamo pjesmu u ______.
questions.explanation: u + locative: studio → u studiju, never without the -j-.

questions.prompt: 'Vidio sam Marija.' — Whom did I see?
questions.explanation: Animate masculine accusative = genitive: Mario → Marija. Seeing the woman Marija would be: Vidio sam Mariju.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Dario works at the radio station.'
questions.explanation: raditi na radiju — the fixed phrase for working at a radio station.

questions.prompt: Possessive: To je ______ auto. (Mario)
questions.explanation: Masculine possessor takes -ev after the inserted j: Mario → Marijev auto.

questions.prompt: Pick the case after bez: Ne mogu zamisliti jutro bez ______.
questions.explanation: bez + genitive: bez radija — I can't imagine a morning without the radio.

days.title: Present classes: pečem, jedem
days.objective: Conjugate peći-type verbs and the -sti → -dem class.
days.paretoFocus: peći → pečem, moći → mogu/možeš, jesti → jedem, sjesti → sjednem — the kitchen-table verb classes. After this, few present tenses can surprise you.
drills: Do Unit 66 of A2.hr.
Study Grammar tab: A2 · more present-tense classes.
Conjugate peći and jesti in full.
Describe cooking a meal: Pečem kruh, jedemo zajedno…

items: Conjugate jesti cold.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Review -io declension with one example.

activities.title: Kitchen-table verbs: pečem, jedem, mogu
activities.intro: Today's verbs live in the kitchen: peći (to bake/roast), jesti (to eat) and their friends. They are consonant-stem verbs with regular -em endings but surprising corners — oni peku, ja mogu — and after today very few present tenses can catch you off guard.

items.en: to bake / roast → I bake…
items.note: Consonant-stem verb. Watch the 3rd plural: oni peku!

items.en: I bake bread every Saturday.
items.note: svaku subotu — accusative of time.

items.en: Grandma is baking cakes for the name day.
items.note: Day 131 meets day 134: za + accusative.

items.en: They are grilling fish on the barbecue.
items.note: 3rd pl. peku — the č becomes k again.

items.en: to eat → I eat…
items.note: The -sti → -dem class; 3rd pl. jedu.

items.en: What do you eat for breakfast?
items.note: za doručak / za ručak / za večeru — for breakfast / lunch / dinner.

items.en: On Sundays we eat together at mum's place.
items.note: nedjeljom — instrumental for a repeated day: 'on Sundays'.

items.en: can → I can…
items.note: 1st sg. and 3rd pl. are both mogu; everything in between has ž.

items.en: I can't eat spicy food.
items.note: Modal + infinitive; ljuto = spicy (also 'angry').

items.en: to sit down → I sit down
items.note: The -sti → -dnem class; perfective, so it mostly appears in commands and futures.

items.en: Sit down, lunch is ready!
items.note: Imperative of sjesti: sjedni! / sjednite!

items.hr: Peče se kruh — cijela kuća miriše.
items.en: Bread is baking — the whole house smells wonderful.
items.note: A se-form sneak preview of day 139's impersonals; mirisati = to smell (nice).

activities.title: pečem, jedem, mogu — no surprises left
activities.intro: Conjugate the kitchen verbs cold: the tricky third plurals, the mogu/možeš split and the sjesti imperative.

questions.prompt: Complete: Oni ______ ribu na roštilju.
questions.explanation: peći has k in the 3rd plural: oni peku (like moći → oni mogu).

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ______ kruh svaku subotu. (peći)
questions.explanation: peći → pečem in the 1st singular: Pečem kruh.

questions.prompt: Complete: Što ______ za doručak? (jesti, ti)
questions.explanation: jesti → jedem, jedeš: the -sti infinitive hides a d-stem.

questions.prompt: Complete: Djeca ______ juhu.
questions.explanation: 3rd plural of jesti is jedu. Djeca is a collective but takes a plural verb.

questions.prompt: 'I can't come to lunch.' — Ne ______ doći na ručak.
questions.explanation: moći is irregular: ja mogu (never *možem), ti možeš, oni mogu.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Grandma is baking cakes for the name day.'
questions.explanation: Subject–verb–object, then za + accusative: Baka peče kolače za imendan.

questions.prompt: Complete the command: ______, ručak je gotov! (sjesti, vi)
questions.explanation: sjesti → sjednem → imperative sjedni / sjednite.

questions.prompt: Which present form is NOT correct?
questions.explanation: The 3rd plural of peći is peku — *pečeju does not exist.

days.title: dajem, prodajem + dio → dijela
days.objective: Conjugate -vati → -jem verbs and decline the noun dio.
days.paretoFocus: davati → dajem, prodavati → prodajem cover give/sell/hand-over verbs; dio → dijela is the last irregular noun on the A2 list. The grammar inventory is now complete.
drills: Do Unit 67 of A2.hr.
Conjugate davati and prodavati in the present.
Decline dio: dijela, dijelu, dijelom; veći dio dana.
Write 4 sentences with dajem/prodajem + a form of dio.

items: Say 'most of the day' (veći dio dana).
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Recap: you have now met every A2 grammar structure — list 3 favourites.

activities.title: dajem, prodajem — and the last irregular noun: dio
activities.intro: Verbs in -vati lose the va in the present: davati → dajem, prodavati → prodajem, poznavati → poznajem. And dio (part) — the last irregular noun on your A2 list — swaps -io for -ijel- before every ending: dijela, dijelu, dijelovi. After today, your A2 grammar inventory is complete.

items.en: to give (impf.) → I give…
items.note: -vati → -jem: the va disappears in the present. 3rd pl. daju.

items.en: I give the dog the lunch leftovers.
items.note: Dative psu + accusative ostatke.

items.en: to sell (impf.) → I sell
items.note: Same pattern: prodajem, prodaješ… prodaju.

items.en: The neighbours are selling their flat.
items.note: 3rd pl. prodaju — looks like the noun prodaja, but it's a verb here.

items.en: to know (a person) → I know
items.note: Poznajem ga godinama. — I've known him for years.

items.en: to give / to sell (perfective)
items.note: The perfective partners keep -am: dam, prodam. Prodali smo auto. — We sold the car.

items.en: part → of / to / with a part
items.note: The -io becomes -ijel- before endings, exactly like posao → posla.

items.en: I work most of the day.
items.note: veći dio + genitive — a phrase you can say every single day.

items.en: Divide the cake into eight parts.
items.note: podijeliti na + A; after osam the noun is genitive plural: dijelova.

items.en: This is the most beautiful part of town.
items.note: dio grada, dio filma, dio priče — dio + genitive.

items.en: I'm giving you a piece of the cake.
items.note: Today's verb + today's noun in one sentence.

items.hr: Prodajem bicikl jer mi treba novac.
items.en: I'm selling my bike because I need money.
items.note: treba mi + nominative = I need. More of this pattern on day 139.

activities.title: Give, sell, and divide into parts
activities.intro: Drill the -vati → -jem present and run dio through its cases — the final pieces of the A2 grammar puzzle.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ______ psu hranu. (davati)
questions.explanation: davati → dajem: the -va- drops in the present.

questions.prompt: Complete: Susjedi ______ stan.
questions.explanation: prodavati → prodajem… oni prodaju. *prodavaju does not exist.

questions.prompt: Complete: ______ ga godinama. (poznavati, ja)
questions.explanation: poznavati → poznajem: Poznajem ga godinama = I've known him for years.

questions.prompt: Pick the genitive of dio: Bez tog ______ priča nema smisla.
questions.explanation: bez + genitive; dio → dijela with the -ijel- stem.

questions.prompt: Complete: Podijelili smo kolač na osam ______.
questions.explanation: After osam (5+) the noun takes the genitive plural: osam dijelova.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I work most of the day.'
questions.explanation: veći dio dana = most of the day (dio + genitive), then the verb.

questions.prompt: Perfective past: ______ smo auto prošli tjedan. (prodati, mi, male speakers)
questions.explanation: prodati → prodali (m. pl.): Prodali smo auto — one completed sale, so perfective.

questions.prompt: Which pair is matched correctly (infinitive → present)?
questions.explanation: All -vati verbs switch to -jem: dajem, prodajem, poznajem. The -vam forms don't exist.

days.title: Output: the story of your name
days.objective: Tell and write the story of your name — or a Croatian name you love.
days.paretoFocus: A personal name story deploys the whole Ime je znak module plus declension of real names — memorable content you'll retell at every Croatian table.
drills: Do Unit 68 of A2.hr.
Write 8-10 sentences: your name's origin, who chose it, nicknames, how Croatians pronounce it.
Deliver it as a 90-second talk, twice.
Post the text for corrections and ask how your name declines in Croatian.

items: Re-deliver the talk cold.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Decline your own name (or its Croatian form) through 3 cases.

activities.title: The story of your name: ready-made frames
activities.intro: Output day. These twelve frames build your 90-second talk: where your name comes from, who chose it, your nicknames and how Croatians handle it. Swap in your own details, say each line aloud, then assemble the story.

items.hr: Moje ime dolazi iz latinskog jezika.
items.en: My name comes from Latin.
items.note: iz + genitive: iz latinskog, iz grčkog, iz hebrejskog, iz španjolskog.

items.en: My mum chose my name.
items.note: Clitic order: Ime mi je izabrala… — dative mi before the auxiliary je.

items.hr: Dobio sam ime po djedu, koji se zvao isto.
items.en: I was named after my grandfather, who had the same name.
items.note: koji se zvao isto — a relative clause you can reuse anywhere.

items.en: My name means 'strong and brave'.
items.note: Look up your name's real meaning and slot it in.

items.en: At home they call me by a short form.
items.note: skraćeno = in shortened form; zvati koga + nickname.

items.hr: Hrvati moje ime izgovaraju malo drukčije.
items.en: Croatians pronounce my name a bit differently.
items.note: izgovarati = to pronounce; drukčije = differently.

items.hr: Na hrvatskom bi moje ime glasilo…
items.en: In Croatian my name would be…
items.note: glasiti = to read / sound (of names and texts); conditional bi glasilo.

items.en: My name is hard to decline.
items.note: sklanjati se = to decline (grammar). Ask your wife: Kako se moje ime sklanja?

items.hr: U mojoj obitelji svi nasljeđuju imena.
items.en: In my family everyone inherits names.
items.note: nasljeđivati (impf.) → nasljeđujem — the process, not one event.

items.hr: Da imam sina, dao bih mu ime po ocu.
items.en: If I had a son, I would name him after my father.
items.note: Unreal condition: da + present, then the conditional dao bih.

items.hr: Nisam znao da je to ime tako često u Hrvatskoj.
items.en: I didn't know that name was so common in Croatia.
items.note: često = frequent/common; the je sits right after da.

items.hr: A ti? Po kome si ti dobila ime?
items.en: And you? Who were you named after?
items.note: po kome = after whom. End your talk by turning it into a conversation.

activities.title: Assemble the name story
activities.intro: Test the frames before you deliver the talk: clitic order, po + locative, the conditional and the connectors that hold a story together.

questions.prompt: Complete the clitic: Ime ______ je izabrala mama. ('to me')
questions.explanation: The dative clitic mi comes before the auxiliary je: Ime mi je izabrala mama.

questions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence: 'I was named after my grandfather.'
questions.explanation: Named after = dobiti ime po + locative: po djedu, po baki, po ocu.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'My name comes from Latin.'
questions.explanation: iz + genitive of the language adjective: iz latinskog (jezika).

questions.prompt: Complete: Moje ime ______ 'kralj'. (to mean)
questions.explanation: značiti = to mean: Moje ime znači… — the heart of your talk.

questions.prompt: Pick the conditional: Da imam sina, ______ mu ime po ocu.
questions.explanation: Unreal condition: da + present in the if-clause, conditional (dao bih) in the main clause.

questions.prompt: What does 'po kome' mean in 'Po kome si dobila ime?'
questions.explanation: po + locative of tko (kome): named after whom.

questions.prompt: Complete: Hrvati moje ime ______ malo drukčije. (izgovarati, oni)
questions.explanation: izgovarati is a regular -am verb: oni izgovaraju.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I didn't know that name was so common.'
questions.answer: Nisam znao da je to ime tako često
questions.explanation: Nisam can stand first; in the da-clause the clitic je comes immediately after da.

activities.title: Proba: priča o mom imenu
activities.intro: You rehearse your 90-second name talk with your wife as the audience — and the clock. Read your lines ('Me') aloud; every one of them is a frame from today's list with your details slotted in.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hajde, vježbajmo. Ispričaj mi priču o svom imenu — imaš devedeset sekundi!
lines.en: Come on, let's practise. Tell me the story of your name — you have ninety seconds!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može. Moje ime dolazi iz latinskog jezika i znači 'snažan'.
lines.en: Okay. My name comes from Latin and means 'strong'.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Lijep početak. Tko ti je izabrao ime?
lines.en: A nice start. Who chose your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ime mi je izabrala mama. Dobio sam ga po djedu, koji se zvao isto.
lines.en: My mum chose my name. I got it after my grandfather, who had the same name.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A imaš li nadimak?
lines.en: And do you have a nickname?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Imam. Kod kuće me zovu skraćeno, ali službeno se zovem punim imenom.
lines.en: I do. At home they call me by a short form, but officially I go by my full name.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kako Hrvati izgovaraju tvoje ime?
lines.en: How do Croatians pronounce your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Malo drukčije nego kod nas. I nisam znao da se moje ime tako teško sklanja!
lines.en: A bit differently than back home. And I didn't know my name was so hard to decline!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! Navikni se. A da imamo sina, koje bi mu ime dao?
lines.en: Ha! Get used to it. And if we had a son, what name would you give him?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dao bih mu ime po tvom ocu. Tako bi naslijedio i ime i priču.
lines.en: I would name him after your father. That way he would inherit both a name and a story.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: To je jako lijepo. Vidiš, priča je gotova — i bila je duža od devedeset sekundi!
lines.en: That's really lovely. See, the story is done — and it was longer than ninety seconds!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Onda idem ispočetka. Vježba čini majstora!
lines.en: Then I'll start over. Practice makes perfect!

days.title: Week 19 speaking day (+ Unit 69)
days.objective: Free conversation on names, family history and customs, plus one unit.
days.paretoFocus: Names lead naturally to family history — a conversation that recycles memories, genitives and possessives while feeling nothing like grammar practice.
drills: 15-minute conversation: names and stories in your family (tutor, partner or spouse).
Do Unit 69 of A2.hr.
Ask your partner about their imendan and family names.
